properties.title: Warrane becomes Sydney Cove
properties.summary: Warrane is the Gadigal name for the cove where the First Fleet anchored on 26 January 1788. The British would call it Sydney Cove.
body: Known as Warrane by the Gadigal people for thousands of years, this cove was the birthplace of the colony of Sydney. The First Fleet anchored here on 26 January 1788, and the settlement began to expand and grow from this spot.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney, Barani

images.caption: Algernon Talmage's painting of the flag-raising at Sydney Cove, 26 January 1788

properties.title: The Tank Stream, a buried river
properties.summary: The freshwater stream that decided where Sydney would be built now runs in a tunnel under the city.
body: The Tank Stream is the reason Sydney sits where it does. When Governor Phillip turned away from Botany Bay, it was this small watercourse running down to the cove that drew him in. For the first years of the colony it was the only fresh water supply, and during the drought of 1790 tanks were cut into its sandstone banks to hold a reserve of water. Each tank ran about five metres deep and held close to 20,000 litres.

The arrangement never really kept pace with the growing town. Runoff and waste fouled the water, and by 1826 the stream was abandoned as a source of drinking water. Through the 19th century the Tank Stream was progressively built over, until it vanished from the surface entirely. It survives as a heritage-listed channel running beneath Pitt Street to an outfall at Circular Quay, controlled now by Sydney Water.
properties.source: Sydney Water; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Tank Stream in 1842, watercolour by John Skinner Prout (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: Randwick: racing on the sand hills
properties.summary: Horses have raced on this ground since 1833, when the track through the sand hills was known simply as the Sandy Course.
properties.body: Racing began here in the autumn of 1833 on a rough track laid out through the sand hills, known then as the Sandy Course. Enthusiasm for these meetings waned by the late 1830s, until the Australian Jockey Club was formed in 1842. The club held its first meeting at Randwick over three days in May 1860, drawing a crowd of more than 6,000 on the opening day. The surrounding district had taken its name from the surveyor Simeon Pearce, who arrived in the colony in 1841 and named it after Randwick in Gloucestershire, near his English home. The course took the same name in turn. Queen Elizabeth II granted the course its Royal title when she visited in February 1992 and opened the Paddock Stand.
properties.source: Australian Turf Club; Dictionary of Sydney

images.caption: Royal Randwick today

properties.title: Observatory Hill: windmill, fort, time-ball
properties.summary: The highest point of The Rocks has carried a windmill, then a fort, then the colony's observatory.
properties.body: At first, this site was known as Windmill Hill, after the colony's first windmill which stood here from 1796. In 1804 work began on Fort Phillip, raised to guard against convict uprising, though it was abandoned a few years later and never finished. For decades, the half-built walls sat there until Sydney Observatory opened on the hill in 1858, with William Scott as the first Government Astronomer. Its time-ball still drops at 1pm each day, a signal by which ships' captains once checked and set their chronometers before sailing. The same mechanism does the job now, helped along by an electric motor.
properties.source: Sydney Observatory (MAAS); Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Sydney Observatory in 1874

properties.title: Waverley Cemetery
properties.summary: Waverley Cemetery opened in 1877 on the clifftops between Bronte and Clovelly, and it became the resting place of writer Henry Lawson and poet Henry Kendall.
body: Waverley Council set aside land for a new cemetery at this site in 1868 to accomodate the growing population of the Eastern Suburbs. The site was chosen to align with changing societal attitudes towards cemeteries as dark and foreboding places. As such, when Waverley Cemetery took its first burials in 1877, the dead were given one of the finest outlooks in Sydney, a peaceful scenic setting for commemoration. Rows of white marble and carved sandstone run right to the edge of the ocean cliffs between Bronte and Clovelly. Designed as reflection of Victorian ideals, Waverley Cemetery was laid out in a geometric fashion, with wide avenues and neatly arranged plots. The use of Italian Carrara marble for the monuments became one of the cemetery's most distinctive features, enhancing its sense of grandeur and reverence.

Two of the country's best loved writers lie here. Henry Lawson was buried at Waverley in 1922 after a state funeral, and the poet Henry Kendall rests nearby beneath his own monument. The most striking memorial of all is the Irish one, completed in 1901, which holds the reburied remains of Michael Dwyer, a leader of the 1798 rebellion against British rule, and his wife Mary. The Bondi to Coogee coastal walk now threads straight past the sea-facing graves, so the cemetery has quietly become one of the most visited spots on the eastern beaches.
properties.source: Waverley Council; Dictionary of Sydney

images.caption: Henry Lawson's funeral at Waverley Cemetery, 1922
One of the finest outlooks in Sydney
1798 Memorial Procession, 19 May 1898
Memorial to the 1798 Uprising

properties.title: Centennial Park and the birth of a nation
properties.summary: Centennial Park opened on 26 January 1888 to mark a century of European settlement, and on 1 January 1901 it became the place where the Commonwealth of Australia was proclaimed.
body: Now a serene escape in the heart of the city, Centennial Park opened on 26 January 1888 to mark a century of European settlement, and on 1 January 1901 it became the place where the Commonwealth of Australia was proclaimed.
properties.source: Centennial Parklands; National Archives of Australia

properties.title: The 1900 bubonic plague
properties.summary: Bubonic plague reached the Sydney waterfront in January 1900, and the clean-up that followed reshaped who owned The Rocks and what was left standing there.
body: The third great bubonic plague pandemic started in northern China in 1855, and slowly made its way to Australia. By 1894, it had reached Hong Kong, by 1896 India and in 1899 Noumea was declared a plague-infected port. The first case in Australia was diagnosed on 19 January 1900, in a Millers Point van driver named Arthur Payne. The disease spread through fleas carried by ship rats coming off the wharves, and the crowded waterfront lanes of The Rocks and Millers Point were quickly blamed. Over eight months that year 303 cases were reported and 103 people died.
properties.source: State Archives NSW 'Views taken during Cleansing Operations'; Dictionary of Sydney

articles.heading: Bubonic Plague.
articles.newspaper: The Port Macquarie News and Hastings River Advocate

images.caption: Cleansing operations in the quarantined streets, 1900

properties.title: Wonderland City at Tamarama
properties.summary: For five summers a sprawling amusement park, complete with an airship over the surf and an elephant on the sand, filled the gully behind Tamarama Beach.
body: From 1906 to 1911 the theatrical entrepreneur William Anderson ran Wonderland City in the gully behind Tamarama, on ground that had once held the Bondi Aquarium. Promoters called it the Coney Island of Australia, and at roughly twenty acres it was the largest open-air amusement park in the southern hemisphere. There was a switchback railway hugging the cliffs, a music hall seating around a thousand, an open-air ice rink, a haunted house and a maze.

Large crowds, estimated at 2,000 people, came every summer weekend through seventy turnstiles at the entrance, greeted by over 160 employees and drawn in by the spectacular headline acts. An elephant, usually billed as Princess Alice, carried passengers along the beach, while an airship nicknamed the Airem Scarem slid along a cable above the waves. One daredevil performer Jack Lewis even roller skated down a ramp, through a hoop of fire and landed in a tank with sharks.

The trouble was the beach itself. Anderson fenced the park with barbed wire and charged for entry, which cut the public off from Tamarama and brought legal challenges and loud protest. On 6 March 1907, the Minister for Lands, James Ashton, issued an order to resume the 12-foot strip of land fronting the beach to "give free access for all time to the beach at Tamarama Bay", but the reputation of the park was damaged. Combined with worries about the animals and the safety of the rides, along with the simple fading of novelty, Anderson decided to close the park in 1911 and was said to have lost £15,000 on the venture.
properties.source: Waverley Council local history; Dictionary of Sydney

images.caption: Wonderland City's 'airship' above Tamarama, 1908
The 20-acre pleasure park drew large crowds every weekend
The Wonderland City site today

properties.title: Bondi and the invention of surf lifesaving
properties.summary: Once daylight ocean bathing was no longer forbidden, the crowds and the drownings arrived together, and in 1907 Bondi's volunteers formed what is recognised as the world's oldest surf lifesaving club.
body: Once daylight ocean bathing was no longer forbidden, the crowds and the drownings arrived together, and in 1907 Bondi's volunteers formed what is recognised as the world's oldest surf lifesaving club.
properties.source: Surf Life Saving Australia; Waverley Council local history

articles.heading: SURF LIFE-SAVING. NORTH BONDI SURF LIFE-SAVING CLUB.

images.caption: Surf lifesavers carrying the reel at a Bondi carnival, c.1940 (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: Coogee's English pleasure pier
properties.summary: For about six years Coogee had a Brighton-style pleasure pier reaching out over the Pacific, until the surf wore it down.
body: The Coogee Pier officially opened on the 24th of July, 1928, at the height of Coogee's seaside resort popularity.  It ran around 180 metres out over the water on timber piles, shorter than the grand 250m length first proposed to rival Brighton. On board were a 1400 seat theatre, a 600 capacity ballroom, a 400 seat restaurant, small shops, and a penny arcade. A shark net also stretched from the pier to the southern end of the beach.

The occasion of the shark net's official opening coincided with the unveiling of the new Giles' Ocean Baths Complex and new surf sheds. The celebration was promoted as "Come to Coogee" Week and attracted a crowd of 135,000 people. Bathers flocked to Coogee and, after four months, the number of bathers had topped the 800,000 mark. When a hole appeared in the net, a crowd of 40,000 came to watch an inspection by a diver. The building also allowed for the advent of Night surfing at the beach, which proved very successful and attracted crowds of between 20,000 and 30,000 a night. The floodlights installed were powerful enough to allow night surf carnivals.

However, the pleasure pier did not last long. The contracting company claimed that the pier would be considerably stronger than any similar pier constructed in England, but over the next few years, coinciding with the depression, crowds diminished and storms damaged the buildings. The dream of an English-style resort ended with the demolition of the pier superstructure in 1933-34.
properties.source: Randwick City Library local history; Dictionary of Sydney; Randwick Council

images.caption: Coogee Pier, photographed by Samuel Wood c. 1928–32
Coogee Pier on the day of the opening of the sharkproof enclosure 16 November 1929
The Pier was initially very popular, attracting large crowds.
The Pier was initially very popular, attracting large crowds.

properties.title: The Bondi Icebergs
properties.summary: A group of Bondi lifesavers who refused to give up swimming over winter founded a club at the beach's southern pool in 1929.
body: In 1929 a group of Bondi lifesavers, keen to keep their fitness through the off-season, formed a winter swimming club at the baths cut into the rocks at the beach's southern end. The club is best known for a punishing membership rule. To earn full standing, a swimmer must turn up for three Sundays out of every four through winter, and keep it up for five years. The rule, known as 15B, was written into the constitution in the 1940s rather than at the start, and it still holds today. The club was upgraded from a tin shed in 196, and after decades as an all-male organisation, the club finally opened its doors to female swimmers in 1995.
Their pool sits low against the rocks and is regularly swamped by storm swell breaking clean over the lane ropes. It has become one of the most photographed swimming pools in the world.
properties.source: Bondi Icebergs Club; Waverley Council local history

images.caption: The Icebergs pool below the clubhouse today

properties.summary: In April 1935 a captured tiger shark at the Coogee Aquarium coughed up a tattooed human arm, opening one of Australia's strangest and still unsolved murder cases.
body: In April 1935 a captured tiger shark at the Coogee Aquarium coughed up a tattooed human arm, opening one of Australia's strangest and still unsolved murder cases.

properties.title: Black Sunday at Bondi
properties.summary: On a sweltering afternoon in 1938, a set of freak waves swept hundreds of swimmers off a Bondi sandbank, and the lifesavers carried out the largest mass rescue in surf history.
body: On Sunday, 6th February 1938 the heat had pushed some 35,000 people onto Bondi Beach. Suddenly, around 3pm, three large waves rolled in one after another and dumped over a crowded sandbank. The water could not drain away fast enough, and the savage backwash dragged hundreds of swimmers off their feet and into a deep channel.

By chance about 60 lifesavers were already on the sand for a club competition. They went straight in with reels, surf floats, boards and skis, and many simply swam out without a belt. In roughly twenty minutes they brought in around 245 people. Thirty-five were pulled out unconscious and revived on the beach. Five men drowned.

It became known as Black Sunday, and it stands as the largest single rescue in the history of surf lifesaving. The scale of it helped cement the volunteer lifesaver as a fixture of the Australian beach.
properties.source: Surf Life Saving Australia; Waverley Council local history

articles.heading: SIX MEN DROWN IN BONDI SURF 200 Caught In Terrific Dumpers

properties.title: The night the war came to the harbour
properties.summary: On the night of 31 May 1942, three Japanese midget submarines slipped into Sydney Harbour, and a stray torpedo sank the depot ship HMAS Kuttabul, killing 21 sailors.
body: On the night of 31 May 1942, three Japanese midget submarines crept into Sydney Harbour, each crewed by two men, hunting the Allied warships at anchor. One of them, M-24, fired two torpedoes at the American cruiser USS Chicago. Both missed.

One torpedo ran ashore at Garden Island without exploding. The other passed beneath the Dutch submarine K9 and struck the sea wall under HMAS Kuttabul, a converted harbour ferry being used as a sleeping quarters. The old ship was torn apart and sank at her mooring. Twenty-one sailors died, 19 Australians and 2 British. A week later a Japanese submarine surfaced off the coast and shelled the eastern suburbs. For a city that had thought of the war as something happening far away, the harbour raid brought it close to home. The 1943 aerial survey on this map was flown in the anxious months that followed.

The crews of the three midget submarines all died. One midget, from the mother submarine I- 27, was detected early in the raid and exploded in the harbour nets. The midget from I-22 became trapped in Taylors Bay and the crew chose suicide over capture. The M24 escaped from the harbour, its fate unknown until November 2006, when the remains of the submarine, with the bodies of the crew still on board, were discovered off Bungan Head, Newport.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; Royal Australian Navy

properties.title: Circular Quay before the Cahill
properties.summary: In the 1943 layer the Quay still opens straight to the sky, before the railway and the Cahill Expressway were stacked across its edge.
body: Set the time machine to 1943 and Circular Quay looks remarkably open. The ferry wharves run right up to the city, with nothing standing between the water and the streets behind it. For more than a century this was the working front door of Sydney, where boats, people and cargo met the town face to face.

That changed in two stages. The elevated Circular Quay railway station opened on 20 January 1956, completing the City Circle that John Bradfield had planned decades earlier. Two years later, on 24 March 1958, the Cahill Expressway was laid on top of it. Named for Premier Joe Cahill, the road drew a concrete line across the head of the Quay and cut it off from the city.

Almost from the day it opened, people have argued about pulling the Cahill down to reconnect the city with its harbour. Architects, planners and lord mayors have floated schemes to demolish or bury it for decades. The expressway is still there, and so is the argument.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW Spatial Services 1943 imagery

images.caption: The Cahill Expressway under construction over Circular Quay

properties.title: The Big Dig
properties.summary: In 1994 archaeologists peeled back a single Rocks block and found a whole colonial neighbourhood, then kept it on display beneath the building that came after.
body: In 1994 a city block bounded by Cumberland and Gloucester Streets was stripped back for excavation. Over about twenty weeks, a team of archaeologists and hundreds of volunteers uncovered the foundations of more than thirty houses and shops, the earliest going back to around 1795. With them came more than 750,000 artefacts, the small leavings of ordinary life such as bottles, crockery, buttons, clay pipes and children's toys.

This was no grand quarter. It was a crowded working neighbourhood of convicts, sailors, labourers and shopkeepers, the kind of place colonial Sydney was actually built from. The dig gave historians a rare close look at how those people lived, ate and got by, street by street and house by house.

Rather than clear the ground and build over it, the site was kept. A youth hostel now sits on pillars above the open foundations, so visitors can look down on the old walls and lanes below. The archaeology and the modern building share the one block, one stacked on top of the other.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour YHA / The Big Dig Archaeology Education Centre
properties.image_caption: The excavated foundations preserved beneath the youth hostel

properties.title: Manly: the swim that broke the law
properties.summary: In 1902 the Manly newspaper proprietor William Gocher set out to break the daylight bathing ban in print, and within about a year Manly Council gave way and let people swim in the sea by day.
body: For decades a colonial-era rule kept people out of the surf during daylight hours, a matter less of safety than of public decency in an age with no changing sheds and few proper swimming costumes. William Henry Gocher, who ran Manly's local paper, decided the rule was absurd and used his columns to say so. In the spring of 1902 he declared that he would bathe at Manly in the middle of the day, and by his own account he did, more than once.

The first times the police left him alone, and Gocher needled them in print for it. On a later occasion an officer walked him out of the water and questioned him, but no charge was ever laid. In November 1903 a reluctant Manly Council gave in and allowed bathing at any hour, on the condition that swimmers wore a costume covering them from neck to knee.

Gocher is often called the man who freed the Australian beach, and Manly leaned into the image with its slogan of being seven miles from Sydney and a thousand miles from care. It is worth saying that no newspaper report of the famous swim has ever been found, so historians treat the tidy story with some caution. Whatever the exact sequence, the daylight ban did fall, and the beach as Sydney came to know it followed.
properties.source: Manly Art Gallery & Museum; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The ferry Fairlight at Manly around 1900, the boat that brought the bathers

articles.heading: ALL-DAY SURF BATHING. HOW IT WAS WON FOR MANLY.

properties.title: Balmain and the birth of Labor
properties.summary: After the great maritime strike of 1890 was beaten, Balmain's waterside workers turned to the ballot box and helped found one of the first Labor leagues, meeting at Unity Hall in 1891.
body: The maritime strike of 1890 was a hard defeat for the unions. Shipowners and employers held firm, the men went back to work on the bosses' terms, and the lesson sank in. Industrial muscle on the wharves was not enough on its own. If working people wanted to change the rules, they needed their own members sitting in Parliament making them.

Balmain was a fitting place to act on that idea. It was a tight, hard-working harbour suburb of dock labourers, ship painters and tradesmen, and in April 1891 its workers set up a local branch of the Labor Electoral League. The Unity Hall Hotel still claims the founding meetings, and the Balmain branch calls itself the oldest in the country.

The new movement moved fast. At the 1891 New South Wales election, Labor candidates won 35 of the 141 seats and arrived in the colonial Parliament as a bloc that the older parties could no longer ignore. Within a few years the same movement would help form some of the first labour governments anywhere in the world.
properties.source: Australian Labor Party records; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The ferry Balmain in the 1890s, the decade Labor was born here

properties.title: Experiment Farm: the deal that fed the colony
properties.summary: With the colony close to starvation, Governor Phillip set convict farmer James Ruse a test: feed yourself off the land. Ruse passed, and in 1791 he was given the first land grant ever made in New South Wales.
body: By 1789 the young colony was hungry and unsure it could ever grow its own food. Governor Arthur Phillip handed convict James Ruse cleared ground at Rose Hill, some basic tools and a simple brief. Make yourself self-sufficient and prove a settler family could live off this country. Ruse worked the soil, raised wheat and maize and kept a kitchen garden.

In February 1791 Ruse told the authorities he no longer needed the public store. Two months later, in April, he received a grant of 30 acres, the first land grant issued in the colony. The experiment had worked, and it carried a larger lesson. The settlement could feed itself if it pushed inland to better soil, and the colony's centre of gravity began shifting west towards Parramatta.

Ruse sold the farm to Surgeon John Harris in 1793. The cottage that stands on the site today, built for Harris in the 1830s, is one of the oldest surviving colonial homes in Australia and keeps the name of the experiment that helped the colony survive.
properties.source: Museums of History NSW, Experiment Farm Cottage (National Trust)
properties.image_caption: Experiment Farm Cottage, Harris Park

articles.heading: EXPERIMENT FARM.
articles.newspaper: The Cumberland Argus and Fruitgrowers Advocate

articles.heading: Experiment Farm.

properties.title: La Perouse: the French who almost beat the British
properties.summary: Just days after the First Fleet, two French ships under the Comte de Laperouse anchored in Botany Bay. They sailed north a few weeks later and were never seen again, lost on the reefs of Vanikoro.
body: In late January 1788, as Arthur Phillip was moving his fleet north to the better harbour at Port Jackson, two French ships commanded by Jean-Francois de Galaup, Comte de Laperouse, came into Botany Bay. The timing was extraordinarily close. Had the French arrived a little earlier, the early story of this coast might have been told in French rather than English.

The two parties were friendly. The French camped on the northern shore of the bay for about six weeks while they repaired their ships and took on water and wood. Before leaving, Laperouse entrusted his journals and letters to the British to carry back to Europe. The ships sailed on 10 March 1788 and vanished. The wrecks were eventually traced to the reefs of Vanikoro in the Solomon Islands, where the expedition had foundered in a storm.

A stone memorial to Laperouse was begun on the headland in 1825, its foundation laid by Hyacinthe de Bougainville on land granted by Governor Brisbane. The headland is Bidjigal and Gweagal Country and home to one of Sydney's oldest continuous Aboriginal communities, a place that holds far more history than the French story that gave the suburb its name.
properties.source: La Perouse Museum; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Lapérouse monument, begun 1825

articles.heading: La Perouse Monument.
articles.newspaper: The Port Macquarie News and Hastings River Advocate

properties.title: Kurnell, 29 April 1770
properties.summary: On 29 April 1770 the Endeavour anchored off this beach and two Gweagal men stood on the shore to face the landing party, the first meeting of two worlds on the continent's east coast.
body: On 29 April 1770 James Cook's Endeavour dropped anchor in the bay the Gweagal and Dharawal know as Kamay. It was the expedition's first landing on the east coast of the continent. As the boat came in to the southern shore, two Gweagal men stood on the beach to oppose the strangers. Cook's party fired and at least one man was wounded, a violent first contact that has never been forgotten here.

The crew stayed about eight days. Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander gathered so many plant specimens that Cook named the place Botany Bay. The event has long been wrapped in stories of discovery, but for the Gweagal and Dharawal it was an arrival on Country that was already lived in and cared for.

The place never stopped being theirs. It is now the Meeting Place precinct of Kamay Botany Bay National Park, where the events of 29 April 1770 are told from both shores, the people who landed and the people who were already there.
properties.source: Kamay Botany Bay National Park; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: The landing place at Kurnell, Kamay Botany Bay

properties.title: From abattoir to Olympics
properties.summary: For most of a century Homebush Bay was where Sydney put its slaughterhouses, brickworks and waste. By September 2000 the cleaned-up site was hosting the Olympic Games and Cathy Freeman's golden run.
body: For most of the twentieth century Homebush Bay was the part of Sydney polite maps looked away from. The State Abattoir killed and dressed the city's meat here. A brickworks dug clay from a great pit, an armaments depot stored munitions, and bay after bay was filled with industrial waste. The water itself ran foul with blood, offal and chemicals.

Cleaning it up became the largest single task of the 2000 Olympics, one of the biggest soil and groundwater remediation projects the country had attempted. Contaminated ground was treated, capped and sealed across many hectares to make the site safe to walk on. For two weeks in September 2000 the old industrial flats were the centre of the world, never more so than on the night a crowd of more than 112,000 watched Cathy Freeman win the 400 metres.

The disused brick pit was too contaminated and too valuable as habitat to build over. It had become home to the endangered green and golden bell frog, and the tiny creature effectively stopped the bulldozers. The quarry is now a protected reserve, a reminder of the land's industrial past sitting in the middle of the Olympic site.
properties.source: Sydney Olympic Park Authority; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: Stadium Australia during the 2000 Games

properties.title: The engravings at Grotto Point
properties.summary: On the headlands above Middle Harbour, whales, fish and a giant kangaroo are cut into the sandstone. This is one of the oldest things you will find anywhere on this map.
body: High on the flat sandstone above Middle Harbour at Dobroyd Head, figures are pecked into the rock. There are whales and sharks, fish, boomerangs, a great kangaroo and beings that are part human and part animal. They belong to the engraving tradition of the Garigal people, within the broader Eora cultural region, and to one of thousands of such sites scattered across the Sydney basin.

Dating these carvings is hard. The grooves were often re-cut over generations, so the figures you see may carry the work of many hands across a long span of time. What is clear is that this is an old gallery, far older than the colonial city, and the signs along the track ask only that you look, learn and keep off the rock.

Long before 1788, this headland was already part of a living country, mapped and storied by the people who made these marks. It is a reminder that the harbour had a deep history long before any ship sailed into it.
properties.source: NSW National Parks, Aboriginal heritage; Australian Museum
properties.image_caption: Engravings on the Manly scenic walkway

properties.title: The Parramatta Female Factory
properties.summary: Above the Parramatta River stood a workhouse, marriage bureau and prison rolled into one, where convict women in 1827 staged some of the earliest collective protests by women in Australian history.
body: From 1821 the Female Factory above the Parramatta River housed convict women waiting to be assigned to settlers. Francis Greenway designed the building, and it did several jobs at once. Women spun and wove wool inside its walls. Settlers came to choose a wife from among the inmates. And it served as a prison for women who fell foul of the colony's rules.

In October 1827 the matron cut the women's tea and sugar rations, and the response was fierce. Around 200 women seized workmen's hammers and sledges, broke down the gates and surged out into Parramatta. It counts among the first collective protests by women in Australian history, and more uprisings followed over the next two decades.

The Factory has left a long shadow. A great many Australians today can trace their family line back to a woman who passed through these walls, which makes the place a quiet root system for much of the country's convict heritage.
properties.source: Parramatta Female Factory Friends; Museums of History NSW
properties.image_caption: The Parramatta Female Factory

articles.heading: FEMALE FACTORY.
articles.newspaper: The Sun and New South Wales Independent Press

properties.title: 1855: the first train
properties.summary: On 26 September 1855 the colony's first steam train pulled out of a tin shed near Devonshire Street, headed for Parramatta Junction.
body: On 26 September 1855 the colony's first steam train left a corrugated-iron terminus on the Cleveland Fields, close to this spot, and ran out to Parramatta Junction near today's Granville. The trip took about fifty minutes each way.

The private Sydney Railway Company had gone broke building the line, so the government bought it out before a single passenger train ran. The assets passed into public hands in early September 1855, which made it the first railway nationalised anywhere in the British Empire. New South Wales railways were state-owned from the very start.
That humble shed was the seed of the network that spread the suburbs across the basin. Fifty years later Central Station rose two blocks to the north, and the modest beginning was soon forgotten.
properties.source: Sydney Trains heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Sydney's first railway terminus, photographed 1871

articles.heading: OPENING OF THE SYDNEY-AND PARRAMATTA RAILWAY.
OPENING OF THE SYDNEY AND PARRAMATTA RAILWAY.

properties.title: The (Royal) National Park
properties.summary: The world's second national park, reserved in 1879 south of Port Hacking, was laid out for picnics and pleasure as much as for preservation.
body: In 1879, seven years after Yellowstone, the colony set aside 18,000 acres south of Port Hacking and called it the National Park. It was arguably the first place anywhere to carry that exact name, and only the second national park in the world.

Audley became the heart of it. From around 1900 the precinct gained a weir, boatsheds and a dance pavilion, and the bush was treated as a backdrop for a day out rather than a wilderness to be left alone. People came to waltz and row beside nature, not to escape into it.
The 'Royal' was added in 1955, after Queen Elizabeth II's train passed through during her 1954 tour. Close to a century and a half on, the park is the city's wild southern edge.
properties.source: NSW National Parks; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Audley weir in 1926 (Royal Australian Historical Society)

articles.heading: ON THE RIVER AT AUDLEY, NATIONAL PARK

properties.title: The guns of Middle Head
properties.summary: Rings of stone fortifications went up at Middle Head against a Russian attack that never came, and the tunnels later served as Cold War interrogation-resistance training rooms.
body: Every few decades colonial Sydney worked itself into a panic about the Russians, and each time the harbour sprouted another ring of stone defences. The batteries built at Middle Head from 1871, designed by James Barnet, were the showpiece. They had underground magazines and guns that could drop out of sight after firing, and for all their cleverness they were never once fired in anger.

The tunnels picked up a grimmer use much later. During the Vietnam era the Army ran interrogation-resistance courses here, locking trainees in concrete cells known as the tiger cages and subjecting them to darkness, noise and mock questioning to prepare them for capture.
Today the whole headland is parkland, and it holds one of the finest outlooks anywhere in Sydney.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust
properties.image_caption: A 10-inch gun being dismantled at Middle Head, 1893 (AWM)

articles.heading: Battery at Middle Head.
articles.newspaper: The Sydney Mail and New South Wales Advertiser

articles.heading: MIDDLE HEAD BATTERY. ALLEGED INSANITARY ARRANGEMENTS.

properties.title: Parliament of the people: the Domain
properties.summary: On Sunday afternoons in the Domain, anyone with a box to stand on could hold forth, and the crowds came to listen and to heckle. Socialists, evangelists, freethinkers and assorted cranks all took their turn.
body: Sydney's Speakers' Corner took shape in the Domain in 1878, when oratory shifted there from Hyde Park after a riot. Sunday was the only day off, with shops, pubs and sport all shut, so the grass under the Moreton Bay figs filled with people looking for something to do. Speakers climbed onto boxes and ladders and worked the crowd, and between the wars those crowds could swell to many thousands.

The line-up was gloriously mixed. Socialists argued with empire loyalists, suffragists shared the field with evangelists, and atheists traded blows with the devout, while unclassifiable visionaries held forth to whoever would stop. Every speaker was heckled with equal enthusiasm, and the best of them turned wit and sarcasm into a kind of sport. It was the city's free-speech safety valve and its finest free theatre.

Television and changing Sunday habits thinned the crowds, but the tradition hung on long enough to be remembered with real affection. From the late 1930s, Aboriginal campaigners such as Jack Patten, Pearl Gibbs and William Ferguson used the same patch of grass to argue for civil rights, proof that the Domain stayed a genuine platform for people the rest of the city preferred not to hear.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

articles.heading: SUNDAY ORATORS IN THE DOMAIN. THE PUBLIC PARKS BILL DEBATED.
SUNDAY IN THE DOMAIN. AMONG THE ORATORS.

properties.title: Jessie crosses the harbour
properties.summary: When Sydney's zoo crossed the harbour to its new Mosman home in 1916, the elephant Jessie made the trip by ferry. The new site was named Taronga, an Aboriginal word for beautiful view.
body: The zoo had outgrown its cramped quarters at Moore Park, and in 1916 the whole collection shifted to a bushland bowl above Athol Bay in Mosman. The new ground was named Taronga, an Aboriginal word usually translated as beautiful view, and the outlook over the harbour earned the name. By early September the move was well advanced, with hundreds of mammals, birds and reptiles rehoused.

The journey of Jessie the elephant became the part everyone remembered. In the early hours of 24 September she walked out through the old Moore Park gates with her keepers, made her way down to the water, and boarded a ferry steamer for the crossing. She balked at the steep ramp on the Mosman side and turned her back on it, so the keepers simply pushed her up backwards.

Taronga Zoological Park was officially opened on 7 October 1916 by the Premier, William Holman. Jessie lived on at the new zoo until 1939, carrying children on her back as she had for decades, and the harbour views she crossed for remain among the finest in the city.
properties.source: Taronga Conservation Society; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Jessie the elephant disembarks from the ferry on her way to Taronga, 1916

properties.title: A bullock paddock becomes an airport
properties.summary: In 1919 the aviator Nigel Love leased a bullock paddock on the Cooks River flats and laid out an aerodrome. It became Mascot, and it has been working ever since.
body: Nigel Love, a returned wartime pilot, came upon an unused bullock paddock at Mascot on the Cooks River flats and leased it late in 1919 for his aircraft company. He laid out an aerodrome there, and it soon found a public role. When Centennial Park was judged unsuitable for landings, Mascot was offered as the place where the great long-distance flights would come down.

The field had its first real taste of fame in August 1920. Ray Parer and John McIntosh had left England in a single-engined DH.9 back in January, backed by the whisky distiller Peter Dawson, whose bottle they carried the whole way for the Prime Minister. After seven gruelling months they reached Darwin, where the race had officially finished, then flew on south. A crowd of around 15,000 met them at Mascot, and the two men became the first to bring a single-engined aircraft from England all the way to Australia.

The Commonwealth bought the field in 1921, and it never closed. Mascot grew into Sydney's main airport and is now among the oldest continuously operating commercial airports in the world. In time it took the name of another aviator, Charles Kingsford Smith, but it began as a paddock where bullocks once grazed.
properties.source: Sydney Airport heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Parer and McIntosh arrive at Mascot after their England–Australia flight, August 1920

articles.heading: MORE MONEY ? Mascot Aerodrome NIGHT FLYING

properties.title: Three Chinatowns
properties.summary: Sydney's Chinatown has moved twice, following the markets and the city's Chinese community south. It settled at last around Dixon Street in the Haymarket.
body: The first cluster of Chinese homes and shops grew up in lower George Street, around The Rocks, among the gold-rush arrivals of the mid-19th century. By the 1870s the centre of Chinese trade had moved south to Campbell and Goulburn Streets near the markets, with people settling into Surry Hills nearby. Chinese market gardeners were feeding much of the city by then, and the community stayed close to where its produce was bought and sold.

When the produce markets shifted to the Haymarket and Darling Harbour early in the 20th century, the community followed once more. Dixon Street became the heart of a third Chinatown, and it held on through the long, hostile decades of the White Australia policy, which was built to keep people like its residents out.

The change of fortune showed in the streetscape. Dixon Street was closed to traffic for the Moon Festival in 1978, made a permanent pedestrian mall in 1979, and crowned with the ceremonial gates that the Lord Mayor opened in October 1980. By then the suburb had quietly outlasted the policy that had tried to shut its people out.
properties.source: City of Sydney, Chinatown heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Dixon Street today

properties.title: The killer track of Maroubra
properties.summary: A vast banked concrete saucer rose in the Maroubra sandhills in 1925, the fastest and deadliest motor racing track in the country.
body: The Olympia Motor Speedway opened in the Maroubra sandhills on 5 December 1925 before a crowd reported at 75,000. It was a mile of concrete bowl, banked at up to 37 degrees, steep enough that cars could cling to the wall at frightening speed. It was one of the great purpose-built motor racing venues of its day, and for a moment it was a sensation.

The thrill came at a terrible price. Drivers and riders kept dying, several at the same spot near the top of the banking where they shot over the edge. Sydney Dutton was killed in practice in 1926, Phil Garlick early in 1927, and Fred Barlow less than a month after that. The papers took to calling it the killer track.

By 1928 it was finished, too fast and too lethal to survive. The suburb later grew over the site, and in time the Coral Sea housing estate covered it. Almost nothing of the great saucer remains, beyond the name lingering in local memory.
properties.source: Randwick City Library local history
properties.image_caption: Racing cars at the Olympia Motor Speedway, Maroubra, 1925 (State Library of NSW)

articles.heading: MAROUBRA SPEEDWAY MOTOR CAR GRAND PRIX
MAROUBRA SPEEDWAY MOTOR PACED CYCLES SATURDAY'S PROGRAMME
MOTOR SPORT. MAROUBRA SPEEDWAY. MOTOR CYCLING GRAND PRIX

properties.title: The Greycliffe, 3 November 1927
properties.summary: On 3 November 1927 the Watsons Bay ferry Greycliffe was cut in two off Bradleys Head, the worst disaster in the history of Sydney Harbour.
body: On the afternoon of 3 November 1927 the little ferry Greycliffe left Circular Quay for Watsons Bay, carrying workers heading home and schoolchildren at the end of the day. Somewhere between Garden Island and Bradleys Head, the outbound mail steamer Tahiti, three times her length, struck her amidships and cut her clean in two.

Forty people drowned, among them several children, and small craft from every cove raced to haul survivors out of the water. One of the dead was Millicent Bryant, who only months before had become the first Australian woman to hold a pilot's licence. Later inquiries shifted the blame about, eventually settling on the ferry's steering and her master rather than the steamer's pilot.
It remains the deadliest day the harbour has ever seen. For decades afterwards the memory of the Greycliffe hung over a city that lived by its ferries.
properties.source: Australian National Maritime Museum; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Greycliffe's remains on Whiting Beach, November 1927

articles.heading: FERRY DISASTER.
articles.newspaper: Murrumburrah Signal and County of Harden Advocate

properties.title: The Block
properties.summary: At Redfern in 1973, Aboriginal people won the first urban land base held by an Aboriginal organisation, the cluster of terraces known as The Block.
body: Aboriginal families had been moving to Redfern for railway work since the 1940s, and by the early 1970s the suburb was the political heart of Aboriginal Australia. The country's first Aboriginal legal and medical services started nearby. When fifteen people squatting in an empty house were charged with trespass, the law student Bob Bellear led a push for proper housing.

In April 1973 the Whitlam government granted the newly formed Aboriginal Housing Company about $530,000 to buy and repair terraces on the block bounded by Eveleigh, Caroline, Louis and Vine Streets. It was the first time an Aboriginal organisation held land in a city, and it is often called the first successful land rights claim. The company kept buying houses for years, acquiring the last of them in 1994.

Funding was cut by the Fraser government from the mid 1970s and many homes fell into disrepair, before support returned under Hawke and Keating. A few streets away, Paul Keating chose Redfern Park for his 1992 speech that named dispossession plainly and said 'we' did it.
properties.source: Aboriginal Housing Company; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: The Aboriginal flag mural on Eveleigh Street, The Block, c. 2003

properties.title: The whales of North Bondi
properties.summary: On the headland above North Bondi, whales, fish and ancestral figures are cut into the sandstone, some of the oldest art in Sydney sitting in plain sight beside a golf course.
body: High on the sandstone shelf at North Bondi, much of it now within the Bondi Golf Course, are engravings of whales, sharks, fish and ancestral figures. The Birrabirragal people of the Eora made them, and the site, known as Murriverie, sits among the same deep-time tradition of harbour rock art that you can see at Grotto Point across the water. When Robert Campbell first recorded the area in 1899, he counted around 85 figures across three nearby sites.

In 1964 Waverley Council had the grooves re-cut to keep them visible. An advisory panel of academics recommended it, but no Aboriginal people were consulted, and the local Aboriginal community has since condemned the work as an act of desecration. The re-cutting saved the outlines and altered them in the same stroke, and a plaque now marks the decision.

Most of the crowds on the beach below have no idea that some of the city's most ancient art lies a short walk uphill. The engravings belong to a living culture, and the headland remains a place of meaning for the people whose ancestors made them.
properties.source: Waverley Council heritage; Australian Museum
properties.image_caption: Engravings on the North Bondi headland

properties.title: The Rum Rebellion
properties.summary: On 26 January 1808, exactly twenty years after the colony was founded, the New South Wales Corps marched on Government House and arrested Governor William Bligh in Australia's only military coup.
body: On the evening of 26 January 1808, Major George Johnston led the New South Wales Corps up Bridge Street with bayonets fixed and arrested Governor William Bligh at the first Government House, which stood on this spot. Around 400 soldiers took part. The roots of the quarrel lay in Bligh's campaign against the officer clique's stranglehold on trade, rum among the goods they controlled, and his bitter feud with the wealthy grazier John Macarthur.

The rebels circulated a cartoon of Bligh being dragged from under a bed, painting him as a coward. It was pure propaganda, and there is no good evidence it was true, but it did its work in the short term. The colony then sat under military rule for two years.

London's reply was to send Lachlan Macquarie with his own regiment to restore order, and Macquarie took office at the start of 1810. Today the Museum of Sydney stands on the footings of the old Government House, marking where the coup played out.
properties.source: Museum of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: 'The arrest of Governor Bligh', the rebels' propaganda image, 1808

articles.heading: TRUTH ABOUT BLIGH. Rum Rebellion. LAW AND HISTORY.

properties.title: Callan Park: asylum in a garden
properties.summary: Callan Park was built on a hopeful Victorian idea, that light, air and harbour views might help heal a troubled mind.
body: The colonial government bought the Callan estate above Iron Cove in 1873, hoping to ease the dangerous overcrowding at Gladesville. The new asylum followed the most optimistic theory of the day, the Kirkbride plan, which held that sunlight, gardens, sea air and graceful architecture were part of the cure rather than just the setting for it. Callan Park was proclaimed a separate institution in August 1878.

Colonial Architect James Barnet worked with the inspector of asylums, Dr Frederick Norton Manning, on a group of neo-classical sandstone buildings, and the grand Kirkbride complex was finished in 1885. It was designed for around 666 patients. Within a few years it held over a thousand, and the gentle ideal was steadily buried under crowding and tight budgets across more than a century of use.

Artists, students and campaigners now occupy much of the site, and the wide grounds have become the inner west's great accidental park. The buildings still carry the original promise in their stonework, even as the history inside them turned out far harder.
properties.source: NSW State Archives; Friends of Callan Park
properties.image_caption: The Kirkbride complex, Callan Park

properties.title: Sugar town: the CSR refinery
properties.summary: For more than a century Pyrmont ran on sugar, with the CSR refinery, its distillery and wharves shaping the whole peninsula.
body: The Colonial Sugar Refining Company opened its Pyrmont works in February 1878 and grew into one of Australia's industrial giants. The refinery, distillery and wharves spread along the peninsula, with workers' terraces packed in behind, so that Pyrmont became something close to a company town sitting inside the city.

Raw sugar came in from Queensland and Fiji, and golden syrup and rum went out. Alongside the refinery, the famous Pyrmont quarries cut away the headland's prized yellowblock sandstone, which built the General Post Office and the Town Hall. That quarrying wound down by the early 1930s while the sugar works rolled on.

CSR decided to wind down its Pyrmont sugar operations in 1992 and the refinery had closed by 1995. Apartment towers now cover the site as part of the Jacksons Landing development, and a few of them keep the bones of the old sugar stores within their walls.
properties.source: City of Sydney Archives; Pyrmont History Group
properties.image_caption: The CSR refinery in 1889 (Illustrated Sydney News)

articles.heading: DISASTROUS FIRE Pyrmont Sugar Refinery SPECTACULAR BLAZE.

properties.title: The masked city
properties.summary: Sydney met the pneumonic influenza of 1919 with compulsory masks, sweeping closures and emergency hospitals, including one at the Showground.
body: The war had just ended when the virus arrived. Pneumonic influenza reached Sydney in late January 1919, and the state moved fast. On 28 January the government closed schools, churches, theatres, halls and libraries, and within days masks were made compulsory in public. Pubs and racecourses shut their doors and public gatherings were banned.

Emergency hospitals went up wherever there was room. The Royal Agricultural Society Showground here at Moore Park was turned into a hospital, with the Royal Hall of Industries pressed into service as a morgue. Randwick Racecourse became a 430-bed ward, and closed schools across the suburbs filled with the sick. By September the influenza had killed more than 6,000 people across New South Wales, thousands of them in Sydney, over two winter waves.
For a century the photographs of masked commuters looked merely quaint. Then 2020 made them familiar again.
properties.source: NSW State Archives; Royal Australian Historical Society
properties.image_caption: A man in a flu mask during the 1919 influenza epidemic, photographed by Sam Hood (State Library of NSW)

articles.heading: Influenza Epidemic. MASKS TO BE WORN. SYDNEY, Thursday.
INFLUENZA MASKS.
articles.newspaper: Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners' Advocate

articles.heading: Influenza Epidemic SYDNEY, Wednesday.
articles.newspaper: The Manaro Mercury, and Cooma and Bombala Advertiser

properties.title: The bodyline summer
properties.summary: In the summer of 1932-33, England bowled fast and straight at the body rather than the stumps, and the tactic they called leg theory turned a cricket tour into a quarrel between two governments.
body: The series opened at the Sydney Cricket Ground on 2 December 1932. England's plan was simple and brutal. Harold Larwood and Bill Voce bowled short and fast at the batsman's body, with a cluster of fieldsmen waiting on the leg side to snap up any fending shot. The aim was to muzzle Don Bradman, though Bradman sat this Test out after falling out with the Australian Board, so he never faced a ball in the match.

Into that gap stepped Stan McCabe. The 22-year-old hooked and pulled everything aimed at his ribs and head, making 187 not out in a shade over four hours. Larwood still claimed ten wickets in the match and England won by ten wickets, but McCabe's innings became the one people remembered, a piece of nerve that gave Depression-era crowds something to cheer.

The real flashpoints came later in the tour at Adelaide, where Bill Woodfull was struck over the heart and Bert Oldfield had his skull fractured. The Australian Board cabled the MCC in London calling the bowling unsportsmanlike, and the row climbed all the way to prime ministers worried about trade between the two countries. The tactic was eventually outlawed, and the word bodyline stuck.
properties.source: SCG Trust; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: The bodyline summer, 1932 (State Library of NSW)

articles.heading: SUNDRY SPORT LARWOOD AND BODY-LINE BOWLING.
BODYLINE BOWLING LARWOOD AND VOCE TO CONTINUE.
Body-Line Bowling.
articles.newspaper: The Wingham Chronicle and Manning River Observer

properties.title: Dancing at the Troc
properties.summary: The Trocadero was Sydney's grand art deco dance palace on George Street, where the city danced for thirty-five years.
body: The Trocadero opened on George Street on 3 April 1936, a streamlined deco palace with a floodlit Hawkesbury sandstone tower and a foyer of marble and polished granite. It was built for crowds of up to two thousand, with Frank Coughlan's big band on the stage.

For thirty-five years it was where Sydney came to dance. There were debutante balls and radio broadcasts, and packed wartime nights when American servicemen taught the city to jitterbug. The Queen attended a luncheon there too.

Television and rock and roll slowly emptied the floor. The Troc closed on 5 February 1971, and the wreckers took what many thought was the finest interior the city ever lost. A cinema complex stands on the site today.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Inside the Trocadero (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: The Push
properties.summary: From the 1950s the back room of the Royal George Hotel on Sussex Street filled nightly with the Sydney Push, a crowd of libertarians, anarchists and unbelievers who lived by argument and beer.
body: From the 1950s the back room of the Royal George Hotel on Sussex Street filled most nights with the Sydney Push. They were libertarian philosophers, journalists, gamblers and students, drawn together by argument, beer and a flat refusal of respectability in Robert Menzies' Australia. The ideas had grown out of the circle around John Anderson, the combative professor of philosophy at Sydney University, though the Push pushed his thinking well past where he was willing to go.

Its alumni went out and rattled the world. Germaine Greer, Clive James, Robert Hughes, Lillian Roxon and Frank Moorhouse all passed through. The Push wrote few books of its own. Its real product, members liked to say, was talk, and the people that talk set loose.

The Royal George stopped being the heart of it around 1964, when the crowd drifted to other inner-city and Rocks pubs. By then the habit of fierce, unsentimental argument had spread far enough to outlive any single back room.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: The Royal George Hotel, the Push's parliament

properties.title: The lottery and the kidnapping
properties.summary: In 1960, weeks after his family won a fortune in the Opera House Lottery, eight-year-old Graeme Thorne was abducted from this Bondi street in Australia's first kidnapping for ransom.
body: The Sydney Opera House was paid for largely by public lotteries, and in 1960 the names and addresses of the big winners were printed in the papers. Bazil and Freda Thorne had won 100,000 pounds, an enormous sum at the time. A month later, on 7 July, their eight-year-old son Graeme was taken as he waited near his Bondi home to be driven to school. A man rang demanding 25,000 pounds.

The boy was already dead. His body was found on 16 August under a rock ledge in bushland at Seaforth, wrapped in a picnic rug, about six weeks after he disappeared. The investigation that followed became a landmark in Australian forensic science. Traces on the body and the blanket, including cypress and pine fronds, pink mortar and animal hair, pointed to the home of Stephen Bradley, a Hungarian-born immigrant who had fled the country by ship.

Bradley was traced through shipping records, arrested in Colombo and brought back to face trial. He was convicted of murder in March 1961 and sentenced to life. The case reshaped the law and the culture around it. Kidnapping became a distinct offence in New South Wales, and lottery winners won the right to keep their names private. Many Australians have since spoken of the Thorne case as the moment a certain national innocence quietly ended.
properties.source: Justice & Police Museum; National Archives of Australia

articles.heading: KIDNAP CASE HOPES CENTRED ON MERRYLANDS Tragic hoax story can now be told
Kidnap drama CITY'S ROLE IN SEARCH

properties.title: 24 June 1978
properties.summary: On the night of 24 June 1978, a street march down Oxford Street ended in police violence and arrests, and out of it grew the Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras.
body: It began as a festive evening march for gay and lesbian rights, called in solidarity with protests overseas. The crowd set off down Oxford Street towards the city late on 24 June 1978. When they reached the edge of Hyde Park, police seized the lead truck and its loudspeaker, and the night turned. The marchers headed instead towards Kings Cross, where police moved in and arrested 53 people. Many were beaten in the cells.

The Sydney Morning Herald then printed the names, addresses and occupations of those charged. People lost jobs and were cut off by their families. The public outcry over the arrests, helped by sympathetic coverage, saw the charges gradually dropped, the last of them by the end of 1979.

The marchers came back the next year, and the year after that. The protest grew into the Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras, now one of the world's great LGBTQ festivals, with the original marchers known as the 78ers leading the parade each year. In 2016 the New South Wales Parliament, the police and the Herald all formally apologised for what happened that night.
properties.source: Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras, 78ers; State Library of NSW Pride archive
properties.image_caption: The parade today, born from the 1978 night march

properties.title: The first night inside the Heads
properties.summary: On 21 January 1788, Governor Phillip's exploring party came through the Heads and camped on this small beach, the first place Europeans slept on Port Jackson.
body: Scouting ahead of the fleet from Botany Bay, Governor Arthur Phillip took a longboat and two cutters up the coast to look at the harbour Cook had named but never entered. On 21 January 1788 they came through the Heads into what Phillip would call one of the finest harbours in the world. They camped on this little beach inside South Head, the first nights any European spent on the shores of Port Jackson.
The Gadigal, whose Country took in the south shore of the harbour, knew the cove and the headland by names recorded later as Kutti and Cadi. A small beach nearby still carries the name Kutti today.

Phillip stayed two nights here, then explored further up the harbour before returning to Botany Bay. Within days he had chosen Sydney Cove over Botany Bay for the settlement. On 26 January the fleet was anchored there, and the colony began.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Watsons Bay heritage walk
properties.image_caption: Camp Cove, the fleet's first night inside the Heads

properties.title: Vinegar Hill: the convict uprising
properties.summary: On 4 March 1804, more than 200 convicts broke out of the Castle Hill government farm in Australia's largest convict uprising, crushed the next day at a place loyalists named Vinegar Hill.
body: On the night of 4 March 1804, around 233 convicts, many of them Irish prisoners transported after the 1798 rising, broke out of the government farm at Castle Hill. Their leader was Philip Cunningham, a veteran of that Irish rebellion. They meant to gather others, seize Parramatta and Sydney, and take ships to carry willing convicts home to Ireland.

Martial law was declared and the New South Wales Corps, under George Johnston, rode out to meet them. The two sides came together on 5 March on a hillside near Rouse Hill that loyalists dubbed Vinegar Hill, after the Irish battle of 1798. Cunningham was lured into a parley under a flag of truce and arrested. The troops then charged the poorly armed rebels and scattered them. About fifteen convicts were killed on the field.

Cunningham, badly wounded, was hanged without a proper trial at the commissariat store at Windsor, which he had boasted he would burn down. Nine rebel leaders were executed in all, and some bodies were left hanging in chains as a warning to others. Hundreds more were flogged or sent to harsher labour before martial law was lifted a week later. A memorial to the battle stands at Castlebrook, Rouse Hill.
properties.source: National Museum of Australia; Hawkesbury district histories
properties.image_caption: The Castle Hill rebellion, contemporary watercolour, 1804

articles.heading: WHEN CONVICTS REBELLED AT CASTLE HILL
articles.newspaper: The Cumberland Argus and Fruitgrowers Advocate

articles.heading: IN THE EARLY DAYS. THE CASTLE HILL INSURRECTION.

properties.title: The whalers of Mosman Bay
properties.summary: Whaling was one of colonial Sydney's biggest earners, and Archibald Mosman ran his greasy, smelly station from this deep north-shore bay.
body: Archibald Mosman set up his whaling station in this sheltered bay in 1831, on a four-acre grant on the north side of the harbour. Ships were fitted out here, oil was casked and rigging was tarred. The work was filthy and reeked of rendered whale, which suited everyone in town just fine, since the deep water sat well clear of polite Sydney.

Whale oil and bone counted among the colony's most valuable exports in the years before the gold rush. The station ran as a small industrial village, with a stone wharf and several solid buildings for storage and for the ships' officers and crews.

Whaling faded in the 1840s and the bay slowly turned residential, but it kept Mosman's name. The Barn, his stone storehouse, still stands. It is thought to be the oldest surviving stone building on the lower north shore, and parents bought it in 1925 for the local Scout troop, which saved it from demolition.
properties.source: Mosman Council local history; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Fullwood's watercolour of the old whaling station, Mosman's Bay

articles.heading: MOSMAN'S BAY. ROMANCE OF AN OLD WHALING STATION.
MOSMAN'S ROMANCE. AN OLD WHALING STATION.

properties.title: Paddy's Markets
properties.summary: Sydney's people's market has traded on these Haymarket blocks since the 1830s, and the name Paddy's has stuck for around 150 years.
body: In 1834 Governor Bourke shifted the hay, grain and cattle markets to Campbell Street, in the area we now call Haymarket. A scrappier fringe market grew up alongside the official one, and that informal market for ordinary buyers became known as Paddy's. Nobody is quite sure how it got the name. It has been in use since at least the 1870s, and the best guess is that it was borrowed from the famous Paddy's Market in the Irish quarter of Liverpool in England.

Generations of growers and sellers worked these blocks. From the 1870s, Chinese market gardeners settled in the area and supplied the city's fruit and vegetables for the best part of a century, an early anchor for the Chinatown that grew nearby.

The red brick market halls that still stand date from 1909 to 1912, built to replace the failed Belmore Markets. Their Edwardian facades were preserved and restored, and Paddy's now trades on beneath the Market City towers built over the top.
properties.source: Sydney Markets history; City of Sydney Archives
properties.image_caption: Workers at the Haymarket markets, photographed by Max Dupain, 1930s (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: Fairyland on the Lane Cove
properties.summary: Deep in the bush on the Lane Cove River, the Swan family ran a quirky picnic ground with a dance floor, swings and painted fairytale figures among the trees.
body: Robert Swan and his family bought the riverside land in 1895 and cleared it for market gardens around 1900. Watching nearby picnic grounds draw the crowds, they slowly turned their patch over to fun, and from about 1905 the place took shape as a pleasure ground. By the 1920s it had a kiosk, swings, a flying fox and a spinning razzle-dazzle ride, with little painted figures like Bo Peep tucked among the trees.

Getting there was half the charm. Ferries carried picnic parties up the river, and a wharf received boats that could land 70 people at a time. When the upper river ferry company folded in 1918, Fairyland bought some of its boats to run its own service.

The motor car, spreading suburbs and a new road bridge slowly stole the magic. A run of floods in 1967, 1968 and 1969 finished it off, and the bush closed back over. National Parks took on the land late in the 1970s. Walkers in Lane Cove National Park still come across old concrete steps and wonder what once stood here.
properties.source: Lane Cove National Park heritage; Ryde & district local histories
properties.image_caption: Fairyland pleasure grounds on the Lane Cove River

articles.heading: The Lane Cove River -- A Picturesque Picnicing Resort.
PLEASANT PLACES FOR THE TOURIST UPPER LANE COVE. A DREAM OF BEAUTY."
LOVELY LANE COVE. THE BEAUTIES ABOVE THE BRIDGE. PREMIER VISITS THE UPPER REACHES. WHAT MAY BE ACCOMPLISHED.

properties.title: The powder magazines of Bantry Bay
properties.summary: Sydney moved its explosives to a tucked-away Middle Harbour bay reachable only by water, where the magazines still stand sealed off in the bush.
body: By the early 1900s, storing the colony's explosives on Goat Island and in floating hulks felt far too close to town. An official inquiry recommended a safer, more remote site, and from 1908 the trade shifted to Bantry Bay on Middle Harbour. A line of sandstone and brick magazines went up along the waterline, and the new magazines were declared Public Magazines in 1915.

There were no roads in. Powder lighters ferried explosives between the magazines and waiting ships, while picnickers were kept well clear of the danger. The store fed Sydney's great public works, including the building of the Harbour Bridge, and the navy used the site during the Second World War.

The depot closed in May 1974, undone by a switch to moving explosives by rail. The buildings survive almost intact in Garigal National Park, sealed off from visitors and quietly contaminated, one of the best-preserved abandoned precincts around the harbour.
properties.source: NSW National Parks; Sydney Harbour Trust records

articles.heading: RESUMED FOR POWDER MAGAZINE HEAVY COMPENSATION CLAIMED BANTRY BAY PROPBRTY

properties.title: The Coal Loader
properties.summary: From 1920 this huge waterfront bunker at Balls Head transferred Newcastle coal into ships through tunnels cut beneath its platform, and it now holds gardens and one of the harbour's best community plots.
body: From 1920 colliers from Newcastle unloaded into the great waterfront bunker on the Waverton peninsula at Balls Head. Coal dropped through chutes in the platform into skips below, which a cable hauled railway shunted along four tunnels driven into the headland, feeding the ferries and steamers berthed at the wharf.

The old loading gear gave way to a high speed conveyor in the mid 1970s, and the loader kept sending coal out to Japan until it shut in 1992. After that it sat as a vast industrial relic beside the Balls Head bushland, one of the early pieces of working harbour foreshore handed back for public use.

Following a long conservation program, the Coal Loader Centre for Sustainability opened in 2011. The platform now carries gardens, solar panels and leased community allotments, and is one of the largest publicly accessible green roofs in the country, with the tunnels still running intact below.
properties.source: North Sydney Council heritage

articles.heading: COAL BUNKERING. PROGRESS OF BALL'S HEAD WORKS. UNLOADING OPERATIONS.
COAL HANDLING. BALL'S HEAD BUNKERING SCHEME. AMERICAN ENGINEER ARRIVES.

properties.title: The dock that cut an island loose from its name
properties.summary: To dock a battleship in wartime, Australia blasted out the Captain Cook Graving Dock and joined Garden Island to the shore, finishing the country's largest engineering job of the war in 1945.
body: In 1940, with war pushing towards the Pacific and no dry dock in the hemisphere big enough to hold a battleship, work began on the Captain Cook Graving Dock at Garden Island. It was the largest engineering project Australia attempted during the war, and after Singapore fell the job ran around the clock.

About thirty acres of harbour were reclaimed to tie Garden Island to the Potts Point shore, and a dock of roughly 347 metres was blasted from the sandstone and lined with concrete over five years. The British carrier HMS Illustrious limped in for emergency repairs on 2 March 1945, three weeks before the Governor-General, the Duke of Gloucester, formally opened the dock on 24 March.
The navy holds the island still. The original garden of 1788, where First Fleet sailors planted vegetables and gave the place its name, survives as a patch of lawn inside the naval base.
properties.source: Royal Australian Navy; Dictionary of Sydney

articles.heading: CAPTAIN COOK DOCK
articles.newspaper: Daily Commercial News and Shipping List

properties.title: The longest arch in the world (briefly)
properties.summary: When it opened in 1964, the great concrete arch at Gladesville was the longest single concrete span in the world, a record it held for about fifteen years.
body: The 1964 Gladesville Bridge replaced a steel lattice bridge of 1881 that had grown hopelessly congested as private cars took over Sydney's roads. The new crossing carried Victoria Road over the Parramatta River on a single concrete arch of 305 metres, the longest of its kind on earth at the time. It was built from precast concrete box segments stressed together, and was one of the first bridges in the world designed with the help of a computer.

The arch was meant to be part of the North Western Expressway, a motorway that was never finished and was finally abandoned by the Wran Government in 1977. That is why a world-record bridge ended up feeding ordinary arterial roads, springing from Drummoyne on one side and landing at Huntleys Point on the other.

The record stood for about fifteen years until Croatia's Krk Bridge overtook it in 1980. Gladesville is still the longest concrete arch span in Australia. Engineers continue to admire it, while the drivers crossing the top of the arch barely notice it is there.
properties.source: Engineers Australia heritage; Roads & Maritime archives
properties.image_caption: The new arch in 1966

properties.title: The flagstaff that watched for sails
properties.summary: Flags have flown on South Head since 1790, which makes it arguably the oldest continuously used signal site in Australia.
body: In January 1790 Governor Phillip sent a party from the Sirius up onto South Head to raise a flagstaff and a lookout. The hungry colony wanted one thing above all else, a sail on the horizon carrying food and news, and this hill was the first to see it. The lookout earned its keep on 3 June 1790 when it flagged the Lady Juliana, the leading ship of the Second Fleet.

For generations the signal crew announced every arrival to the town by hoisting flags, so word of the wider world climbed this hill before it reached Sydney Cove. A code introduced in 1832 let messages relay onward as far as Government House at Parramatta, telling officials a ship's type, origin and cargo.

The present stone station was built to a design by Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis across the late 1830s and early 1840s. Its modern successor, Marine Rescue NSW, still keeps watch from the same headland, which gives the site a fair claim to being the oldest continuously used signalling station in the country.
properties.source: Woollahra Council heritage; Marine Rescue NSW
properties.image_caption: The signal station on South Head, flags have flown here since 1790

articles.heading: FLAGSTAFF COST Move For Retention Of Station
(The Signal-Master to the Port-Master.) South Head Signal Station, 2lst August, 1857.
THE SIGNAL MASTER TO THE PORT MASTER. South Head Signal Station, 21st August, 1857.

properties.title: The pilots' village
properties.summary: Watsons Bay worked the harbour as a pilots' base and fishing village long before it became a Sunday outing, and the seafood business on its beach dates back to 1885.
body: Watsons Bay was working water long before it was a postcard. Harbour pilots were stationed here from the 1790s, ready to row their whaleboats out to ships coming through the Heads, and a fishing village grew up around the beach. The pilots gave the place its early character, and the regattas of the 1860s drew crowds to the bay.

The seafood story on the beach goes back to 1885, when Henry and Hannah Newton opened tea rooms here. The Newtons were the grandparents of Alice Doyle, and the business passed down the family. After the original rooms blew down in 1907, their daughter Eva built the Ozone Cafe on the same spot, and Alice revived it in 1948 as the seafood restaurant that has carried the Doyle name ever since. It claims to be Australia's oldest continually running fish and chip shop.
The ferry, the pub and the cliff walk out to the Gap turned Watsons Bay into one of the harbour's favourite day trips, and it has stayed that way for well over a century.
properties.source: Woollahra Council heritage; Doyle's history
properties.image_caption: Doyle's on the beach, fish and chips since 1885

articles.heading: Clovelly, Watson's Bay. (See ilustrations on this and previous page.)

properties.title: The summer republic of Palm Beach
properties.summary: On the sandy neck between ocean and Pittwater, Palm Beach grew into Sydney's summer retreat, and the world now knows its beach as Summer Bay.
body: Palm Beach sits on a sandy isthmus with the Pacific on one side and the calm water of Pittwater on the other. The land was sold off in residential blocks in the 1912 subdivision, and through the 1920s it became the summer ground of Sydney's establishment, where weekend shacks slowly grew into mansions.

The surf club, founded in 1921, helped pioneer surf-boat racing on this coast, and the long northern beach drew carnivals and crowds through the interwar years. Barrenjoey Headland and its lighthouse close off the northern end and give the place its familiar profile.

Since 1988 the beach and the headland have stood in for the fictional town of Summer Bay in Home and Away. The soap has run for decades and sells worldwide, which has made this one of the most internationally recognised stretches of Australian sand, second only to Bondi.
properties.source: Northern Beaches Council heritage; Palm Beach SLSC
properties.image_caption: The Barrenjoey isthmus between ocean and Pittwater

articles.heading: THE SURF SURF BOATS FOR PITTWATER
Social Surf Season Opens at Palm Beach

properties.title: Fisher's Ghost
properties.summary: Australia's most famous ghost story began at Campbelltown in 1826, when a man swore he had seen the spectre of a murdered farmer pointing from a bridge rail to the paddock where the body lay.
body: Frederick Fisher, a Campbelltown farmer, vanished on 17 June 1826. His neighbour George Worrall put it about that Fisher had sailed for England and signed his property over before he left. Four months later a respectable local settler named John Farley rushed into the hotel in a state, swearing he had just seen Fisher's ghost sitting on the rail of a creek bridge. The apparition said nothing. It simply pointed to a paddock down the creek, then was gone.

Trackers searched the paddock and found Fisher's body. Worrall was arrested, confessed and went to the gallows. The court records say nothing of any ghost, and one tidy theory holds that Farley invented the spectre to mask how he really knew where the body lay. The truth of that is past recovering.

Campbelltown has never let the story go. The creek carries Fisher's name, and every November the town parades for its phantom at the Festival of Fisher's Ghost. A colonial murder has become a piece of local pride, retold so often that the ghost now outranks the crime.
properties.source: Campbelltown City Council, Festival of Fisher's Ghost; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Campbelltown still parades for its ghost every November

articles.heading: The Legend of Campbelltown. FISHER'S GHOST.

properties.title: The tunnels of the Hero of Waterloo
properties.summary: This sandstone corner pub at Millers Point carries Sydney's favourite tall tale, of a cellar trapdoor and a smugglers' tunnel that delivered drunk patrons to short-handed ships.
body: The Hero of Waterloo went up around 1843 from sandstone hewn out of the Argyle Cut, took its licence in 1845, and has poured ever since from its corner of Lower Fort Street. It is the second oldest surviving hotel in the city, just behind the Lord Nelson up the road. Beneath it runs the legend every generation in The Rocks retells.

The story goes that drunk patrons dropped through a cellar trapdoor, were dragged along a tunnel to the water and sold to ships looking for crew, waking up shanghaied somewhere past the Heads. The cellars and a bricked tunnel mouth are real and still shown to visitors. How many sailors ever made that involuntary journey is the part no record can settle.

The pub keeps a resident ghost as well. Anne Kirkman, a publican's wife, is said to still play the piano. Whether you believe the tunnel or not, the building itself is the genuine article, a convict-era hotel that has traded without a break for the best part of two centuries.
properties.source: Hero of Waterloo heritage; The Rocks oral history collections
properties.image_caption: The Hero of Waterloo on Lower Fort Street

properties.title: The lake under the city
properties.summary: St James station opened in 1926 with platforms and tunnels for railway lines that were never built, and one abandoned stretch has since flooded into a hidden underground lake.
