body: John Bradfield's city railway dreamed bigger than it was ever built. St James opened in December 1926 with four platforms, two of them set aside for eastern and western suburbs lines that never came. The branching tunnels were dug, then left to sit empty beneath the city.

Those dead tunnels found other uses. One southern stretch was fitted out as an air raid command shelter in the Second World War, complete with concrete blast doors, and the tunnels have since served as a mushroom farm and a film set. Another section slowly filled with water and became an artificial lake, around a kilometre long and up to five metres deep, fed by the steady drip from the Botanic Gardens above. Urban legend gives it a population of blind eels.

The ghost platforms are real and occasionally opened for tours. They lie directly under Hyde Park, a few metres below the lunchtime crowds, a piece of unfinished railway frozen at the moment the money ran out.
properties.source: Sydney Trains heritage; Transport for NSW
properties.image_caption: Inside the never-used tunnels beneath St James

articles.heading: CITY RAILWAY Work Delayed ST. JAMES'S STATION
IN THE CITY RAILWAY TUNNELS. CITY STREET COMPLETELY CLOSED
CITY RAILWAY. St. James' Shunting Facilities. DEPARTMENTS PLANS.

properties.title: Shoot through like a Bondi tram
properties.summary: Sydney once ran one of the largest tram networks on earth, then pulled it apart inside a decade. All that survives in daily speech is the idiom.
body: At its 1923 peak Sydney's tramways stretched about 291 kilometres, the largest network in Australia and one of the busiest in the world. By the mid-1940s the trams carried well over a million passengers a day. The Bondi expresses got away so smartly that 'shoot through like a Bondi tram' entered the language as the local way of describing a quick exit.

After the war the road lobby and the buses won the argument. The system came apart line by line through the late 1950s. The Bondi line closed in February 1960, and the final tram of all ran on 25 February 1961, rolling back from La Perouse to the Randwick Workshops. Many of the old cars were broken up and burnt at Randwick.
The idiom has now outlived the trams by more than sixty years. Sydney even has trams again, since the light rail follows parts of the old alignments through the eastern suburbs.
properties.source: Sydney Tramway Museum; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: A tram smash at Bondi Junction (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: The most haunted address in Australia
properties.summary: When the North Head Quarantine Station finally closed, its long reputation for ghosts simply carried on under new management.
body: For more than 150 years, ships carrying disease were held off North Head before their passengers could enter Sydney. The station ran from 1832 until February 1984, and around 572 people died there and were buried on the headland. The early records are incomplete, so the real toll may be higher.

The ghost stories arrived with the visitors who came after. Tours running several nights a week trade in the same recurring figures, including a hospital matron, a limping gravedigger from the third cemetery, and a girl in a nightdress said to reach for people's hands. Staff at the hotel now housed in the old quarters keep an incident log of their own.

Treat all of it as folklore and the place still gets under your skin. The surviving buildings hold the city's quarantine history in one preserved cluster above the harbour, kept much as the last patients left it.
properties.source: Q Station; Sydney ghost-tour oral tradition (folklore, presented as such)

properties.title: Wanda Beach, 11 January 1965
properties.summary: The unsolved 1965 murders of two fifteen-year-old friends at Wanda Beach changed how a generation of Australian parents thought about letting children roam the sand.
body: Marianne Schmidt and Christine Sharrock were both fifteen, best friends and neighbours from West Ryde. On Monday 11 January 1965 they took Marianne's four youngest siblings to Cronulla for the day. The wind was up and the beach was closed, so the group sheltered in the dunes and the two girls walked north along Wanda Beach. Their partly buried bodies were found the next day.

The investigation became the largest in Australian history to that point, with around 7,000 people interviewed. It has never closed. Suspects, theories and the occasional flicker of hope from new forensic testing have come and gone across sixty years, and it remains the oldest unsolved homicide on the New South Wales books.

The case deserves to be told plainly. Two named girls, fifteen years old, were killed, and their younger siblings survived to carry it. That is the weight of Wanda Beach, not the puzzle of who did it.
properties.source: NSW Police unsolved homicide; National Archives
properties.image_caption: Wanda Beach, north of Cronulla

properties.title: Sea-serpent season
properties.summary: Between the wars, Sydney's newspapers could be relied on to land a harbour monster most silly seasons.
body: In the 1930s the harbour seemed to grow a monster about as regularly as it ran regattas. Ferry passengers, fishermen off the Heads and picnickers around Middle Harbour reported humps, strange wakes and serpents, and the papers obliged with artists' impressions and a parade of sober explanations. A log, perhaps. A whale, a seal, or simply a hoax.

The 1934 reports were especially busy, helped along by the world's new fascination with the Loch Ness Monster, which had filled British and Australian papers the year before. No serpent was ever hauled up to settle the matter.

As the Opper cartoon on this card cheerfully admits, 'sea-serpent season' was a recognised fixture of the press right across the English-speaking world. Sydney simply did its bit to keep the tradition afloat.
properties.source: Trove digitised newspapers, 1930s (the flap is the story)
properties.image_caption: 'The sea-serpent season upon us again', the press knew the genre well

properties.title: The railway that never came
properties.summary: For decades the Northern Beaches were promised a railway, and the Harbour Bridge was even built with tracks ready for it. The trains never came.
body: John Bradfield's grand transport scheme for Sydney imagined a railway reaching out to the north-east, over his new Harbour Bridge and on through the lower north shore towards Manly and the beaches beyond. The bridge was built with two extra tracks on its eastern side waiting for those trains. The line itself was never part of the 1915 railway Bill, and the promised extensions to the Beaches were never built.

With no railway to feed them, the spare bridge tracks were put to other use. Trams ran across them from 1932 until 1958, after which they were paved over as road lanes. One of those lanes became a bus lane in 1992. The old Spit Bridge of 1924, a low timber crossing with no tram lines, was eventually replaced by the present bridge in 1958, and the dreamed-of railway across it never came either.

The Depression, the rise of the car and the spread of bus services finished the idea off, line by unbuilt line. The Northern Beaches remain the largest part of Sydney never reached by train. You can still read the ghost of the plan in odd road reservations and in a bridge that was quietly built ready for trains that never arrived.
properties.source: Transport for NSW heritage; Bradfield scheme records
properties.image_caption: The old Spit Bridge in the 1920s, waiting for a railway that never came

articles.heading: North Shore Railways. EXTENSION TO MANLY WANTED. A LEAGUE FORMED.
LINKING UP THE SUBURBS. EXTENSION LEAGUE FORMED. RAILWAY SCHEME NOT OPPOSED.

properties.title: Tumbalong to Darling Harbour
properties.summary: An Eora gathering place for seafood, then a century of wool stores and the country's biggest rail goods yards, rebuilt for the 1988 Bicentenary.
body: For the Gadigal and Wangal people of the coastal Eora, the cockle-rich inlet was Tumbalong, a place where seafood is found. The First Fleeters called it Long Cove for its length, and by the early 1800s it was Cockle Bay, after the shell middens along its shore. In 1826 Governor Ralph Darling put his own name to it.

Cockle Bay became the city's industrial back door. Wool stores and warehouses crowded the water, the iron Pyrmont Bridge swung open for shipping, and from the 1870s the largest railway goods yards in the country spread across the Ultimo side. The trade ran for more than a century. The last goods train pulled out in 1984.

In that same year Premier Neville Wran announced a plan to clear the yards and hand the foreshore back to the public in time for the 1988 Bicentenary. The result was a convention centre, museums and a monorail looping overhead, opened by Queen Elizabeth II on 4 May 1988.
The 1988 precinct has since been pulled down and rebuilt twice. One older name came back along the way. The park at the heart of it all is now called Tumbalong.
properties.source: City of Sydney Archives; Placemaking NSW
properties.image_caption: Cockle Bay, Darling Harbour today (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

properties.title: The city's outdoor room
properties.summary: Opened from the 1890s so the grand new GPO could be seen, Martin Place became the place Sydney gathers to mourn, to protest and to meet.
body: Martin Place began as a widened laneway, opened up from the early 1890s to give a clear view of James Barnet's grand General Post Office once it was finished in 1891. Named for Sir James Martin, three times Premier and Chief Justice of New South Wales, it grew over the following decades into something the city had never quite planned, a civic heart in the middle of the business district.

The Cenotaph made it sacred ground. On Anzac Day 1927, veterans found an elderly woman laying flowers at the unfinished memorial and stayed to keep vigil with her, and the first organised dawn service followed the next year. The Cenotaph's stone was dedicated in August 1927, and the completed monument was unveiled by Premier Thomas Bavin in February 1929. Sydney has gathered there every Anzac Day since.

The first stage of the pedestrian plaza opened in 1971, and by 1979 the whole street had become a mall, the city's outdoor room. War-bond rallies, the Occupy camp and the sea of flowers after the 2014 Lindt Cafe siege have all unfolded on its granite. To meet someone at Martin Place needs no further address.
properties.source: City of Sydney; RSL NSW, Cenotaph history
properties.image_caption: The Cenotaph before the GPO, the city's hearth

articles.heading: MARTIN PLACE, SYDNEY.
articles.newspaper: Casino and Kyogle Courier and North Coast Advertiser

properties.title: Mile zero
properties.summary: Macquarie Place was the colony's first proper square, home to the 1818 obelisk that every main road in New South Wales is still measured from, and to an anchor of the wrecked First Fleet flagship Sirius.
body: Macquarie Place was the young town's forum, a triangle of ground above the Tank Stream where Governor Macquarie raised an obelisk in 1818. Francis Greenway designed it, and from this stone the colony's main roads were measured as they pushed inland. Many of those distances are still reckoned from the same spot today.

Under the figs sit an anchor and a cannon from HMS Sirius, the First Fleet flagship. The Sirius came to grief on the reef off Slaughter Bay at Norfolk Island on 19 March 1790, a loss that left the struggling colony with almost no shipping. The relics now rest in the heart of the city the fleet had founded two years earlier.

From the south-western corner a bronze figure of Thomas Sutcliffe Mort looks on. Mort was a wool seller, dock owner and pioneer of meat refrigeration. After his death in 1878 a meeting of Sydney working men resolved to honour him, and the statue, paid for by his dock company, other firms and public subscription, was unveiled in 1883 as one of the city's first public statues.
properties.source: City of Sydney; Museums of History NSW
properties.image_caption: Macquarie Place, the colony's first square, with the Sirius anchor

articles.heading: The Distance Obelisk, Macquarie-place.
articles.newspaper: The Sydney Mail and New South Wales Advertiser

properties.title: Windsor: Macquarie's chief town
properties.summary: The grandest of Macquarie's five Hawkesbury towns, laid out on high ground after the floods and home to Greenway's St Matthew's church.
body: The Hawkesbury floods of 1809 drowned the river farms that fed the young colony, and Governor Macquarie answered with a plan. In December 1810 he proclaimed five towns on ground above the flood line. Windsor, Richmond, Castlereagh, Wilberforce and Pitt Town. Windsor was the largest, named for the royal town on the Thames, and it became the working capital of the Hawkesbury.

At its heart stands St Matthew's, designed by the convict architect Francis Greenway. Macquarie laid the foundation stone in 1817 and the church was finished around 1820, though it was not consecrated until 1822. It is the oldest Anglican church in Australia and is often called Greenway's finest work. The square around it, with its courthouse and old inns, gives Windsor one of the most intact Macquarie-era townscapes anywhere.

The river still shapes the place. The farms that once fed Sydney now share the floodplain with weekenders and day trippers, and the floods still come. Windsor remembers both the practical genius of Macquarie's planning and the stubborn fact that no town plan can fully outwit the Hawkesbury.
properties.source: Hawkesbury City Council heritage; Macquarie's journals
properties.image_caption: Greenway's St Matthew's, the finest colonial church in Australia

articles.heading: Historic Hawkesbury.
articles.newspaper: Dungog Chronicle : Durham and Gloucester Advertiser

articles.heading: PRESSMEN TOUR HISTORIC WINDSOR AND NOBLE HAWKESBURY

properties.title: Richmond on the Hawkesbury
properties.summary: One of the five Hawkesbury towns Governor Macquarie laid out in 1810, on river flats the Darug call Dyarubbin. From 1925 it became home to the RAAF's first air base outside Victoria.
body: On 6 December 1810 Governor Macquarie proclaimed five towns on the high ground above the Hawkesbury, after floods had ruined the farms on the flats. Richmond was one of them, taking its name from Richmond Hill nearby, which Governor Phillip had named in 1789 after the Duke of Richmond. The river itself runs through Darug Country, where it is known as Dyarubbin.

Richmond stayed a farming town for a century. The lowlands still flood and still feed the district, much as they did in the colony's early grain-growing years, when the Hawkesbury supplied a large share of Sydney's wheat.

In 1925 the paddocks east of town became RAAF Station Richmond, the air force's first base outside Victoria and the oldest in New South Wales. The land had already served as a military flying school during the First World War. The air force's story and the river's have run side by side here ever since.
properties.source: Hawkesbury City Council heritage; RAAF history

articles.heading: THE HAWKESBURY DISTRICT. Richmond, Saturday Night.

properties.title: The Dunera Boys
properties.summary: In 1940 Britain shipped about 2,000 refugees from Nazism to Australia as 'enemy aliens'. The men interned here became one of the country's great strokes of luck.
body: On 6 September 1940 the troopship Dunera berthed at Pyrmont after 57 days at sea. Among the roughly 2,542 men aboard were close to 2,000 anti-Nazi German and Italian civilians, most of them Jewish refugees, whom Britain had rounded up and deported in the panic of a feared invasion. The voyage was brutal. The guards robbed and bullied the internees and threw their belongings overboard.

Australia locked the men away in camps at Hay and later Orange in New South Wales, and at Tatura in Victoria. From late 1941 most were released and reclassified as friendly refugees. A good number stayed on and joined the army's 8th Employment Company, a labour unit raised in 1942 for the Dunera and Queen Mary men.

The 'Dunera Boys' went on to shape Australian science, art, music, law and business out of all proportion to their numbers. What began as a wartime blunder by Britain turned into one of the best things ever to happen to this country, and it is now remembered with gratitude rather than shame.
properties.source: National Archives of Australia; Dunera Association
properties.image_caption: HMT Dunera, 1940

properties.title: White City
properties.summary: From 1922 the White City courts at Rushcutters Bay were the home of Australian tennis, hosting Davis Cup ties and the NSW Championships for sixty years.
body: White City opened at Rushcutters Bay in 1922, built on the site of a former amusement park and keeping its name. It was laid out as a home for the New South Wales Championships, and it quickly became the centre of the Australian game.

Through the 1950s and 1960s, the golden decades when Harry Hopman captained Australia to fifteen Davis Cups, White City staged some of those famous ties along with the NSW Championships year after year. It was here that a girl raised at Barellan in country New South Wales, Evonne Goolagong, came up through the ranks and made her name.

In time the grass gave way to synthetic surfaces, the big tournaments shifted out to Homebush, and the old stadium fell quiet. The Hakoah Club bought the site around 2010 and rebuilt it as a modern sports and tennis venue. For sixty years, though, White City was where Sydney spent its summer.
properties.source: Tennis NSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Championship tennis at White City, 1934 (Sam Hood)

properties.title: The hammerhead crane
properties.summary: For more than sixty years the navy's 250-tonne giant defined the Garden Island skyline. In 2014 the Commonwealth pulled it down.
body: The Garden Island hammerhead crane went to work in March 1951 after seven years of building, though the navy did not declare it officially finished until January 1952. It could lift 250 tonnes in a single hoist, enough for a battleship gun turret, a submarine section or a whole patrol boat. When it was completed it was the largest crane in the southern hemisphere, and it was still the largest in Australia half a century later.

It was also a rare machine. By the time it came down it was one of only about fifteen cranes of its kind left anywhere in the world. That rarity, and its place in the working harbour, turned it into a heritage cause. Engineers, historians and the National Trust argued for years that the great steel silhouette should be saved.
The case did not hold. The crane was obsolete by the 1990s, and in 2014 the Commonwealth demolished it. The harbour skyline still carries the gap where it stood.
properties.source: Engineers Australia; Naval Historical Society
properties.image_caption: The hammerhead crane before its 2014 demolition

articles.heading: GARDEN ISLAND CRANE UNLOADS U.S. ENGINES
LINER STRATHMORE'S MAST REMOVED AT GARDEN ISLAND

properties.title: The Royal Easter Show at Moore Park
properties.summary: For 116 years the country came to the city each Easter at Moore Park, until the Show moved out to Homebush in 1998.
body: The Agricultural Society's show settled at Moore Park in 1882, after the Government granted the land the year before, and it grew into the city's great annual ritual. There was woodchopping and show jumping, the district exhibits with their produce murals, and sample bags that began as free handouts of advertising before they became the showbags children saved up for. The smell of the country drifted over Surry Hills every Easter.

Generations of Sydney children measured the year by it. People who never went near a farm came to watch the grand parade, eat their way through the food halls and queue for the rides. For more than a century the Show was where the bush and the city met.

The Show moved out to Homebush in 1998, ending a run of 116 years at Moore Park. The old grounds became Fox Studios and the Entertainment Quarter, where the 1924 members' grandstand and the great pavilions, the Royal Hall of Industries from 1913 and the Hordern from 1924, still stand among the cinemas.
properties.source: Royal Agricultural Society of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Crowds at the Royal Easter Show, c.1910–1920, photographed by Sam Hood (State Library of NSW)

articles.heading: ROYAL AGRICULTURAL SOCIETY. THE EASTER SHOW. SYDNEY, Wednesday.
ROYAL AGRICULTURAL SOCIETY. The Easter Show. SYDNEY, MONDAY.
articles.newspaper: Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners' Advocate

properties.title: Griffin's temple of rubbish
properties.summary: Walter Burley Griffin gave Sydney its grandest building for burning rubbish, and the city tore it down anyway.
body: Built between 1934 and 1936 and burning from 1937, the Pyrmont Incinerator was a high point of the practice Walter Burley Griffin ran with Eric Nicholls. The pair designed reverberatory incinerators for councils right across Australia, but this one was the showpiece. They wrapped a working furnace in reinforced concrete and dressed it in Mayan and Art Deco ornament, a ziggurat of a building that treated the burning of household waste as something worth doing with style.

At its busiest the incinerator swallowed more than a hundred truckloads of rubbish a day. As the inner city changed after the war the furnaces grew obsolete, and the last load was burned in 1971. The building then sat idle and slid into disrepair through the following two decades.

A loud heritage campaign tried to save it, but a National Trust listing was not enough. The incinerator was demolished in 1992, and Meriton apartments went up on the site soon after. Sydney lost the centrepiece of its Griffin legacy. The smaller incinerator at Willoughby outlived it and now works as a gallery, the Incinerator Art Space, listed on the State Heritage Register.
properties.source: Walter Burley Griffin Society; City of Sydney Archives
properties.image_caption: Griffin's incinerator in 1939 (City of Sydney Archives)

properties.title: Crown Street Women's Hospital
properties.summary: For ninety years, the Crown Street Women's Hospital was where a great many Sydneysiders were born.
body: Dr James Graham founded the Women's Hospital in 1893, starting small in Hay Street with a couple of beds for the so-called genteel poor. In 1897 it settled at 351 Crown Street in Surry Hills, the address that gave it its name. Over the next ninety years it delivered an astonishing share of the city. The last baby born there before it closed in March 1983 was reportedly its 273,569th.

By its golden jubilee in 1943 it was the largest maternity hospital in New South Wales, a teaching hospital tied to the University of Sydney that trained generations of midwives and doctors. It ran the first fertility clinic in Australia and New Zealand, and it earned an international name for its care of newborns and its work reducing eclampsia, once a leading cause of death in childbirth.

The hospital also carried out the adoption practices of its day, and the grief left by forced and pressured adoptions is something later generations have had to reckon with. Even so, the building holds a particular place in local memory. Ask many Sydneysiders born before 1983 where they came into the world, and the answer is often Crown Street.
properties.source: NSW State Archives; Sydney Local Health District heritage
properties.image_caption: The hospital where a quarter of old Sydney was born

properties.title: Wentworth Park: swamp, sport, dogs
properties.summary: Reclaimed from Blackwattle Swamp and named for W.C. Wentworth, the park hosted cricket and football before greyhound racing arrived in 1939 and made it the home of the dogs in New South Wales.
body: Wentworth Park began as the muddy edge of Blackwattle Swamp. By 1879 the reclamation was nearly done, and the new recreation ground opened in 1882, taking its name from the colonial figure William Charles Wentworth. For its first decades it was a sporting field in the ordinary sense, with cricket drawing crowds of ten thousand by the 1890s, then rugby, soccer and the rough local football of Glebe.

The dogs changed all that. The mechanical tin hare had first lured greyhounds around a track at nearby Harold Park in 1927, and the new sport spread fast. The licence for Wentworth Park came through in 1938, and the track opened beneath the great railway viaduct on a Saturday in October 1939. It soon became the headquarters of greyhound racing in the state, the Saturday night home of a cheap punt and a loud crowd.

The dogs still run under the viaduct, but their days at the park are numbered. The racing lease ends in 2027, after which the land is to pass to the City of Sydney for sports fields and parkland while the streets around it are rezoned for housing. What the place finally becomes is now a matter for Glebe to argue over.
properties.source: Glebe Society; Greyhound Racing NSW
properties.image_caption: The 1932 grandstand at Wentworth Park

articles.heading: BALMAIN BEAT GLEBE AT WENTWORTH PARK.
WENTWORTH PARK COURSING CLUB, GLEBE, SYDNEY.

properties.title: The night the sky fell on the east
properties.summary: On the evening of 14 April 1999 a supercell that forecasters thought would head out to sea swung over Sydney's east instead, dropping giant hail with no warning and causing the costliest insured disaster in Australian history to that point.
body: The autumn evening of 14 April 1999 was mild and unremarkable. A storm cell had formed that afternoon north of Nowra, and forecasters expected it to drift out to sea, so no warning went out. Instead it swung up the coast and over the eastern suburbs. Between about 7.45 and 8.05pm it dropped an estimated 500,000 tonnes of ice on the city.

Confirmed hailstones reached 9cm across, big enough to punch through slate roofs and shatter windscreens. The worst of it ran along the coast between Lilli Pilli and Darling Point, around 25km apart. About 24,000 houses and 70,000 vehicles were damaged, 23 aircraft and helicopters were battered on the tarmac at Sydney Airport, and a man fishing offshore was killed when lightning struck his boat.

The insured bill came to roughly A$1.7 billion, the costliest single natural disaster Australian insurers had ever faced, more than the 1989 Newcastle earthquake. For months afterwards the eastern suburbs wore a patchwork of blue tarpaulins while roofs waited their turn to be repaired.
properties.source: Bureau of Meteorology; Australian Institute for Disaster Resilience
properties.image_caption: Hailstones of 14 April 1999

properties.title: The Cammeraygal of the lower north shore
properties.summary: The Cammeraygal were the dominant clan on the lower north shore and the keepers of the tooth ceremony that made boys into men. Their Country is remembered in the name Cammeray and in the sites of Berry Island.
body: The Cammeraygal held the lower north shore of the harbour, and the First Fleet's chroniclers noticed them straight away. David Collins recorded them as the most numerous and most powerfully built of the harbour clans, and theirs alone was the right to perform the tooth ceremony that turned boys into men. Clans from around the harbour brought their young initiates across the water to the Cammeraygal for it. Collins watched the great initiation gathering at Yoo-lahng, today's Farm Cove, in 1795.

Berry Island at Wollstonecraft reads like one long record of that life. The foreshore carries layered shell middens of gadyan, the Sydney cockle, along with grooves worn into the rock from sharpening stone axes. Among the carvings is a large marine creature whose meaning was never written down.

The Gadyan Track now loops past these sites, and North Sydney Council runs walks along it with an Aboriginal heritage guide. The suburb of Cammeray still carries the clan's name, a small daily reminder of who held this Country first.
properties.source: Aboriginal Heritage Office; David Collins, An Account of the English Colony (1798); North Sydney Council
properties.image_caption: Berry Island, Cammeraygal country, Gadyan Track

properties.title: The engraving galleries of Ku-ring-gai Chase
properties.summary: A row of wallabies hops across the sandstone on a platform off the Basin Track, one of more than 800 recorded Aboriginal sites in this single national park.
body: A short signed detour off the Basin Track leads to a sandstone platform carrying about 53 engraved figures. There are fish, a whale, men and women, boomerangs, and a procession of wallabies caught mid-hop. They show best when low morning or evening light fills the grooves. The figures were first pecked as rows of small pits, then ground into continuous lines.

This is the Country of the Garigal, whose clan name belongs to the upper Northern Beaches and Broken Bay. The park's name comes from 'Guringai', a word coined by an outsider in 1892 and now regarded as inauthentic, so local clan names are the truer guide. The wider park holds over 800 recorded Aboriginal sites, part of the many thousands across the Sydney basin and one of the densest concentrations of rock engravings in Australia.
Look, but never touch. The grooves are old beyond reliable dating, and once worn away they cannot be made again.
properties.source: NPWS; Aboriginal Heritage Office, Filling a Void (2015)
properties.image_caption: Engraved sandstone, Ku-ring-gai Chase

properties.title: Tumbalong: the bay built into the city's mortar
properties.summary: Early Sydney was held together with Gadigal history, its mortar burnt from the shells of meals eaten over thousands of years.
body: The Gadigal knew the head of Darling Harbour as Tumbalong, a name recorded as meaning the place where seafood is found. Generations of shellfish meals had piled up along its shores in great middens, and it was these heaps of shell that led the colonists to call the bay Cockle Bay.

The young colony had no limestone, so it sent in the lime-burners. They fed the middens into their kilns, burning thousands of years of accumulated shell into the lime that mortared early Sydney's brick and stone. The first buildings of the town were quite literally bound together by Gadigal history.
Governor Darling renamed the bay after himself in 1826. The older name returned with the bicentennial redevelopment of Darling Harbour, when Tumbalong Park opened in 1988.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW Eora resources
properties.image_caption: Cockle Bay as working Darling Harbour, 1900

properties.title: The ceremony at Farm Cove, 1795
properties.summary: At nightfall in early 1795, Eora men beached their canoes at Wogganmagully and let the colony's judge advocate watch boys be made men.
body: In the first days of February 1795, Eora men landed their nawi after dark at Wogganmagully, the cove the colonists called Farm Cove, to hold the yoo-lang erah-ba-diang. This was the multi-day initiation in which the harbour clans' boys were made men. It ended with the removal of the upper right front tooth, a rite that only the Cammeraygal were authorised to perform.

Judge advocate David Collins was invited to attend, and he wrote that he was fortunate enough to watch the whole of the second operation on 2 February. His 1798 Account set the ceremony down scene by scene in engravings by James Neagle, showing the boys carried on men's shoulders, the dances and the drawing of the tooth. It remains the fullest record of an Eora initiation ever made.

Among the initiates was Nanbarry, the Cadigal nephew of the clan leader Colebee, who acted as his sponsor and pressed a cooked fish to the boy's wounded gum. The cove was a ceremonial ground for two decades before it became the Botanic Garden.
properties.source: David Collins, An Account of the English Colony (1798); State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Wogganmagully, Farm Cove, the ceremonial ground

properties.title: The fires on the water
properties.summary: Eora women fed the harbour's people, fishing by hook and line from bark nawi with small fires burning aboard.
body: The harbour's fishers were women. They worked hook and line from nawi, the canoes made from tied bark, often with babies aboard and a small fire burning on a clay and seaweed hearth between their knees so the catch could be cooked on the water. At night the First Fleet's diarists looked out on a harbour dotted with these floating fires.

Their crescent-shaped hooks, called bara, were ground from the broad whorl of the turban shell, and the surgeon George Worgan reckoned them the most ingenious thing the Eora made. The women twisted their fishing lines from bark fibre, sang to the fish, and spat chewed mussel overboard as burley. Men speared from the rocks while the women brought in the steady food that fed the clans.

In 2012 torch-lit bark nawi slid back onto Darling Harbour for the first time in roughly a hundred years, launched for the maritime museum's Nawi conference. Judy Watson's large bara sculpture near the Opera House now honours the fisherwomen.
properties.source: Australian National Maritime Museum; Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Nawi Cove, Barangaroo, named for the canoes

properties.title: The ancestor under the bus shelter
properties.summary: A cable trench under a Narrabeen bus shelter in 2005 uncovered a man who had lain in the dune for some 3,700 years, the oldest dated human remains then known in the Sydney region.
body: In January 2005, contractors digging a cable trench beneath a bus shelter near the corner of Octavia and Ocean Streets at Narrabeen uncovered the skeleton of an Aboriginal man. He had been about 30 to 40 years old and tall, around 183 centimetres, and radiocarbon dating put him at roughly 3,700 years before present. He was the oldest dated human remains then known in the Sydney region.

Seventeen stone artefacts lay with him, several lodged against his vertebrae. These were barbs from at least two death spears, the first direct archaeological evidence in Australia of a death by spearing. A blow to the head and the position of the body led researchers to read it as a ritual killing, possibly a punishment under traditional law.

A road, a footpath and a bus stop had all been built over him while he lay undisturbed below. His remains were placed in care in consultation with the Metropolitan Local Aboriginal Land Council, an ancestor returned to the record of the beaches he belonged to.
properties.source: McDonald et al., Antiquity 81 (2007); Australian Museum
properties.image_caption: Narrabeen, the dune country where he lay for 3,700 years

properties.title: The ship that named a suburb
properties.summary: The paddle steamer Collaroy ran ashore in fog in 1881 and sat on the sand for three years and eight months. The beach quietly took her name and never gave it back.
body: On 20 January 1881 the Newcastle paddle steamer Collaroy was feeling her way through dense fog when she misjudged Long Reef and ran hard onto the beach just beyond it. All 24 passengers reached shore in the lifeboats, and the livestock aboard, some 40 pigs and 30 sheep, were driven into a nearby paddock. One of the crew, a Shetlander named Hercules Dalziel, drowned during the rescue.

She was not going anywhere in a hurry. The Collaroy settled so deep into the sand that picnickers could climb aboard her paddle-boxes, and she became an attraction in her own right. People began calling that stretch of Narrabeen Beach after the stranded ship, and the name stuck to the suburb that grew up around it.

Refloating her took a purpose-built cradle, hydraulic rams, the tug Leveret and three attempts before she slid off at high tide on the evening of 9 September 1884. Stripped back to a sailing ship, she met her end in 1889, wrecked in another fog off the California coast while bound for Eureka with a load of coal. The ship had taken her own name from a sheep station near Merriwa, and now it belongs to a Sydney beach.
properties.source: Australian National Maritime Museum; Northern Beaches Council heritage
properties.image_caption: The Collaroy on the beach that took her name

properties.title: Seven miles from Sydney, a thousand miles from care
properties.summary: The ferry run to Manly built a seaside resort and bred a fleet of famous steamers. One slogan summed up the whole appeal of the crossing.
body: From 1853 Henry Gilbert Smith began buying up land at Manly with the idea of giving Sydney its own Brighton, complete with Norfolk pines, promenades and a resort reached by a ferry ride. The Port Jackson and Manly Steamship Company turned the journey itself into the attraction. Its slogan, 'Seven miles from Sydney and a thousand miles from care', did the rest.

A line of well-loved ferries carried the holiday crowds across. The twins Dee Why and Curl Curl arrived in 1928. The Bellubera caught fire at her Kurraba Point depot on 16 November 1936, killing two of the crew, and came back rebuilt as Australia's first diesel-electric ship.

The grandest of them was the South Steyne, the largest steam ferry in the world, built by Henry Robb at Leith in Scotland and launched in April 1938. She sailed herself out to Sydney over a voyage of 64 days, grounding briefly in the Suez Canal on the way, all to spend her working life on a seven-mile run. She carried Manly's crowds until 1974 and survives today.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Heritage Fleet
properties.image_caption: South Steyne at Manly Wharf, 1939

articles.heading: HARBOUR MISHAP Manly Ferry Steamer.
articles.newspaper: Daily Commercial News and Shipping List

properties.title: The riot that closed the pubs at six
properties.summary: In February 1916 thousands of army trainees walked off Casula camp, drank Liverpool dry and seized trains to the city, and the chaos handed the temperance movement its case for six o'clock closing.
body: On 14 February 1916, trainees at Casula camp learned their weekly drill would be lengthened. They downed rifles and struck, and around three thousand marched on Liverpool, where they emptied the hotels without paying. From there they commandeered trains into the city, drawing in more men along the way.

In Sydney the crowd wrecked shops and the German Club. At Central Station soldiers pelted an armed picket with bottles and stones and turned a fire hose on the guard. A revolver was fired, by most accounts by Ernest Keefe, a twenty-year-old Light Horse trainee, and the guard answered with about fifty shots. Keefe fell dead on the platform and six others were wounded. Courts martial later discharged hundreds of men.

The wowsers could not have asked for a better gift. Four months later New South Wales voted for six o'clock hotel closing as a wartime measure. It outlasted the war by decades, surviving until 1955 and giving Sydney its long, frantic six o'clock swill.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Archives
properties.image_caption: The six o'clock swill the riot helped bring, Max Dupain

articles.heading: Soldiers' Riot at Liverpool and Sydney.
articles.newspaper: The Hillston Spectator and Lachlan River Advertiser

properties.title: Freedom as wages: the road over the mountains
properties.summary: A gang of about thirty convicts cut a road over the Blue Mountains in under six months, and their wages were paid in pardons and freedom.
body: From the ford on the Nepean at Emu Plains, where Blaxland, Lawson and Wentworth had crossed in May 1813, William Cox set his men to work. His gang of about thirty convicts, helped by a handful of free men and soldiers, cut a cart road roughly 101 miles over the Blue Mountains to the Bathurst plains. They built it between July 1814 and January 1815, raising more than a dozen bridges and splitting hundreds of posts and rails, all without serious accident.

Cox, a Windsor magistrate, was known among convicts for his readiness to recommend mercy. He offered freedom as the wage for this work and he delivered, with pardons and tickets of leave following the last mile. The reward was as much the point of the road as the road itself.

Macquarie drove his carriage along the finished route in April 1815. The artist John Lewin painted the vice-regal party at the Nepean crossing, and at the western end the Governor proclaimed Bathurst, the first inland town in the country.
properties.source: Cox's journal, State Library of NSW; ADB; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Surviving works of Cox's Road at Mount York

properties.title: The Black Town: the institution and Maria Lock
properties.summary: The mission school that gave Blacktown its name took Darug children from their families, and its first pupil, Maria Lock, outlasted the lot of it.
body: Governor Macquarie's Native Institution took Aboriginal children in as residential pupils. Parents were forbidden to remove them and were allowed a single visit each year, on the day of the annual Native Feast. Darug people today regard the school as an early link in the chain that led to the Stolen Generations. In 1823 it shifted from Parramatta to a site on Richmond Road, near land granted to the Darug men Colebee and Nurragingy. The settlement around it became known as the Black Town, and the name fixed itself to the suburb.

The institution's first pupil was Maria, daughter of Yarramundi of the Boorooberongal clan. She enrolled in December 1814, and in the 1819 examinations she took first prize ahead of more than a hundred settler children. On 26 January 1824 she married Robert Lock, an illiterate convict carpenter then working on the new buildings at Black Town. It was the first officially sanctioned marriage between an Aboriginal woman and a convict in the colony.

Maria secured land at Liverpool and Blacktown and raised ten children. Her 1878 burial entry called her the last of the Aboriginals from Blacktown, which was bitterly wrong. Dozens of Darug families trace their descent through her. In 2018 the Native Institution site was returned to the Darug people.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register; Dharug Strategic Management Group
properties.image_caption: The Native Institution site, Richmond Road, 1866

properties.title: 'Yankstown': the airfield and the bunker
properties.summary: Kittyhawks were thrown together here in a hurry, American squadrons gave it the nickname Yankstown, and Sydney's air defence was run for a time from a suburban picture theatre.
body: Bankstown aerodrome was proclaimed in June 1940. During the invasion scare of March 1942 it filled with Curtiss Kittyhawk fighters, assembled at speed from crated parts after shipments were diverted to Australia. American fighter units passed through as well, and their presence earned the field its wartime nickname, Yankstown.

The odd part is where the defending was done. From 14 March 1942, No. 1 Fighter Sector Headquarters plotted Sydney's air defence from the Capitol Theatre in Chapel Road, and later from air-raid shelters in the tunnel beneath St James station. The purpose-built bunker at Condell Park, with thick reinforced concrete meant to shrug off a 500-pound bomb, was not commissioned until January 1945. By then the danger had long since faded.

The aerodrome closed as an RAAF station in 1947, and the bunker was gutted by fire in 1972. It still sits intact beneath a quiet park near the end of Taylor Street. The airfield itself went on to become one of Australia's busiest general-aviation airports.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register (Bankstown Bunker); RAAF unit histories
properties.image_caption: A RAAF Kittyhawk, the type rushed through Bankstown in 1942

properties.title: Palestine, just behind Wanda Beach
properties.summary: Charles Chauvel staged the Light Horse charge in the dunes behind Cronulla, and the same sand later stood in for deserts and the post-apocalypse.
body: The thundering Beersheba charge in Forty Thousand Horsemen was filmed early, on 1 February 1938, in a single day among the Kurnell dunes. Chauvel borrowed real Light Horsemen who were in Sydney for the sesquicentenary celebrations, and a crowded camera team worked the sand, Frank Hurley among them. Hurley had a pit dug and planked over, with a hole left for his lens, so he could shoot the bellies of the horses leaping overhead. Much of the charge across open ground, though, was shot later near Orange on the Western Plains.

A fake desert village was raised on the sandhills for the battle scenes, and the finished film reached cinemas on Boxing Day 1940. It became one of Australia's first international hits and made a star of Chips Rafferty. Released ten months after the first Australian troops landed in the Middle East, it carried a clear propaganda charge.

The dunes went on to a long second career, standing in for the desert in The Rats of Tobruk and Phar Lap, the wasteland of Mad Max Beyond Thunderdome, and countless commercials. All the while sand miners carted away tens of millions of tonnes of the place. What remains behind Wanda Beach is now heritage-listed.
properties.source: NFSA; NSW State Heritage Register (Cronulla Sand Dune)
properties.image_caption: Chips Rafferty in Forty Thousand Horsemen (1940)

properties.title: Como: Sydney's Lake Como
properties.summary: When the railway bridged the Georges River in 1885, a land agent's fondness for Italy turned a rocky headland into Sydney's favourite picnic ground.
body: The Illawarra railway crossed the Georges River on Boxing Day 1885, and a platform opened at Como at the end of the new bridge. James Murphy, manager of the Holt-Sutherland Estate Land Company, thought the river scenery worthy of Lake Como in Italy, and he had both the name and the resort ready to go. From 1886 the pleasure grounds spread across the headland, with terraces, summer houses, a dance pavilion and rowing boats on the water below. A paddle steamer joined the trade in 1899.

The Como Hotel had stood since 1878, built as a club for the German railway workers and licensed as a hotel around 1890. It became the natural haunt of day trippers and honeymooners drawn out from the city by the train.

The 1885 bridge still stands beside its 1972 replacement and now carries a cycleway. The hotel was the first building in Sutherland Shire to win heritage listing, in 1977. It burned down on 3 November 1996 and reopened on the same date in 2001, exactly five years later. A fire-blackened door from the old pub hangs inside the restaurant named for it, the Burnt Door.
properties.source: Sutherland Shire heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Como and its railway bridge, c. 1904

articles.heading: A GEORGE'S RIVER PLEASURE RESORT. OFFICIAL OPENING.

properties.title: An elephant at Botany
properties.summary: From 1851 a picnic at Botany came with an elephant and a Bengal tiger, in what is remembered as Australia's first private zoo. Decades later the same grounds drew betting crowds to watch champion sprinters, including the Aboriginal runner Charlie Samuels.
body: In the 1840s the timber merchant William Beaumont and his partner James Waller turned the grounds behind the Sir Joseph Banks Hotel into a landscaped pleasure garden. In 1851 they brought in the animals from the colony's earlier zoo in Hyde Park, among them a docile elephant, a Bengal tiger, Himalayan bears and Manila red deer. It became the country's first private zoo, and a Botany day out by steamer was suddenly a real attraction. More than five thousand people came on Boxing Day in 1852.

In the 1880s the grounds found a second life as the home of professional foot racing. Thousands packed the Botany handicaps to bet on the sprinters, and the biggest draw was Charlie Samuels, a Kamilaroi man and the finest runner in the colony. He raced the visiting world champion Harry Hutchens four times and won three of those contests. His best mark at Botany was startling for the day.
The 1840s hotel still stands on Anniversary Street, converted now to apartments. Beside it is a park where sculpted animals mark the spot where the old menagerie once stood.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Bayside Council heritage
properties.image_caption: The surviving 1840s Sir Joseph Banks Hotel, Botany

articles.heading: Old Sydney. SIR JOSEPH BANKS HOTEL, BOTANY.
articles.newspaper: The Queanbeyan Age and Queanbeyan Observer

articles.heading: SIR JOSEPH BANKS FAVILION AND PLEASURE GARDENS.

properties.title: Saywell's New Brighton
properties.summary: A tobacco merchant built his own steam tramway, shark-proof sea baths and a grand hotel on the sandhills south of Sydney, and turned the place into a seaside resort.
body: In November 1885 Thomas Saywell opened a private steam tramway running down Bay Street from Rockdale station to Lady Robinsons Beach. At the end of the line he laid out a resort. There were twin shark-proof sea baths, each about 200 feet long, one for men and one for women, along with a picnic ground and later a racecourse.

In 1887 he crowned it with the grand New Brighton Hotel. The hotel lost its licence in 1892 amid drunken disorder, much of it driven by the hard times of the 1890s depression. From January 1893 it served two years as the genteel first home of Scots College, until the racecourse opened in 1895.
Saywell called his resort New Brighton. The suburb was later renamed Brighton-le-Sands, after the seaside town on Merseyside, to avoid confusion with another New Brighton near Manly.

The trams ran on under government ownership until September 1949, when buses replaced them. One curious relic survives. A steam tram motor that Saywell bought in 1905 now sits in MOTAT, the Museum of Transport and Technology, in Auckland.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography (Saywell); Bayside Council heritage
properties.image_caption: Saywell's New Brighton Hotel and steam tramway, 1887

articles.heading: SAYWELL'S NEW SEA BATHS, LADY ROBINSON'S BEACH, ROCKDALE.
articles.newspaper: The Sydney Mail and New South Wales Advertiser

articles.heading: Lady Robinson's Beach. (See illustrations on page 1382.)

properties.title: The bushman's bible
properties.summary: From a rented hall off Pitt Street, The Bulletin built a radical, republican and distinctly Australian voice in 1880, and it made the names of Lawson and Paterson.
body: J.F. Archibald and John Haynes launched The Bulletin on 31 January 1880 with about £140, a second-hand press and battered type. The first 3,000 copies sold out on Archibald's account of the hanging of Captain Moonlite and his mate Thomas Rogan at Darlinghurst Gaol.

The paper was published from Bulletin Place, a lane on land reclaimed at the mouth of the Tank Stream and said to be the only Sydney street named for a publication. From there 'the bushman's bible' preached nationalism and republicanism, and it built a literature to match. In September 1892 Archibald gave Henry Lawson five pounds and a rail ticket to Bourke, and over the years he also published Banjo Paterson and Miles Franklin.

A.G. Stephens set the literary tone with his 'Red Page', and Norman Lindsay began drawing for the paper in 1901. When Archibald died in 1919 he left money for two lasting things, the Archibald Prize for portraiture, first awarded in 1921, and the Archibald Fountain in Hyde Park.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: J.F. Archibald and John Haynes, founders of The Bulletin

articles.heading: Birth of "The Bulletin." J. F. ARCHIBALD'S MEMORIES.
J. F. ARCHIBALD DEAD. FOUNDER OF THE "BULLETIN." UNIQUE PERSONALITY.

properties.title: Busby's Bore: the tunnel that wandered
properties.summary: Sydney's second water supply was a 3.6 kilometre tunnel dug by hand by convicts and finished in 1837. With a fall of just 53 centimetres end to end, and no proper survey, the bore wandered off line the whole way.
body: When the Tank Stream failed and fouled, Sydney turned to Busby's Bore. John Busby designed a tunnel to carry water from the Lachlan Swamps, today's Centennial Park, to a reservoir at Hyde Park. Convicts dug it by hand from 1827, mostly with picks through rock and often up to their waists in water, and it was finished in 1837. The bore ran about 3.6 kilometres, five feet by four, and was gravity-fed across a fall of only 53 centimetres from one end to the other.

Dug without a proper survey, the tunnel wandered off line underground in a way that was close to an engineering disaster. Yet it worked. The bore delivered up to about 400,000 gallons a day and remained Sydney's main supply until the 1850s.

Water emerged at Hyde Park high enough to reach the General Hospital up on Macquarie Street, and fed the standpipes where townsfolk queued with buckets and carts. For a generation, much of Sydney drank from a hand-dug hole that never ran where it was meant to.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Water heritage
properties.image_caption: Busby's Bore, 1857

articles.heading: BUSBY'S BORE. SYDNEY'S EARLY WATER SUPPLY.
PADDINGTON PUMP Busby's Bore HISTORIC WATER SUPPLY
WATER SUPPLY RELIC BUSBY'S BORE TO BE FILLED IN

properties.title: The harbour minefield
properties.summary: From a depot at Chowder Bay, the colonial Submarine Mining Corps wired a field of electric mines across Sydney Harbour. The mines were never once fired at an enemy.
body: Chowder Bay was chosen in 1889 and the Submarine Mining Depot was finished by 1892. It became the base of the colonial Submarine Mining Corps, whose task was to defend Port Jackson with a field of electrically detonated mines. The mines sat across the shipping channels, joined to the shore by cables run out from the depot, ready to be set off by command when an enemy ship steamed through.

The field was operational through the 1890s, fired from the depot at the press of a switch. It never went off in anger. No enemy fleet ever forced the Heads, and the rise of the submarine soon made the whole scheme of fixed harbour mines look antique. The Corps was disbanded in 1922.

The 19th-century buildings survive as part of the Harbour Trust's Chowder Bay precinct. The Trust calls the old depot the only remaining mine laboratory in Australia and the most intact facility of its kind in the world. The rooms where men once wired the harbour's defences now hold restaurants looking out over the water.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Submarine Miners' Depot precinct, Chowder Bay

articles.heading: Submarine Miners. IN CAMP AT CHOWDER.
THE SUBMARINE MINERS AT CHOWDER BAY. END OF THE ENCAMPMENT.

properties.title: The Athens of the inner west
properties.summary: Postwar Greek migration turned Marrickville into the heart of Greek Sydney, with up to a third of the city's Greeks settling here and Greek heard on every corner.
body: Greek migrants had been running cafes, milk bars and continental delicatessens across Sydney and country New South Wales since early in the twentieth century. It was the great chain migration between 1957 and 1975 that made Marrickville Greek. Cheap housing, good transport and the suburb's belt of factories drew newcomers in such numbers that by the 1960s as many as a third of Sydney's Greeks lived here.

With them came churches, Greek-language schools, clubs and newspapers. Locals reckoned you heard more Greek than English along the main streets. The St Nicholas Greek Orthodox parish, founded around 1961 and opened in its Livingstone Road church in 1966, became the community's anchor.

Later Vietnamese and other arrivals reshaped the suburb again, as migrant suburbs tend to. The Greek mark stayed, and in 2022 the Inner West Council formally named the stretch of Marrickville Road between Livingstone and Victoria roads 'Little Greece'.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Inner West Council heritage
properties.image_caption: St Nicholas Greek Orthodox parish, Marrickville

properties.title: The Tivoli: Sydney's home of vaudeville
properties.summary: From 1893, Harry Rickards's Tivoli on Castlereagh Street was the flagship of Australian variety, with its comics, chorus girls and a circuit that reached across the country.
body: On 18 February 1893 the London-born entrepreneur Harry Rickards leased the Garrick Theatre on Castlereagh Street, renamed it the Tivoli, and turned it into the flagship of a national vaudeville circuit. For the next sixteen years he reigned as the leading variety man in Sydney, serving up topical songs, sketches, sight acts and a chorus line of young women in glittering costume.
Fire destroyed the theatre in September 1899. Rickards's people rebuilt it at remarkable speed and reopened in April 1900, about seven months later.

The original Tivoli closed on 28 September 1929, but the name did not die. In 1932 it was taken up by the former Adelphi at 329 Castlereagh Street near Haymarket, reborn as the New Tivoli, which carried the tradition on. Its high point came in 1948 when Laurence Olivier and Vivien Leigh brought the Old Vic to its stage. Television then thinned the crowds, the last revue played in 1966, and the building was pulled down in 1969.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Variety Theatre Archive
properties.image_caption: The Tivoli Theatre, Castlereagh Street

articles.heading: NEW VAUDEVILLE ACTS SYDNEY TIVOLI THEATRE.

properties.title: The chimneys of Sydney Park
properties.summary: The St Peters brick pits helped build Victorian Sydney, then swallowed the city's rubbish, and finally became one of its biggest parks.
body: Josiah Gentle established the Bedford Brick Works at St Peters in 1893, naming it for the English county his family had come from. He had bought the land in 1887 and kept buying neighbouring blocks for years afterward. Clay dug from deep pits and fired in great kilns supplied the bricks for huge swathes of Victorian and Federation Sydney.

Gentle outlasted his rivals by investing in steam machinery and modern kilns. After his death in 1912 his sons carried on, and Austral Bricks took the works over in the 1930s, running them until around 1970. Then the worn-out pits did something fitting. From 1948 they became one of the city's main rubbish tips, swallowing Sydney's household waste until 1976. The ground that had built the city now consumed its leftovers.

Capped with soil and rubble and then landscaped, the site reopened as Sydney Park, one of the largest parks in the city. Its wetlands and grassy hills sit over the buried pits. Four brick chimneys still stand at the corner as the last of the old industrial skyline.
properties.source: City of Sydney heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The surviving kiln and chimneys of the Sydney Park brickworks, St Peters (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

properties.title: Spectacle Island: the powder magazine
properties.summary: A small island in the Parramatta River that has stored the navy's gunpowder since 1865, and now keeps Australia's oldest naval heritage collection.
body: Spectacle Island sits in the Parramatta River, upstream of the Harbour Bridge off Drummoyne. It takes its name from its old shape, two rocky islets joined by a narrow sandy neck that together looked like a pair of spectacles. The colonial government chose it in 1865 as a place to store gunpowder, and the first sandstone magazine was drawn up in the office of Colonial Architect James Barnet, centred on a three-bay store.

Handling explosives on the island was done with great care. Workers used spark-proof gear, and ordnance was shifted on wooden rails and trolleys fitted with non-sparking wheels rather than ordinary iron ones. An internal railway grew across the island as the depot expanded through the late nineteenth century.

The Royal Australian Navy took the depot over in 1913, the year the new fleet first steamed into Sydney Harbour. It is reckoned the navy's oldest continuously operating establishment, and since the 1980s it has held the service's heritage collection. This is no walk-in museum. It remains a working island, open only by appointment.
properties.source: Royal Australian Navy heritage; Commonwealth Heritage List
properties.image_caption: Spectacle Island, the naval powder depot

articles.heading: SPECTACLE ISLAND POWDER MAGAZINE. REPLY FROM THE ADMIRAL.

properties.title: The great flood of 1867
properties.summary: In June 1867 the Hawkesbury rose to about 19.7 metres at Windsor, the highest flood in the river's recorded history. In a single night twelve members of the Eather family drowned at Cornwallis.
body: In late June 1867 the Hawkesbury climbed to roughly 19.7 metres on the gauge at Windsor. No flood since has reached that mark, and it remains the highest the river has been in recorded history. The water spread across the flats until the valley looked like an inland sea.

The worst of it came on 21 June at Cornwallis, where two Eather brothers, Thomas and William, sheltered with their families on the roof of a relative's house beside Rickabys Creek. The house gave way before a rescue boat could reach them. The two wives, Catharine and Emma, and ten of their children were swept off and drowned. The brothers and one boy survived. Those twelve deaths were the heart of a toll of around a dozen lives.

The flood tore through Windsor, Richmond and Pitt Town and the low river-flat farms that fed colonial Sydney. Crops and stock were lost, hundreds were left destitute, and food prices climbed in the city downstream. More than 150 years on, the 1867 level is still the benchmark against which every later Hawkesbury flood is measured.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Hawkesbury historical records

articles.heading: THE FLOOD IN THE HAWKESBURY.
articles.newspaper: The Armidale Express and New England General Advertiser

properties.title: The mansions of Strathfield
properties.summary: Strathfield grew into a garden suburb of grand merchant estates after the railway and the subdivision of the Redmyre Estate from 1867. The 1880s land boom filled it with mansions before the early-1890s crash froze the building.
body: Strathfield was a suburb the railway built for the well off. The line through Homebush opened in 1855, and from 1867 the old Redmyre Estate was carved into large allotments. Merchants and professionals could now keep a multi-acre estate in the country air and still reach their city offices by train. A fashionable commuter suburb took shape around the station.

The land boom of the 1880s filled the streets with grand Victorian and Federation houses. The number of dwellings roughly doubled, from about 130 in 1885 to around 275 by 1890, before the depression of the early 1890s brought the building to a halt. Many of the great estates were later subdivided or demolished as the suburb tightened around them.

Some survivors still stand. Llandilo, built in 1878 for Sir Philip Sydney Jones, outlasted its neighbours by becoming a school and is now part of Trinity Grammar's Strathfield campus. Redmyre Road and The Boulevarde still hold clusters of the old mansions, a reminder of the suburb's first decades of money and ambition.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Strathfield Council heritage
properties.image_caption: A surviving Strathfield mansion, Llandilo Avenue

articles.heading: SYDNEY SUBURBS. No. XIX. STRATHFIELD, BURWOOD, CONCORD AND FIVE DOCK. A WESTERN QUARTETTE. THEIR ATTRACTIONS AND IDIOSENCRACIES

properties.title: Cooks River: the river they moved
properties.summary: Goolay'yari was a saltwater fishing and shellfish river for the Wangal, Bediagal and Gameygal long before colonists fouled it. By the mid 20th century it had been lined in concrete and its mouth shifted west to make way for the airport.
body: For thousands of years the Cooks River, Goolay'yari, was a saltwater fishing and shellfish river for the Wangal of the Darug language in its upper reaches and, nearer the sea, the Bediagal and Gameygal. Stone tools and shell middens around Tempe speak of a long Aboriginal presence on its banks. After the colony arrived it became one of the most altered and degraded urban rivers in Australia.

Through the 19th century sugar mills, woolwashing, tanneries and boiling-down works poured their waste into the water. Concrete lining of the banks began in the 1930s, and the Cooks River Improvement Act of 1946 set out to confine the river within cement walls. The living estuary was steadily turned into a managed channel.

Then came the runways. Between 1947 and the early 1950s, engineers moved the river's whole lower mouth about 1.6 kilometres west to reclaim land for Sydney Airport. The stretch beside the airport today is a man-made cut, not the natural watercourse. The Gumbramorra Swamp that once fed the system was drained and built over. Restoration work along the river continues today, a slow attempt to bring some life back to Goolay'yari.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Cooks River Alliance
properties.image_caption: Boating on the Cooks River at Canterbury, before the concrete

articles.heading: THE IMPROVEMENT OF COOK'S RIVER. CONSIDERATION BY THE PUBLIC WORKS COMMITTEE.

properties.title: The guns of Malabar Headland
properties.summary: Rifles have fired across Malabar Headland toward the sea since 1855, which is largely why this cliff top stayed open ground rather than housing. It took the name Anzac Rifle Range in 1970.
body: Target shooting began on Malabar Headland, above Long Bay, in 1855, the same year the new village of Long Bay was gazetted. By 1888 a dozen targets were in use on the headland. In 1910 the government set the land aside for military purposes, and from 1919 it ran as the Long Bay Rifle Range.

The Anzac name came later. In 1970 the Army renamed the range to honour rifle-club members who had served in the two world wars and in Korea. The title was inherited from an older Anzac range at Liverpool, whose clubs had transferred to Long Bay from 1967 after that range closed.

Today the headland is headquarters of the NSW Rifle Association and home of the annual Queen's Prize. It is reckoned among the oldest rifle ranges in continuous use in Australia. Long-range shooting fires out over the ocean, and that line of fire is the reason the cliff top stayed open space while houses filled the suburbs around it.
properties.source: NSW Rifle Association; Randwick Council heritage
properties.image_caption: The full-bore range on Malabar Headland

articles.heading: RIFLE SHOOTING. LONG BAY RANGE. DEPARTMENT'S POLICY.

properties.title: Maltese Woolloomooloo
properties.summary: There were two Maltese Sydneys after the Second World War. Town families worked the wharves and fishing boats at Woolloomooloo, while village families settled out west around Pendle Hill, which became known as Little Malta.
body: The Maltese story in Sydney runs back to 1811, when two former privates of the Royal Regiment of Malta, named Farrugia and Spiteri, arrived as convicts after being transported for desertion. For more than a century the numbers stayed small. That changed with the Assisted Passage Agreement signed between Australia and Malta in May 1948, the first such deal Australia struck with any country other than Britain. It opened the door to thousands, and arrivals peaked through the 1950s and 1960s.

The new community did not settle in one place. It split roughly along the lines people had come from. Migrants from the towns of Malta took up familiar work near the water, on the Woolloomooloo wharves, in the fishing boats and in small shops close to the city. Families from the villages of Malta and Gozo gathered out west around Pendle Hill, near Parramatta, where the concentration grew thick enough to earn the nickname Little Malta.

As the community found its feet it built institutions to hold itself together, among them the Phoenician Club, the Maltese Guild of Australia and the newspaper The Maltese Herald. Pyrmont is sometimes named as the old Maltese quarter, but the record does not bear that out. Woolloomooloo was the inner-city anchor.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Maltese community histories
properties.image_caption: The Woolloomooloo finger wharf, where Maltese migrants found work

articles.heading: Archbishop Of Malta Celebrates Mass For Migrants NOTED PRELATE HERE 4000 Maltese at Cathedral welcome

properties.title: The bridge that joined the colonies
properties.summary: Opened in 1889 on some of the deepest bridge foundations on Earth, it closed the last gap in the rail line linking Adelaide, Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane.
body: Until 1889 a traveller heading from Sydney towards Newcastle had to step off the train and cross the Hawkesbury by ferry. The first Hawkesbury River railway bridge, opened on 1 May 1889, closed that gap. For the first time it was possible to ride the rails between Adelaide, Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane, though breaks of gauge between the colonies still meant changing trains along the way. Henry Parkes seized on the new link as a symbol in his campaign for federation.

The contract went to the Union Bridge Company of New York, which built the bridge between 1886 and 1889. Its seven iron spans were assembled on nearby Dangar Island and floated out on barges to rest on piers sunk as deep as 49 metres below high water, the deepest bridge foundations in the world at the time. John Whitton, the engineer who built the railway line itself, was kept off the bridge design after a colonial inquiry into railway bridges.

The triumph did not last. A serious crack found in a pier in 1938 had trains crawling across at walking pace, just 6 km/h, with only one train allowed on the bridge at a time. War delayed the fix, and a new bridge finally opened just upstream in 1946. The old sandstone-capped piers still stand in the river beside it.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW railway heritage
properties.image_caption: The opening ceremony, 1 May 1889

articles.heading: The Hawkesbury Railway Bridge. THE OPENING CEREMONY.
articles.newspaper: The Maitland Mercury and Hunter River General Advertiser

properties.title: Shark Island: the picnic island
properties.summary: Boambilly sits off Rose Bay and takes its name from its shape rather than its waters. Once a quarantine pen, it is now the harbour's favourite picnic island.
body: Shark Island, known to the Eora as Boambilly, lies about a kilometre off Rose Bay. The name comes from the long, low outline of the island, which a generous eye can read as a shark, and not from anything in the water around it. The island is small, roughly 250 metres by 100 metres, rising barely ten metres above the harbour.

Its early uses were about keeping danger at bay. In the 1830s it was pressed into service as a quarantine ground when a cholera outbreak in Europe frightened the colony. From 1871 to 1900 it held imported cattle and dogs, penned in isolation until they could be proven free of disease. The government restored it as a public reserve in 1900, and in 1905 it reopened to picnickers, who found shady trees, hand built sandstone grottoes and little beaches laid out in the manner of an Edwardian English park.

On Boxing Day 1945 the island had a brush with sporting history. Nine yachts crossed a start line off its shore for the first Sydney to Hobart, the race that has run every year since. Today Boambilly is part of Sydney Harbour National Park, declared in 1975, and you can reach it only by ferry or charter boat, as with its fellow picnic islands Clark and Rodd.
properties.source: NSW National Parks; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Shark Island off Rose Bay

articles.heading: Shark Island. A NEGLECTED BEAUTY SPOT. WHY PICNICKERS MUST NOT USE IT.

properties.title: The Bogle-Chandler mystery
properties.summary: On New Year's Day 1963, two bodies were found on the bank of the Lane Cove River, and the coroner could not say what had killed them. The mystery held Sydney for half a century.
body: On the morning of 1 January 1963, boys hunting for golf balls along the Lane Cove River near Fullers Bridge came across the bodies of CSIRO physicist Dr Gilbert Bogle and Margaret Chandler. The pair were partly undressed and oddly covered with pieces of cardboard and carpet. They had left a New Year's Eve party together the night before.

The inquest held in May 1963 recorded death by acute circulatory failure, but it could not name a cause. No poison was found in either body. That blank space, and the strangeness of the scene, fed decades of theories. Some blamed a Cold War assassination, others an exotic toxin or a lover's revenge, and the case stayed officially unsolved.

In 2006 the documentary maker Peter Butt argued that the answer had been there all along. He pointed to hydrogen sulphide gas rising from the polluted, weir-stilled riverbed, where a flour mill had pumped waste since the 1890s. The heavy gas could pool in the riverside hollow where Bogle and Chandler lay, then break down in the body too fast for the equipment of 1963 to detect.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Peter Butt, Who Killed Dr Bogle and Mrs Chandler? (2006)
properties.image_caption: Fuller's Bridge on the Lane Cove River, near where the bodies were found

properties.title: The Day of Mourning, 1938
properties.summary: On 26 January 1938, while the city marked 150 years since the First Fleet, Aboriginal activists met in an Elizabeth Street hall to mourn and to demand citizenship and equality.
body: On 26 January 1938, as Sydney celebrated 150 years since the First Fleet, about a hundred Aboriginal people gathered at the Australian Hall on Elizabeth Street for the Day of Mourning. It is remembered as the first national Aboriginal civil rights protest in the country.

The meeting was organised by the Sydney-based Aborigines Progressive Association of Jack Patten and William Ferguson, with the support of William Cooper's Melbourne-based Australian Aborigines' League. They had been refused the Town Hall. As a condition of using the Australian Hall, the delegates first had to watch the sesquicentenary parade from the Town Hall steps and then walk behind it to their own gathering.

Those present passed a resolution demanding full citizen status and equality, and they later took their case in a deputation to Prime Minister Joseph Lyons. The hall, which later became the Cyprus-Hellene Club and is now in Aboriginal ownership, sits on the National Heritage List as a birthplace of the modern Aboriginal political movement.
properties.source: AIATSIS; National Heritage List; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Jack Patten reads the resolution, Day of Mourning, 26 January 1938

articles.heading: Day of Mourning and Protest ABORIGINES’ CONFERENCE
ABORIGINES Claim Citizen Rights DAY OF MOURNING AND PROTEST

properties.title: The Redfern Address, 1992
properties.summary: On 10 December 1992, in a Redfern park rather than a parliament, a Prime Minister said it plainly for the first time. "We took the traditional lands... we committed the murders."
body: On 10 December 1992, Human Rights Day, Paul Keating stood among the people of Redfern in Redfern Park to launch the International Year of the World's Indigenous People. He spoke not from a podium or the floor of Parliament but out in the open, in the heart of an Aboriginal community.

The speech, drafted with his adviser Don Watson, was the first time an Australian Prime Minister had publicly named the truth of dispossession. "We took the traditional lands and smashed the traditional way of life. We brought the diseases. The alcohol. We committed the murders." Keating opened by asking non-Aboriginal Australians to imagine that all of it had been done to them.

Its reputation has only grown. In a 2007 ABC Radio National listeners' poll, the Redfern Address was ranked the third greatest speech of all time, behind only Martin Luther King's "I Have a Dream" and the Sermon on the Mount. A line from it is now set into a water sculpture in the park where it was given.
properties.source: National Museum of Australia; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Paul Keating delivering the Redfern Address, 1992

properties.title: Wendy Whiteley's Secret Garden
properties.summary: After the artist Brett Whiteley died, his former wife Wendy turned a derelict patch of railway land below Lavender Bay into a beloved public garden, and did it without permission.
body: When the artist Brett Whiteley died in 1992, his former wife Wendy began clearing the overgrown, rubbish-strewn parcel of railway land directly below their Lavender Bay house. The ground had been an unofficial tip for years. She planted it at her own expense and kept at it for more than two decades, without any formal permission to be there.

What she made became one of Sydney's most loved gardens. The ashes of Brett, and later of Wendy's daughter Arkie, who died of adrenal cancer in 2001, are scattered among the Bangalow palms that Arkie had given her. The result is a private memorial sitting openly in a public place.

Only in 2015 did the state grant a 30-year lease to North Sydney Council, securing the garden that Wendy had simply willed into being. By then it had drawn books, a television story and a steady stream of visitors who climb down the slope to find it.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; North Sydney Council
properties.image_caption: Wendy Whiteley's Secret Garden, Lavender Bay

properties.title: Muslim Lakemba
properties.summary: Haldon Street and the Lakemba Mosque became the heart of Lebanese Muslim Sydney from the 1970s, home to Australia's largest mosque and one of its largest Eid gatherings.
body: From the 1970s, Lakemba grew into the centre of Lebanese-Australian and Muslim Sydney, with Haldon Street as its commercial and cultural spine. The Imam Ali bin Abi Talib Mosque on Wangee Road began as a modest house the Lebanese Muslim Association bought in the 1960s. Completed in 1977 after five years of building, it was Sydney's first purpose-built mosque, paid for partly by local fundraising and partly by donors in the Middle East.

Today it is Australia's largest mosque. Its Eid prayers draw some of the biggest crowds in the country, with tens of thousands of worshippers filling the building and spilling out across the surrounding streets. Through Ramadan, Haldon Street turns into the Ramadan Nights food market, open late into the night and packed with visitors from across the city, Muslim and non-Muslim alike.
Lakemba sits alongside Turkish Auburn and its Gallipoli Mosque as one of two distinct Muslim Sydneys, each shaped by a different community and a different story of arrival.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Lebanese Muslim Association
properties.image_caption: Eid prayers at the Lakemba Mosque

properties.title: The Falleni case
properties.summary: For more than twenty years Eugenia Falleni lived in Sydney as Harry Crawford, until a 1920 murder trial turned a life lived across gender into a national spectacle.
body: Born in Italy in 1875 and raised in New Zealand, Falleni came to Sydney in 1898 and lived for over two decades as a man named Harry Crawford. Crawford worked as a labourer and married Annie Birkett in 1913. When Birkett vanished in 1917 and her charred remains were later found in bushland near Lane Cove, suspicion eventually settled on Crawford.

Arrested in 1920 and tried at Darlinghurst, Falleni was convicted of murder and sentenced to death. The premier and cabinet commuted the sentence to life, and Falleni was sent to the Long Bay reformatory.
