Released in 1931 on condition of living as a woman, Falleni took the name Jean Ford and ran a Paddington boarding house before dying in 1938 after being struck by a car in Oxford Street.

The newspapers of the day made a cruel show of the case, fixated on questions of gender. Modern historians read Falleni as a transgender man, and the trial as a difficult landmark in Australia's gender history.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Eugenia Falleni, who lived as Harry Crawford

properties.title: Clark Island: the raided garden
properties.summary: On the small harbour island the Gadigal knew as Billong-olola, a First Fleet officer tried to grow vegetables, and a half-starved colony kept stealing them.
body: The little island off Darling Point carries the recorded Aboriginal name Billong-olola. The colonists named it for Lieutenant Ralph Clark of the First Fleet, who in November 1789 was given permission to plant a vegetable garden there. He tried potatoes, onions and corn.

The hungry settlement would not leave the crop alone. Convicts, marines and Aboriginal people alike helped themselves to the produce, and Clark soon gave up the venture. The island remains less than a hectare of sandstone and trees, a reminder of one officer's failed bid for self-sufficiency in the lean first years of the colony.
Today it forms part of Sydney Harbour National Park. There is no ferry, so it is reached only by private boat or charter, which makes it the most secluded of the harbour's picnic islands.
properties.source: NSW National Parks; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: A day's picnic on Clark Island, 1870 (Montagu Scott)

properties.title: Westmead: from west meadow to hospital city
properties.summary: The colonial 'west mead' beyond the Governor's meadow at Rose Hill grew into one of Australia's largest hospital and research precincts.
body: Westmead takes its name from the colonial 'west mead', the acres lying west of the Governor's meadow at Rose Hill, today's Parramatta. For a century and a half it stayed farms and estates, and from the 1930s part of it served as the Parramatta Showground.

Medicine then remade the district. Westmead Hospital opened in November 1978 as a major teaching hospital, launched by Premier Neville Wran with Gough Whitlam as guest of honour. In 1995 the children's hospital moved out from Camperdown to sit beside it as The Children's Hospital at Westmead.

Within a single generation a stretch of paddocks became a hospital city. Adult and paediatric tertiary care, research institutes and universities now cluster on one precinct, anchoring health care for Sydney's fast-growing west.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Western Sydney Local Health District
properties.image_caption: Westmead Hospital

properties.title: The birth of rugby league, 1907
properties.summary: A class revolt over injured players' wages split football in Sydney, and a Test cricketer helped lend the breakaway code respectability.
body: Rugby union's refusal to compensate working men for wages lost to injury drove the split. On 8 August 1907, at Bateman's Hotel in George Street, the breakaway New South Wales Rugby Football League was founded, spurred on by the touring professional New Zealand side known as the All Golds.

The businessman James Giltinan, Labor man Harry Hoyle and the great Test batsman Victor Trumper were at the heart of it, and Trumper's name lent the rebel code instant respectability. The masterstroke was luring union superstar Dally Messenger across. His recruitment, said to have been won over with the help of his mother Annie, became part of league legend.

The first premiership kicked off on 20 April 1908 with nine teams. The four opening matches were split between Birchgrove Oval and Wentworth Park, drawing about three thousand people to each ground. A working-class game was born, and it would go on to remake winter in Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Rugby League history
properties.image_caption: Dally Messenger, the code's first star

articles.heading: RUGBY LEAGUE RULES.
articles.newspaper: Newcastle Morning Herald and Miners' Advocate

articles.heading: RUGBY FOOTBALL LEAGUE V. UNION THE LEAGUE'S PROGRESS TO DATE CAN AMATEUR RUNNERS PLAY UNDER ITS RULES?

properties.title: The Ghost Train fire, 1979
properties.summary: Seven people died when the Ghost Train burned at Luna Park in 1979, among them a visiting father and his two young sons, with the cause still officially unresolved.
body: On the night of 9 June 1979, fire tore through the Ghost Train ride at Luna Park, Milsons Point. Seven people died, six of them children. John Godson, in Sydney on holiday from Warren in the state's west, was killed with his two young sons, Damien and Craig. The other four were Waverley College boys, aged about twelve and thirteen, Jonathan Billings, Richard Carroll, Michael Johnson and Seamus Rahilly.

The ride was built of timber and canvas, and it burned so fast that getting out was almost impossible. The 1979 inquest could not fix the cause. The coroner named a discarded cigarette or match in flammable litter as the most probable source. The ride was destroyed and the park closed for years.

Suspicions of arson have never faded. Some link the fire to organised-crime interest in the harbourside land, and the name of Abe Saffron has been raised again and again, though nothing has been proven. The artist Martin Sharp spent three decades gathering material and pressing for the truth until his death in 2013.

In 2021 the ABC series 'Exposed: The Ghost Train Fire' alleged the blaze was lit deliberately and that police had covered for Saffron. A fresh police review, Strike Force Sedgeman, reported in September 2024 without settling the cause. No second inquest has been ordered, so the original open finding still stands.
properties.source: NSW State Coroner; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: The Ghost Train fire memorial at Luna Park

properties.title: The Appin Massacre, 1816
properties.summary: On Governor Macquarie's orders in April 1816, soldiers attacked a sleeping Dharawal and Gandangara camp near the Cataract gorge at Appin, one of the gravest massacres of the Sydney frontier wars.
body: In the early hours of 17 April 1816, during Governor Lachlan Macquarie's punitive campaign against Aboriginal people of the Sydney basin, a detachment of the 46th Regiment under Captain James Wallis rode up to a camp near the Cataract River gorge at Broughton Pass, close to Appin. Macquarie had ordered the troops to inflict severe punishment and to strike the people with terror. He had also spoken of sparing women and children and taking prisoners, but on the night that instruction counted for nothing.

Fourteen Dharawal and Gandangara people are the figure usually given, though the real toll was almost certainly higher. Some were shot in the camp, others fell or were driven over the cliffs into the gorge below as they fled in the dark. Most of the dead were old men, women and children. Among them were the leaders Cannabaygal, a Gandangara warrior from the Burragorang valley, and Durelle, a Dharawal speaker. Their bodies were hung from trees on a hill near Appin so the survivors would carry the fear with them, and the soldiers cut off their heads and brought them back to Sydney.

The killing ground is now recognised as the Appin Massacre Cultural Landscape on the NSW State Heritage Register. Each year around 17 April, hundreds gather near Cataract Dam for a walk of remembrance, an act of mourning and a step toward reconciliation on Dharawal Country.
properties.source: AIATSIS; NSW State Heritage Register; Winga Myamly Reconciliation Group

properties.title: The Sydney Fish Market
properties.summary: On Blackwattle Bay, the largest seafood market in the Southern Hemisphere, where buyers bid against a clock that ticks downward.
body: The Sydney Fish Market settled on the Blackwattle Bay foreshore at Pyrmont in 1966, under the new state Fish Marketing Authority. It grew into the largest seafood market in the Southern Hemisphere and reckons itself second only to Tokyo's for sheer variety. On a typical day around 55,000 kilograms of seafood and more than a hundred species change hands.

The auction is the thing worth getting up for. Since October 1989 the market has run a Dutch auction, where the price starts high and a falling electronic clock ticks downward until a buyer accepts. It is the reverse of the usual rising bid, and the early start rewards anyone who wants to watch the floor at work.

After sixty years in the old Pyrmont shed, the market crossed to the Glebe side of the bay. The vast new building at 1 Bridge Road, with its wave-shaped solar roof, opened to the public on 19 January 2026.
properties.source: Sydney Fish Market; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: The Chinese market gardens
properties.summary: Chinese gardeners, many of them former goldfields men, fed much of Sydney from the sandy soils of Botany and La Perouse. Some of those gardens are still worked more than a century on.
body: From the middle of the 19th century, Chinese market gardeners grew a large share of Sydney's vegetables. They worked the deep sandy soils around Botany, La Perouse, Rockdale and Alexandria, ground that drained and turned much like the flood plains of the Pearl River delta in their home province of Canton. Many had come out for the goldfields. When the rushes faded and the discrimination of the White Australia years closed most doors, market gardening was one of the few trades left open to them.

Through the lean decades, and especially the Depression, these gardens kept fresh vegetables on tables across urban Sydney. The work was hard and the leases were never secure, but the gardeners stayed, and the patches passed from one family to the next.

The Chinese Market Gardens at La Perouse, on Bunnerong Road, went onto the NSW State Heritage Register on 13 August 1999. The listing recognises Chinese cultivation here documented from at least 1909, when the first formal sub-leases to Chinese tenants appear, with some accounts tracing gardening on the site back further still. Families such as the Teng and the Ha kept growing bok choy, shallots, parsley and coriander on the same ground into recent years.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Chinese market gardeners loading produce at Windsor, 1938 (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: Camperdown Cemetery
properties.summary: A Gothic graveyard in Newtown that holds about 18,000 colonial burials, including many victims of the Dunbar wreck, with a public park laid over most of the graves.
body: Camperdown Cemetery opened in 1848 beside the church that would become Edmund Blacket's St Stephen's. For around twenty years it served as one of Sydney's main burial grounds before Rookwood took over, and about 18,000 people were laid to rest here.

On the night of 20 August 1857 the clipper Dunbar struck the cliffs near the South Head signal station. Of roughly 121 people aboard, only one survived, and the wreck plunged the city into mourning. Twenty-four of the dead lie in the mass grave here beneath a memorial. The same grave later received the victims of the Catherine Adamson, wrecked below North Head nine weeks afterwards with the loss of 21 lives.

In the 1940s most of the cemetery was turned into the public Camperdown Memorial Rest Park. The headstones were cleared but the dead were left beneath the lawn. The consecrated Gothic graveyard around the church survives as one of the oldest in Sydney, a quiet green pocket of colonial memory in inner Newtown.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; St Stephen's Newtown heritage
properties.image_caption: Camperdown Cemetery, Newtown

properties.title: Jewish Sydney in the east
properties.summary: Refugees from Nazism and survivors of the Holocaust made Bondi and the eastern suburbs the heart of Jewish Sydney.
body: Bondi, Bondi Junction, Dover Heights and Rose Bay became the heart of Jewish Sydney, a community enlarged by refugees fleeing Nazism in the 1930s and by survivors of the Holocaust who came after the war. About 4,500 German and Austrian Jews reached Australia in the late 1930s, and many more followed once the camps were liberated. Australia took in roughly 30,000 survivors in all, the largest number per capita of any country outside Israel.

Many came to a then-cheap, working-class Bondi. Its cafes, kosher delis and clubs soon carried the sound of Yiddish and Central European accents, and the beach itself became a gathering place for people rebuilding their lives. The Hakoah Club, founded in 1939 and rebuilt on Hall Street in 1975, was the social headquarters of the community for decades, hosting bar mitzvahs, weddings and bridge nights until it left the site in 2009.

In 1992 the survivors made their memory permanent. Led by John Saunders and members of the Australian Association of Jewish Holocaust Survivors, they opened the Sydney Jewish Museum in Darlinghurst, dedicated on 18 November that year. It turned private grief and personal testimony into a public record of the Shoah, told in the survivors' own voices.
properties.source: Sydney Jewish Museum; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Sydney Jewish Museum, Darlinghurst

properties.title: Little India at Harris Park
properties.summary: Three streets beside Parramatta became the heart of Indian Sydney, officially named Little India by the council in 2021.
body: Harris Park sits just east of Parramatta, and three of its streets have become the centre of Indian life in Sydney. Wigram Street, Marion Street and Station Street East were branded Little India by the City of Parramatta in June 2021, after a council survey found eighty per cent of local businesses and residents backed the name. The precinct had been growing since the 2000s, carried along by skilled migration from India.

More than twenty Indian eateries and shops line the footpaths, selling saris, bangles, spices and sweets. The streets fill with crowds and light for Diwali and for Indian Independence Day. In May 2024 the council signed a $3.5 million federal grant to add a welcome gate, murals and decorative lighting, with the work scheduled to be built across 2026.

There is a long view here too. This busy migrant precinct sits right on top of one of colonial Sydney's oldest patches of ground. Experiment Farm and Hambledon Cottage are a short walk away, so two very different chapters of Parramatta share the same few streets.
properties.source: City of Parramatta; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: The Games that changed the Paralympics
properties.summary: The 2000 Sydney Paralympics packed the Homebush stadiums with record crowds and became the moment the Paralympic movement turned a corner. Wheelchair racer Louise Sauvage was the home hero who lit the cauldron.
body: From 18 to 29 October 2000, a fortnight after the Olympic flame went out, the Paralympics took over the same Homebush venues. A record 3,801 athletes from 123 nations competed across 18 sports, in front of crowds the movement had never seen before.

These were the first Games to pass a million tickets, with about 1.16 million spectators turning up, almost 400,000 more than organisers had predicted. A cheap fifteen dollar day pass helped fill seats even at the smaller sports, and athletes who had often performed before half-empty stands suddenly found themselves cheered by full houses.

Louise Sauvage lit the cauldron and went on to win on home soil. The day pass idea was copied by Athens and Beijing, and Sydney 2000 is now widely regarded as the turning point in the rise of the Paralympics.
properties.source: International Paralympic Committee; Australian Paralympic Committee
properties.image_caption: Louise Sauvage at the Sydney 2000 Paralympics

properties.title: The railway that took a lifetime
properties.summary: Planned for more than half a century and abandoned twice before it opened in 1979, the Eastern Suburbs Railway still leaves you a bus ride short of Bondi Beach.
body: The Eastern Suburbs Railway became Sydney's byword for the broken political promise. A Woollahra station was mooted as far back as 1915, and the engineer J.J.C. Bradfield drew the line into his great harbour and rail scheme, setting its alignment in 1926. Then the Depression and the war buried the idea. Construction got going under the Department of Railways but was abandoned in the early 1950s with little to show for it.

A third and final attempt from 1967 at last delivered the line. Premier Neville Wran opened it on 23 June 1979, around fifty years after it was first planned, with deep tunnels diving beneath Kings Cross and Edgecliff to a terminus at Bondi Junction. The opening drew a crowd. In the first day and a half, about 250,000 people rode the new line for the novelty of it.

After sixty years of promises, though, the train stopped at the Junction. The long-touted extension to Bondi Beach was never built, blocked over the years by cost, by local objection and by shifting governments. To this day you finish the journey by bus, the last reminder of a railway that took a lifetime to arrive and still fell short.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW rail heritage
properties.image_caption: Bondi Junction, the line's terminus

properties.title: Rodd Island: Pasteur's rabbit lab
properties.summary: In 1888 a team sent by Louis Pasteur set up on a tiny island in Iron Cove to test a cure for the rabbit plague, infecting the pests with chicken cholera.
body: When rabbits overran the colony, Premier Henry Parkes offered a prize of 25,000 pounds in 1887 for anyone who could be rid of them. The reward caught the attention of Louis Pasteur in Paris, who had shown that the bacterium behind chicken cholera could kill rabbits. In 1888 he sent a team led by his nephew Adrien Loir, and a laboratory was built on Rodd Island, a speck of land at the edge of Iron Cove.

The method worked on rabbits, but the commission judged it too dangerous to native birds and other animals, so the prize went unclaimed. The lasting answer, the myxomatosis virus, was still decades away. The little laboratory found other work all the same, turning out vaccine that inoculated huge numbers of livestock against anthrax through the 1890s.

Rodd Island had one more brush with fame. In 1891 Loir offered it as a quarantine for the dogs of the French actress Sarah Bernhardt, then touring Australia, sparing them a forced return home. The island later became a much loved spot for picnics and film shoots, and it is now part of Sydney Harbour National Park.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW National Parks
properties.image_caption: Rodd Island in Iron Cove

articles.heading: The Rabbit Pest. EXPERIMENTS AT RODD ISLAND.

properties.title: The Hungry Mile
properties.summary: The Hungry Mile was the run of Darling Harbour wharves where Depression wharfies tramped from gate to gate begging a day's work under the brutal bull system.
body: The Hungry Mile was the name waterside workers gave to the line of wharves reaching north from Darling Harbour, walked each morning by men hoping to be picked for a day's pay. Through the Depression the strip earned its name in earnest, as far more men turned up than there was work to share.

The picking was done under the casual bull system. A foreman chose the fittest looking men, the bulls, for the work that was going, then in some yards flung the remaining tickets into the air for the rest to scramble after in the dirt. It was demeaning by design, and it kept the workforce desperate and divided.

The wharfie poet Ernest Antony caught the bitterness of it in his 1930 poem The Hungry Mile, which became an anthem of the Waterside Workers' Federation. The bull system was finally scrapped in 1943, replaced by organised gangs and steadier conditions. Today the towers of Barangaroo rise over the old ground, and a stretch of the foreshore carries the name in memory.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Maritime Union of Australia
properties.image_caption: The Darling Harbour wharves, the Hungry Mile

properties.title: Koreatown at Eastwood
properties.summary: Eastwood grew into the heart of Korean Sydney over half a century, a settlement made official when Rowe Street East was named Koreatown in 2023.
body: Eastwood, in Sydney's north-west, is the centre of Korean Sydney. Korean supermarkets, barbecue restaurants, grocers and churches cluster around Rowe Street near the railway line, and the suburb has long been the city's unofficial Koreatown.

The community first became visible in the early 1970s, with an initial wave of arrivals between 1972 and 1975. Skilled and business migrants swelled the numbers through the 1980s, and growth continued strongly into the 1990s. Churches became central to community life, and by the late 2000s Sydney counted more than 150 Korean Protestant congregations. Strathfield, Campsie and later Lidcombe grew into further Korean precincts across the city.

In July 2023 the City of Ryde and the local Korean chamber formally launched Rowe Street East as Koreatown, marked with banners, street decals and place signs. After fifty years of quiet settlement, the name on the street finally matched the life already there.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Ryde
properties.image_caption: Eastwood, the heart of Korean Sydney

properties.title: The Hilton bombing, 1978
properties.summary: Often called Australia's first major terrorist bombing. A bomb hidden in a street bin outside the Hilton during a Commonwealth meeting killed two garbage collectors and a policeman in 1978.
body: In the early hours of 13 February 1978, a bomb hidden in a rubbish bin outside the Hilton Hotel on George Street exploded as the bin was tipped into a garbage truck. The hotel was hosting the first Commonwealth Heads of Government Regional Meeting, and the blast went off around twenty to one in the morning. It missed the visiting leaders and struck the city's workers instead. Garbage collectors Alec Carter and William Favell were killed at once, and police constable Paul Burmistriw died of his injuries nine days later.

The attack is usually remembered as Australia's first major terrorist bombing, and the response reshaped the country. Prime Minister Malcolm Fraser sent the army onto the streets for the rest of the meeting, the first peacetime call-out of troops to aid the civil power, and the shock of it helped build modern Australian counter-terrorism.

Suspicion settled on the Ananda Marga sect. Three of its members were jailed on the word of a police informant whose evidence was later discredited. All three were pardoned and freed in 1985, and one was acquitted on appeal in 1991. No one was ever convicted of the bombing itself, which stays officially unsolved and still draws conspiracy theories to this day.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; NSW Police history
properties.image_caption: The Hilton bombing memorial

properties.title: Pasifika western Sydney
properties.summary: From the 1970s, Mount Druitt, Blacktown and the south-west became the heart of Pacific Sydney, home to Samoan, Tongan, Cook Islander and Fijian families and anchored by church and rugby league.
body: From the 1970s, Pacific Islander families began settling across western and south-western Sydney, around Mount Druitt, Blacktown, Campbelltown and Liverpool. Many moved into the new public-housing estates going up on the edge of the city. Samoan, Tongan, Cook Islander and Fijian communities took root, along with Maori families who had often arrived by way of New Zealand. The settlement grew quickly through the 1980s, and Sydney now holds the largest Samoan community in Australia.

Church became the steady centre of community life. Samoan, Tongan and Cook Islander congregations gathered every Sunday, carrying language, food and the customs of faa Samoa into the suburbs. Rugby league grew into a defining force as well, and Pasifika families went on to reshape the modern game.

The island marketplace came too. Shops near Mount Druitt sell taro, green bananas and woven mats, and local canteens serve dishes like sapasui and panikeke. Over half a century, these suburbs have become one of the largest Pacific communities outside the islands themselves.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Blacktown City heritage

properties.title: The Sentimental Bloke
properties.summary: Raymond Longford's The Sentimental Bloke, released in 1919, is the finest of the Australian silent films, a larrikin love story shaped in large part by his partner and collaborator Lottie Lyell.
body: Raymond Longford shot The Sentimental Bloke in 1918 and the film opened in October 1919. It drew its story from C.J. Dennis's hugely popular verse and moved the action to the back lanes of working-class Woolloomooloo. Longford cast faces that looked the part, including Arthur Tauchert, a former labourer and vaudeville performer, as the Bloke. The result felt true to the city in a way that imported pictures never managed.

Much of the film's heart came from Lottie Lyell. She played the sweetheart Doreen, but she also helped write, edit and shape the picture, and she was among the first Australian women to work as a screenwriter and behind the camera. Filming ranged across Woolloomooloo, the open-air sets at Wonderland City at Bondi, and an orchard out in the Hornsby valley.

The Bloke was a great popular success and a high point for a busy local film industry that imported capital and distribution would soon squeeze. Lyell's health was already failing as she moved into production work. She died of tuberculosis on 21 December 1925, aged just 35.
properties.source: National Film and Sound Archive; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Sentimental Bloke, 1918

properties.title: Tranby: the co-operative college
properties.summary: Since 1957 a 19th-century cottage in Glebe has held Tranby, the oldest independent Aboriginal-controlled adult college in Australia and a hub of the rights movement.
body: Tranby began in 1957 in an old cottage on Mansfield Street in Glebe, on Gadigal land. It is the oldest independent, not-for-profit Aboriginal-controlled adult education provider in the country. The Anglican cleric Alf Clint set it up through a Christian co-operative for Aboriginal people, convinced that the co-operative model fitted Aboriginal community traditions. He taught co-operation as a core subject and treated economic self-determination as part of learning itself.

What started as a training centre for co-operative practice grew into something larger. From the early 1970s Tranby threw itself into the land-rights campaigns and kept close ties to the Aboriginal cultural and political life flowering nearby in Redfern. Its students and staff turned up at the demonstrations that shaped a generation.

When Clint died in 1980, Kevin Cook became the first Indigenous secretary of Tranby. Every secretary since has been Indigenous. That was a deliberate handing over, a move to put Aboriginal education firmly under Aboriginal control, and it is the reason the place still matters today.
properties.source: AIATSIS; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Tranby, Mansfield Street, Glebe

properties.title: The ghost platforms under Wynyard
properties.summary: Beneath Sydney's busiest station lie abandoned platforms where trams once rose out of the ground and ran straight across the Harbour Bridge.
body: When the Harbour Bridge opened in 1932 it carried two tram tracks on its eastern side, and those trams ran down into an underground terminus at Wynyard. Platforms 1 and 2 became the only underground tram terminus in Australia. For 26 years the North Shore trams climbed out of the ground here and swung straight onto the bridge.

The eastern tracks had really been laid for a railway to the Northern Beaches that was never built, so the trams borrowed the alignment in the meantime. When the North Shore tram services stopped in 1958, the tracks were lifted, the platforms were walled off from the railway alongside, and part of the space became a car park.

The disused tunnel and the old tram ramp curving off the bridge's eastern approach still survive, heritage-listed remnants of a tramway that once spread across the whole city. They sit as a mirror image of the rail tracks still running on the western side of the bridge today.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW heritage
properties.image_caption: The abandoned tram tunnels under Wynyard

properties.title: Rosehill and the Golden Slipper
properties.summary: Carved out beside the Parramatta River in the 1880s, Rosehill Gardens gave Sydney the Golden Slipper, which grew into the richest race for two-year-olds in the world.
body: The landowner John Bennett laid out Rosehill Gardens beside the Parramatta River and held the first race meeting there on 18 April 1885. It became Sydney's great western racecourse, the counterweight to Randwick in the east. For seventy years it ran on as a solid fixture without a signature race to call its own.

That changed in 1957 with the Golden Slipper Stakes, a short sprint for untried two-year-olds run over six furlongs, a touch over 1,200 metres. The distance went fully metric in 1973. What began as a local feature swelled into the richest race for two-year-old horses anywhere on earth.

In 1986 it became the first race in New South Wales worth a million dollars, and by the 2020s the prize money had pushed past five million. Each autumn it turns a couple of frantic minutes of the youngest and most expensively bred horses in the country into one of the most lucrative sprints in world racing.
properties.source: Australian Turf Club; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Rosehill Gardens Racecourse

properties.title: The Harbour fireworks
properties.summary: Sydney is among the first of the world's great cities into the new year, and its Harbour Bridge fireworks are watched around the globe.
body: Sitting far to the east, Sydney is one of the first major cities to cross into the new year, and it announces the moment with fireworks fired from the very arch and deck of the Harbour Bridge, along with the Opera House, barges and city rooftops.

The modern spectacular took its current shape when the City of Sydney took over the event for New Year's Eve 1996 and the Nine Network carried it live across the country for the first time. Two shows are now standard. The 9pm display gives families and children an earlier option, and the midnight show ends with its famous waterfall of sparks cascading off the Bridge.

Around a million people pack the foreshore at Circular Quay, the Opera House forecourt and Mrs Macquaries Point, while the show is beamed worldwide. Its global audience is often put at roughly a billion viewers.
properties.source: City of Sydney; ABC
properties.image_caption: New Year's Eve fireworks on the Harbour Bridge

properties.title: The monorail that went the wrong way
properties.summary: A 3.6 kilometre loop opened for the Bicentenary in 1988, was mocked for running one direction round too small a circle, and came down in 2013.
body: The Sydney Monorail opened on 21 July 1988, timed for the Bicentenary and the new Darling Harbour precinct. It threaded between buildings at first-floor level on a 3.6-kilometre loop through Darling Harbour, Chinatown and the city, sliding past the old Pyrmont Bridge as it went. The company behind it was the transport operator TNT.

From the start it was more tourist novelty than serious transport. The single loop ran in one direction only, and it was so tight that walking was often quicker than riding the full circle back to where you had started. Many Sydneysiders never warmed to the way it loomed over the streets below.

It closed on 30 June 2013, with a state minister conceding it had always been more of a novelty than a real transport service. The track was pulled down to clear room for the rebuilt convention centre and the new light rail. Two carriages and a short length of track survive in the Powerhouse collection.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Transport for NSW
properties.image_caption: The monorail over Darling Harbour, 1990

properties.title: The Cronulla riots, 2005
properties.summary: On 11 December 2005 a mob gathered at Cronulla beach and the retaliatory attacks that followed became one of Australia's worst episodes of racial violence.
body: On Sunday 11 December 2005 a crowd of more than 5,000 gathered at Cronulla beach. It came a week after two surf lifesavers were assaulted, and it had been whipped up by SMS chain messages and parts of talkback radio. By midday the mob had turned violent, attacking people it judged to be of Middle Eastern appearance.

That night and over the nights that followed, convoys of young men of Middle Eastern background drove into Cronulla, Brighton-le-Sands and Maroubra and carried out hit-and-run reprisals. The violence ran in more than one direction. In all, about 26 people were injured and police laid 285 charges against 104 people, some from the original riot and some from the retaliation.

The headlines, including the front-page line 'our racist shame', set off a bout of national soul-searching. They led to changes in policing and to lasting efforts at community reconciliation. The events at Cronulla remain among the worst outbreaks of civil and racial unrest in the country's history.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; NSW Police; news archives
properties.image_caption: Cronulla Beach

properties.title: The beer-barn years
properties.summary: In sweaty inner-city pubs through the 1970s and 1980s, Sydney's live circuit forged Cold Chisel, INXS, Midnight Oil and the Angels. The bands learned to fill stadiums by first surviving small, beer-soaked rooms.
body: Sydney's pub-rock circuit through the 1970s and 1980s was a hard training ground. Cold Chisel, INXS, Midnight Oil, the Angels, Rose Tattoo and Radio Birdman all came up through it. They learned to project to stadiums by playing small, beer-soaked rooms three and four nights a week, week after week.

Some rooms became legends. The Bondi Lifesaver was one of the best loved, and Cold Chisel recorded a live set there in February 1980, just days before they cut the album East and only months before the venue closed in August that year. Selina's, the big purpose-built room at the Coogee Bay Hotel, stayed a reliable rock barn for decades. In 1975 Radio Birdman secured a residency at the Oxford Tavern at Taylor Square in Darlinghurst, took the room over and renamed it the Oxford Funhouse after a Stooges song. It became Sydney's answer to New York's CBGB, a crush of bikies, labourers, drinkers and art students.

The good years did not last. Poker machines, noise complaints and the steady creep of redevelopment slowly strangled the circuit. The Hopetoun in Surry Hills hung on longer than most, and when it too fell quiet it became the emblem of everything the city had lost.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Film and Sound Archive
properties.image_caption: The Coogee Bay Hotel, home of Selina's

properties.title: The Swans come north
properties.summary: In 1982 South Melbourne, a foundation club of the VFL born in 1874, became the first League side to base itself interstate, carrying Australian Rules north to a Sydney Cricket Ground crowd that lived and breathed rugby league.
body: The club that became the Sydney Swans started out as South Melbourne, one of the foundation members of the Victorian competition in 1874. By the late 1970s it was bleeding money in a crowded Melbourne market, losing at least 150,000 dollars a year. So the board gambled on an untapped city to the north. In 1982 the move was made, and South Melbourne became the first League club ever to set up home interstate, a decision that split its old supporters and drew a fierce 'Keep South at South' campaign.

The first Sydney home game came on 28 March 1982, when the Swans beat Melbourne by 29 points in front of 15,764 people at the SCG. Behind the scenes the operation was shoestring. Players trained on the No. 2 ground when it was free of cricket or duty as a car park, and the harder contest was always the same one, persuading a rugby league town to fall for a foreign game played on an oval.

The reward took a generation. After a heavy Grand Final defeat to North Melbourne in 1996, the Swans finally broke through in 2005, edging West Coast by four points at the MCG. Leo Barry's last-gasp mark in a thicket of Eagles players sealed it. The win delivered the club's first flag since South Melbourne's of 1933, ending a drought of 72 years, the longest the competition had ever seen.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Swans
properties.image_caption: The Sydney Swans at the SCG

properties.title: Oxford Street and the AIDS years
properties.summary: The heart of gay Sydney became the front line of the AIDS epidemic, and the place where a community-led response first took shape.
body: Through the 1970s, 80s and into the 90s, Oxford Street in Darlinghurst was the heart of gay Sydney. From the early 1980s it was also the front line of the AIDS epidemic, and the response began on the strip itself. When Bobby Goldsmith, a much-loved figure in the community, was dying, his friends threw a fundraising party for his care at the Midnight Shift on 13 May 1984. It became Australia's first AIDS fundraiser and brought in more than $6000, far more than his care required.

Goldsmith died at his home in Surry Hills on 18 June 1984. He was the first person in NSW publicly acknowledged to have died of AIDS. Out of the effort around him grew the Bobby Goldsmith Foundation, set up in July 1984 and now Australia's longest-running HIV charity.

The organising did not stop there. A community meeting in February 1985 agreed to form the AIDS Council of NSW, known later simply as ACON, which was formally incorporated that August. Volunteers handed out safe-sex information, ran their own campaigns and cared for the sick.

Time and again the community filled the gaps that government left, even paying for frank safe-sex messages when the state pulled back. That partnership between affected communities and health authorities is now widely credited with keeping Australia's epidemic far smaller than it might have been.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; ACON; Bobby Goldsmith Foundation
properties.image_caption: Oxford Street, Darlinghurst

properties.title: Little Italy, Leichhardt
properties.summary: After the war, Norton Street in Leichhardt became the centre of Italian Sydney, lined with espresso bars and clubs and known to many as Little Italy.
body: Italians had been settling in Leichhardt since the 1920s, but the great wave of postwar migration is what turned the inner-west suburb into the capital of Italian Sydney. More than 400,000 Italians arrived in Australia between 1947 and 1971, drawn by work and by hardship at home, and many of them found their way to Leichhardt. The APIA Club opened on Norton Street in 1954, and the street filled with espresso bars, delicatessens and social clubs.

Locals took to calling the strip Little Italy, though the name was only made official in 2020, when the Geographical Names Board endorsed a proposal from Inner West Council. Cafes and gelato bars passed the Italian habits of coffee and the evening passeggiata down the generations. The Capuchin priests at St Fiacre's Church held the first Norton Street Festa in 1986, modelled on the religious festivals of Italy, and it still closes the street each spring.

In 1988 Premier Neville Wran granted a 12,000 square metre parcel of Norton Street land to the Italian community for a cultural precinct. The Italian Forum, a Mediterranean-style piazza ringed by apartments, shops and restaurants, opened in 1999 after several years of building. Leichhardt Library moved into the Forum in 2003. The piazza has had a mixed life since, with quiet patches and shop closures, but Norton Street remains the heart of Italian Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Inner West Council
properties.image_caption: The Italian Forum on Norton Street, Leichhardt

properties.title: Wedding Cake Island
properties.summary: The low rocky island about a kilometre off Coogee Beach takes the brunt of the swell and keeps the beach calm. In 1980 Midnight Oil turned its name into a surf instrumental.
body: Roughly a kilometre east of Coogee Beach sits a low, flat shelf of rock that locals call Wedding Cake Island. The likeliest reason for the name is the white water that breaks over its edges in a heavy swell, sitting on the rock like icing on a cake. Others point to the gulls and the droppings they leave behind. Either way, the island earns its keep. It takes much of the ocean's force before the water reaches the sand, which is part of why Coogee bathes so gently compared with its neighbours.

In 1980 the island found a second life in music. Midnight Oil, a band cut from the surf pubs of the eastern suburbs, named a guitar instrumental after it and put the track on their EP Bird Noises, released that November. Recorded up at Byron Bay, it is loose and salty, the sort of thing that sounds like a good day out the back. The song carried the name of a small Coogee rock to listeners who would never see it.

The island still does its quiet work for the beach. Each year swimmers in the Coogee Island Challenge, run by the local surf club, head out and round the island before turning for home, so the rock that shelters the bathers also sets the course for the brave.
properties.source: Geographical Names Board of NSW; Midnight Oil discography
properties.image_caption: Wedding Cake Island off Coogee Beach

properties.title: The Coca-Cola Sign
properties.summary: Sydney's great illuminated billboard above William Street has been the unofficial gateway to Kings Cross since 1974.
body: Since 1974 a vast Coca-Cola sign has blazed above the fork of William Street and Darlinghurst Road. Built by Claude Neon, it is the first thing drivers see as they climb the hill out of the city, and by long habit it has become the gateway to Kings Cross. At about 41 metres long and 13 metres high, it grew into one of Sydney's best known billboards and a fixed star above the nightlife district below. People often call it the biggest billboard in the southern hemisphere, though that title has always been argued over.

The original was a creature of neon, more than a thousand red and white tubes that hummed over the Cross every night. It was switched off only rarely. In 2007 the lights went dark for the inaugural Earth Hour, a quiet gesture from a sign built to never stop glowing.

The neon came down in 2015 and its letters were auctioned to raise money for the nearby Wayside Chapel. A colour-changing LED version, switched on in September 2016, took its place and used far less power. The technology changed, but the habit of looking up for it did not, and a half-century-old landmark keeps lighting the road into the Cross.
properties.source: City of Sydney; contemporary news reports
properties.image_caption: The Coca-Cola sign at Kings Cross, lit at night

properties.title: The Syrian Quarter, Redfern
properties.summary: From the 1880s, one of Australia's oldest Arabic-speaking communities took root along Elizabeth Street, Redfern.
body: From the 1880s, families from Mount Lebanon began settling in inner Sydney. Mount Lebanon was then an Ottoman district, so the newcomers were recorded as 'Syrians', and the word 'Lebanese' only became a separate official category here in the 1940s. They gathered along Elizabeth Street, Redfern, opposite Redfern Park, and spread into Surry Hills and Waterloo. By 1892 this Syrian colony already ran to about two dozen shops.

This first wave was overwhelmingly Christian, drawn from the Maronite, Melkite and Antiochian Orthodox churches. The Melkites had a church in Waterloo by 1895, and the Maronites had their own in Redfern by 1897, when St Maroun's was consecrated by Cardinal Patrick Moran. The church still stands on Elizabeth Street.

Most arrivals started out as travelling hawkers, carrying packs of drapery into the suburbs and out along country roads. As they saved, many moved up into shopkeeping and the rag trade, and a few built warehouses and small factories in Redfern itself.

This was a distinct and much earlier community from the largely Lebanese-Muslim Sydney later centred on Lakemba. Muslim Lebanese began arriving from the 1950s, and their numbers grew after war broke out in Lebanon in 1975.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; MHNSW Migration Heritage Centre

properties.title: BridgeClimb
properties.summary: Since October 1998, climbers in grey suits have walked to the top of the Sydney Harbour Bridge arch, the first commercial bridge-summit climb anywhere in the world.
body: The idea came to businessman Paul Cave in 1989, when he helped organise a climb of the Harbour Bridge for a visiting Young Presidents' Organization congress. He spent close to a decade winning the permissions and working out how to do it safely before BridgeClimb opened to its first paying guests on 1 October 1998.

Climbers go up in single file, clipped to a static line that runs the length of the route. They wear grey 'bridge suits' chosen to blend against the steel so they barely show from the streets below. The walk reaches the summit of the arch at about 134 metres above the water, with the city and harbour opening out on every side.

More than four million people from over 130 countries have since made the climb, turning Dorman Long's 1932 steelwork into one of Sydney's signature experiences. What began as a one-off favour for a business delegation became a fixture of the harbour.
properties.source: BridgeClimb Sydney; contemporary news reports
properties.image_caption: Climbers on the summit of the Sydney Harbour Bridge

properties.title: The Archibald Prize
properties.summary: Australia's most famous portrait prize, awarded at the Art Gallery of NSW since 1921 and once fought all the way to the Supreme Court.
body: When J.F. Archibald, the founding editor of The Bulletin, died in 1919, he left money for an annual portrait prize. The trustees of the Art Gallery of New South Wales awarded the first Archibald in 1921, to W.B. McInnes for his portrait of the architect Harold Desbrowe-Annear. It has been the country's most argued-over art prize ever since.

The sharpest dispute came over William Dobell's win for the 1943 prize, a portrait of his fellow artist Joshua Smith. A group of artists led by Mary Edwell-Burke and Joseph Wolinski took the trustees to the NSW Supreme Court, arguing that the gaunt, elongated image was a caricature and not a portrait at all.

In October 1944 the judge dismissed the challenge. He found that no court could set aside the decision of the trustees who had been appointed to judge the prize, so Dobell's win stood. The Archibald still fills the gallery, and the papers, every winter.
properties.source: Art Gallery of New South Wales; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Art Gallery of New South Wales, home of the Archibald Prize

properties.title: Mrs Macquarie's Chair
properties.summary: A bench cut straight from the sandstone by convicts in 1810, named for the Governor's wife, Elizabeth Macquarie, who liked the view from the tip of the Domain.
body: At the tip of the Domain peninsula, known as Yurong to the Gadigal, convicts cut a curved seat directly from the sandstone in 1810, early in Lachlan Macquarie's governorship. It is named for his wife, Elizabeth, who is said to have come here for the sweep of the harbour. The story that she sat watching for ships rounding in from Britain is folklore rather than anything the record proves.

An inscription on the rock face tells a separate story. It records the completion of Mrs Macquaries Road, measuring three miles and 377 yards, on 13 June 1816. The road ran from the first Government House out to the point, so the 1816 date belongs to the road and not to the seat carved six years earlier.
Two centuries on, the chair is one of the most photographed lookouts in Sydney. Visitors still line up where Elizabeth Macquarie once took in the same view of the water.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Mrs Macquarie's Chair on Mrs Macquaries Point

properties.title: Macedonian Rockdale
properties.summary: Postwar Macedonian migrants settled heavily around Rockdale and the wider St George district, turning it into a heartland of Macedonian Sydney.
body: Macedonians came to Sydney in postwar waves, with the largest numbers arriving through the 1960s and 1970s. They came from both Vardar Macedonia, then part of Yugoslavia, and the Aegean Macedonia of northern Greece, where the Greek Civil War had displaced many. In New South Wales they gathered around Rockdale and the wider St George district, close to industrial work, and Rockdale grew into a recognised centre of the community.

Orthodox churches anchored that community. St Petka at Rockdale was founded in 1975 by Macedonians from the village of Capari, and the Resurrection of Christ church served another congregation nearby. The two parishes reflected a wider split in church jurisdiction, but for ordinary families they were the places where weddings, christenings and saint's day feasts kept identity and language alive.

The imprint is still easy to read in the shops, social clubs and church calendars of the area. A walk past the churches on a feast day shows how firmly Macedonian Sydney took root in this corner of the city.
properties.source: Multicultural NSW; SBS Cultural Atlas
properties.image_caption: A Macedonian Orthodox church at Rockdale

properties.title: The Star Amphitheatre
properties.summary: A 1920s open-air amphitheatre above Balmoral Beach, built by Theosophists for the man they expected to be the World Teacher, and remembered for a myth that it awaited a messiah walking through the Heads.
body: Across 1923 and 1924 the Order of the Star in the East, an arm of the Theosophical Society, raised an open-air amphitheatre on the hillside above Balmoral Beach for about 10,000 pounds. Designed in a Grecian Doric style and seating around 2,500 people, it was meant for lectures and gatherings, and built chiefly with one figure in mind. The Theosophist Charles Leadbeater had proclaimed the young Jiddu Krishnamurti the coming World Teacher, and the Star was to be his hall.

A persistent Sydney legend says the building was placed so members could watch that messiah walk across the water and through Sydney Heads, with tickets sold for the spectacle. The Theosophists always denied it, and leaders of the society stated flatly that there had never been any such suggestion. The tale seems to trace to sensational newspaper writing rather than to the society itself.

Krishnamurti renounced the role and dissolved the Order in 1929. The amphitheatre was sold off in the early 1930s, later passed to the Catholic Church, and was demolished in 1951. An apartment building stands on the site today.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Mosman Council heritage
properties.image_caption: The Star Amphitheatre at Balmoral, around 1925

properties.title: Royal Prince Alfred Hospital
properties.summary: Camperdown's great teaching hospital opened in 1882, founded as a memorial to Prince Alfred after he was shot at a Clontarf picnic in 1868.
body: In March 1868 Prince Alfred, Duke of Edinburgh and a son of Queen Victoria, was shot in the back at a picnic at Clontarf by Henry James O'Farrell. He survived, and the colony's relief took a lasting and very practical form. Money poured in by public subscription, the Duke asked that it go towards a hospital, and Parliament passed an Act to incorporate the institution in 1873.

Royal Prince Alfred opened at Camperdown on 25 September 1882 with about 146 beds. The University of Sydney granted the land, so the hospital could double as a teaching hospital for its new medical school next door. That bond between hospital and university shaped everything that followed.

RPA grew into one of Australia's foremost hospitals and research centres, still tied to the university beside it. It remains a healing institution that began, improbably, with an assassination attempt on a visiting prince.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; University of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The administration building of Royal Prince Alfred Hospital

properties.title: Holsworthy Internment Camp
properties.summary: Australia's largest First World War internment camp, at Holsworthy near Liverpool, where thousands of so-called enemy aliens were held without trial.
body: From early 1915 until 1920 the army held Australia's largest body of wartime internees at Holsworthy, near Liverpool in Sydney's south-west. People knew it as the Liverpool German Concentration Camp. Several thousand men were confined at its height, and around 6,890 passed through in all, making it the biggest such camp in the country.

The inmates were a varied lot. There were German and Austro-Hungarian nationals, ships' crews caught in port, Dalmatian, Czech and Hungarian subjects of the empire, and naturalised or Australian-born men of German descent. None had been charged or tried. Unlike the internees at Berrima and Trial Bay, the Holsworthy men were treated as prisoners, and the camp earned a hard reputation in the local German community.

It was a crowded, makeshift city of huts, plagued by heat, dust, boredom and worry about families left outside. Friction over rations and forced labour sparked riots in 1915. From late 1915 an extortion gang the internees called the Black Hand terrorised the camp, until a general uprising in April 1916 turned on the gang and its ringleader was killed.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: The German internment camp at Holsworthy, near Liverpool, c.1919 (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: The Pylon Lookout
properties.summary: The Harbour Bridge's four granite-faced pylons carry no real load, yet the south-eastern one has spent most of its life as a lookout. Since 1934 it has drawn tourists with everything from a camera obscura to a rooftop colony of white cats.
body: The four pylons that frame the Harbour Bridge look like they are holding it up, but they are not. The steel arch does the work, and the engineer J.J.C. Bradfield kept the pylons largely for the sake of appearance, giving the span a sense of weight and balance at each end. They were faced in granite to dress up what is essentially structural theatre.

The south-eastern pylon found a second life almost as soon as the bridge opened. In 1934 the showman Archer Whitford turned it into a tourist attraction, complete with a cafe, a camera obscura and an Aboriginal museum. After the pylon was put to military use during the Second World War, the ex-servicewoman Yvonne Rentoul took it on. From 1948 to 1971 she ran her All Australian Exhibition of dioramas and souvenirs there.

Rentoul's real drawcard climbed onto the roof. She kept a cattery of white cats in the rooftop garden, among them a pair of twins named Bridget and Pylon and a much-photographed tom called George. Visitors came as much for the cats as for the view, and when her lease ended in 1971 she took the survivors with her and refused to return. The pylon is once again a museum and lookout today, reached by a climb of roughly 200 steps above the deck.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; MHNSW
properties.image_caption: The south-east pylon of the Sydney Harbour Bridge

properties.title: Correy's Pleasure Grounds
properties.summary: A riverside pleasure ground at Cabarita Point on the Parramatta River, where ferry-loads of picnickers came to dance and race from the 1880s.
body: At Cabarita Point on the Parramatta River, Thomas Obed Correy opened pleasure grounds in the 1880s, having earlier run gardens at Botany. They became one of Sydney's favourite picnic excursions. Correy planted out the site with flowers, shrubs and trees, and laid on swings, a merry-go-round, a cricket field, a running track and summer houses for the crowds the ferries brought in.

From 1887 a dance pavilion could seat around 900 people, entertained by a ten-piece string orchestra, and it drew the annual picnics of countless social and sporting clubs. The point also made a fine grandstand for the rowing races on the river. A tram line reached Cabarita in 1907, which only widened the trade.

Like many Victorian pleasure grounds, Correy's faded during the First World War, and the business was sold in 1918. The public reserve beside it, Cabarita Park, carries the leisure tradition on today.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Canada Bay Heritage Society

properties.title: Kelly's Bush
properties.summary: The last bushland on its reach of the Parramatta River, saved in 1971 by the world's first green ban.
body: Kelly's Bush at Hunters Hill was the last patch of original bushland on its stretch of the Parramatta River. The developer A.V. Jennings, which had bought the land in 1966, planned a luxury housing estate.

A committee of local women, the Battlers for Kelly's Bush, led by Betty James, fought the plan through every conventional channel. When that failed, they turned to the New South Wales Builders Labourers' Federation for help. The union set one condition, a strong public meeting, and more than six hundred people turned up. On 17 June 1971 the BLF, under leaders including Jack Mundey, Bob Pringle and Joe Owens, refused to work on the site. It was called a black ban at the time, and soon became known as the world's first green ban.

The development was beaten and Jennings sold the land to council. Premier Neville Wran ruled out building there in 1977, and in 1983 the state bought the land and dedicated it to the public for good. The ban launched a movement that went on to save much of old Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; greenbans.net.au
properties.image_caption: Bushland at Kelly's Bush, Hunters Hill

properties.title: Sydney Mechanics' School of Arts
properties.summary: Founded in 1833 to bring science, books and learning to working people, the Sydney Mechanics' School of Arts holds Australia's oldest continuously running lending library.
body: The Sydney Mechanics' School of Arts began in 1833 with a simple aim, to put science, lectures and books within reach of ordinary working people. It had no home at first, but by early 1837 it had moved into its own building at 275 Pitt Street, helped along by a land grant and a small subsidy from Governor Bourke's administration. It became the model for hundreds of Schools of Arts that sprang up across the colony.

Its lending library is the oldest one in Australia still running today. The evening classes mattered just as much. In 1878 the School founded the Working Men's College in a building behind the main hall, and the government soon took over that technical work to create Sydney Technical College, assuming full responsibility by 1883. That thread runs all the way to the University of Technology Sydney.

The School is still going. It moved across the road in 1996 into Lincoln House at 280 Pitt Street, a few doors from its old address, and continues as a membership library and lecture society. For nearly two centuries it has been a quiet engine of adult education in the city.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Mechanics' School of Arts
properties.image_caption: The Sydney School of Arts building, photographed in 1869

properties.title: The King's School
properties.summary: Founded under royal sanction in 1831 and opened in colonial Parramatta in 1832, The King's School is the oldest independent school in Australia.
body: The King's School was founded in 1831 with the sanction of King William IV, which is where its name comes from. It opened its doors on 13 February 1832 in rented premises on George Street, Parramatta, the building now known as Harrisford. It began with three boys, but numbers approached a hundred by the end of that first year. The school council later fixed the official foundation date at 1831, a year before the doors actually opened.

An Anglican school from the start, King's built a strong military streak early on. Drill began in the 1850s and one of the country's oldest cadet corps was formed in 1868. That tradition shaped the school's character for well over a century.

After roughly 130 years in central Parramatta, the school moved in the 1960s to its present campus of about 148 hectares on the northern edge of the city. Its origins still reach back to the first decades of the colony.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; The King's School
properties.image_caption: The entrance to The King's School, North Parramatta

properties.title: The Sydney Harbour Tunnel
properties.summary: A road tunnel under the harbour, opened in 1992 to take pressure off the Bridge. Its central section was cast on shore in giant concrete pieces, floated out and sunk into a trench on the harbour floor.
body: Sixty years after the Harbour Bridge opened, Sydney built a second cross-harbour road link, this time beneath the water. The Sydney Harbour Tunnel opened in late August 1992. It runs a little over two kilometres, just east of the Bridge and roughly parallel to it, and took a share of the traffic that had long choked the older crossing.

The clever part lies under the harbour itself. The central stretch is an immersed tube, about 960 metres made up of eight giant concrete units, each around 120 metres long. The units were cast on shore, floated out across the water and then sunk into a dredged trench on the harbour floor, where they were joined end to end and sealed.

Work began in February 1988. The tunnel was delivered under one of Australia's first big Build-Own-Operate-Transfer toll deals, by a joint venture of Transfield and the Japanese firm Kumagai Gumi, with the operating rights running for thirty years before the road reverted to the state.
properties.source: Transport for NSW; contemporary news reports

properties.title: Nielsen Park
properties.summary: A netted harbour beach in Vaucluse, wrapped around an 1850s Gothic villa and saved for the public in 1910.
body: In July 1910 the New South Wales government resumed about nine hectares of the Vaucluse foreshore and set it aside as a public reserve. The land was first called Vaucluse Park, then renamed for Niels Nielsen, the Danish-born Secretary for Lands who had pushed the resumption through. It gave ordinary Sydneysiders a piece of harbour shoreline that had until then belonged to a private estate.

At the centre of the park stands Greycliffe House, a sandstone Gothic Revival villa completed about 1851 to a design by John Frederick Hilly. After it passed into public hands the house served various purposes, including a hospital for babies and later a Tresillian mothercraft home. Today it is the headquarters of Sydney Harbour National Park.

Below the house lies Shark Beach, a calm swimming spot behind a harbour net. A larger shark-proof enclosure went in during 1931, and on a hot day the small beach is one of the most loved corners of the harbour. Note that this is a different place from the 1927 Greycliffe ferry disaster, which took its name from the same headland.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW National Parks
properties.image_caption: Shark Beach at Nielsen Park, Vaucluse

properties.title: The El Alamein Fountain
properties.summary: In Fitzroy Gardens at Kings Cross, a 1961 fountain of fine brass stalks honours the Australian 9th Division and the battles of El Alamein. Locals know it as the dandelion.
body: At the top of Macleay Street, where Fitzroy Gardens opens out, the El Alamein Memorial Fountain spreads like a giant blown dandelion. A globe of 211 fine brass stalks rises from a low pool, each stalk tipped with a mist of water that catches the light. The young architect Robert Woodward designed it, and the Lord Mayor Harry Jensen opened it on 18 November 1961.

The fountain commemorates the Australian 9th Division and the country's part in the North African campaign of the Second World War, above all the two battles fought at El Alamein in 1942. Woodward had studied fountains in Europe, and his Kings Cross design was quietly radical for its day. It made his name and went on to be copied around the world.

For decades the dandelion has been the visual signature of the Cross, glittering at the centre of the suburb's restless nights. It is one of Sydney's few war memorials that asks nothing solemn of the passer-by. People simply meet beside it, and the water keeps misting over the brass.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The El Alamein Memorial Fountain in Fitzroy Gardens, Kings Cross

properties.title: The City2Surf
properties.summary: Sydney's great mass run from the city to Bondi began in 1971 with fewer than 1,600 starters. It now draws tens of thousands down to the sea each winter.
body: On 5 September 1971, 1,576 runners set off from George Street beside the Town Hall in a new fun run dreamed up by staff at The Sun newspaper. They had borrowed the idea from San Francisco's Bay to Breakers. The Amateur Athletic Association of New South Wales and its women's counterpart helped run that first event, and 1,509 starters finished inside the time limit.

The City2Surf soon settled into the route that made it famous, around 14 kilometres out through the eastern suburbs to the finish on Bondi Beach. The long grind up New South Head Road, climbing from Rose Bay towards Vaucluse, earned the name Heartbreak Hill. It is the stretch that every runner remembers.

From that modest first field the run has grown into one of the largest of its kind on earth, with well over 80,000 entrants in big years. Elite athletes, costumed charity runners and families all pour downhill together on a winter Sunday, and the road tips them out at the sand.
properties.source: City2Surf official history; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Runners stream through the City2Surf

properties.title: The Goods Line
properties.summary: A disused freight railway through Ultimo reopened in 2015 as a green pedestrian spine, a Sydney answer to New York's High Line.
body: The corridor through Ultimo began carrying freight in 1855, hauling goods down to the wharves of Darling Harbour. Trains rolled through the narrow cutting for well over a century, past the Ultimo power station, until the last goods train made its way out in 1984. After that the line fell silent and the rails rusted.

In 2015 the corridor reopened as The Goods Line, an 800-metre walking and cycling park designed by ASPECT Studios with CHROFI for the Sydney Harbour Foreshore Authority. It threads past the Powerhouse Museum, the University of Technology Sydney, TAFE and the ABC, linking Railway Square through to Darling Harbour and Chinatown.

Comparisons with New York's High Line were inevitable, and the designers leaned into the idea of a railway reborn as public space. Timber decks, study pods and table-tennis tables now sit where boxcars once stood. In one of the densest parts of the city, it has become a busy everyday shortcut as much as a park.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Foreshore Authority; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Goods Line linear park at Ultimo

properties.title: The 1965 Freedom Ride
properties.summary: In February 1965 a busload of University of Sydney students toured country New South Wales, confronting segregation and forcing the nation to see it on television.
body: On 12 February 1965 a hired bus left the University of Sydney carrying students from a group called Student Action for Aborigines. Their president was Charles Perkins, an Arrernte and Kalkadoon man and one of only two Aboriginal students at the university at the time. Over the next fortnight the group toured towns across western and northern New South Wales, returning on 27 February.

They went looking for the colour bar and found it everywhere. At Walgett the Returned and Services League club barred Aboriginal ex-servicemen. At Moree and Kempsey the public pools shut Aboriginal children out. The students picketed and protested, and at Walgett and Moree they met real hostility, including a run-in that almost forced their bus off the road.

Reporters and cameras travelled with them, and the footage carried Australia's everyday segregation into living rooms around the country. The Freedom Ride is remembered as a turning point on the road to the 1967 referendum, and it launched Perkins into a long public life in Aboriginal affairs.
properties.source: AIATSIS; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Student Action for Aborigines bus at Boggabilla, February 1965

properties.title: Andrew 'Boy' Charlton
properties.summary: At sixteen Andrew Charlton won Olympic gold in world-record time at the 1924 Paris Games. The Domain harbour pool where he made his name still carries it.
body: Andrew Murray Charlton, known to a besotted public simply as 'Boy', was sixteen when he lined up for the 1500 metres freestyle at the 1924 Paris Olympics. He had already shaken the swimming world that January by beating the great Swede Arne Borg over 440 and 880 yards at the Domain Baths on Sydney Harbour. In the Paris final he ran down Borg and won by about forty metres in 20 minutes 6.6 seconds, a new Olympic and world record.

Paris also brought him a silver in the relay and a bronze in the 400 metres. Four years later at Amsterdam he added two more silvers, in the 400 and the 1500. He was modest about the fuss, uneasy in the spotlight, and he left the pool young to take up sheep on the land near Goulburn.

The harbourside Domain Baths where he first made his name were later renamed the Andrew (Boy) Charlton Pool. It remains one of the loveliest places to swim in the city, looking out across the water he once tore through as a teenager.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: Andrew 'Boy' Charlton (left) with other swimmers at the Domain Baths

properties.title: The Tramsheds
properties.summary: Sydney's largest surviving tram depot opened at Forest Lodge in 1904, fell derelict after the trams stopped running, and was reborn in 2016 as a food hall built around a single restored tram.
body: The Rozelle tram depot opened at Forest Lodge on 17 April 1904, built to house the trams of the newly electrified Balmain line. Enlarged in 1907, the great Federation shed could hold around 166 trams and kept the inner-western services running until Sydney's tram network was wound up in the late 1950s.

After the trams went, the building simply sat. For decades it stood derelict, its stored carriages broken and tagged by vandals, a vast brick shell waiting for a use. Mirvac eventually bought the site as part of the Harold Park redevelopment.

In September 2016 the heritage shed reopened as Tramsheds Harold Park, a food and retail hall under its restored sawtooth roof. At its centre sits tram R1 1995, the very last tram to run on Sydney's original network, restored and parked where thousands of carriages once sheltered between shifts.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The restored Tramsheds at Forest Lodge

properties.title: The Spanish Quarter
properties.summary: A pocket of Spain grew up at the southern edge of the city, anchored by the Spanish Club on Liverpool Street and a cluster of restaurants and tapas bars born of post-war migration.
body: A migration agreement signed by Spain and Australia in August 1958 brought thousands of Spaniards to Sydney over the following years, some of them leaving Franco's regime behind. Many settled near the southern edge of the city, and a small Spanish quarter began to take shape.

The Spanish Club, Club Espanol, opened at 88 Liverpool Street in 1962. By the late 1960s it counted around two thousand members and billed itself as the centre for Spaniards in Australia, with a library, a cinema, art on the walls and Spanish food and wine.

Restaurants gathered around it over time, among them Don Quixote and Capitan Torres. By the 1980s the corner near Liverpool and Kent Streets was known, unofficially, as the Spanish Quarter, and its banners and paella still draw crowds for the annual fiesta.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; MHNSW Migration Heritage Centre

properties.title: The Eight Hour Day
properties.summary: Sydney stonemasons won an eight-hour working day in 1855, putting them among the first organised workers anywhere to achieve it.
body: In August 1855 the Operative Stonemasons' Society gave Sydney's employers six months' notice that masons would work no more than eight hours a day. The men cutting stone for Holy Trinity, the Garrison Church, and the nearby Mariners' Church did not wait for the deadline. They downed tools early and won the shorter day, then celebrated with a dinner on 1 October 1855.

Their banner carried Robert Owen's old creed, eight hours labour, eight hours recreation, eight hours rest. The three eights became one of the lasting symbols of the labour movement, stitched onto union banners for generations.

Sydney's win came months before Melbourne's more famous stonemasons' action of 1856, and the two cities have argued ever since over which was truly first. Both fed the grand annual Eight Hour Day processions, and both stand behind today's Labour Day holiday.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: A trades-union float in the Eight Hour Day procession, 1934 (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: First Government House
properties.summary: Governor Phillip's 1788 residence was the colony's first seat of government, and its buried foundations now lie beneath the Museum of Sydney.
body: On 15 May 1788, barely three months after the First Fleet landed, Governor Arthur Phillip laid the foundation stone of a permanent house on the rise above Sydney Cove, at what became the corner of Bridge and Phillip Streets. He moved into the modest two-storey Georgian house in April 1789.

For the colony's first nine governors, from Phillip through to Gipps, the house served as both residence and seat of government. As Sydney grew and the waterfront pressed in around it, the building fell into disrepair. It was demolished through the mid-1840s and had almost disappeared by the end of 1846.

Its buried foundations were rediscovered in the 1980s. In 1995 the Museum of Sydney opened on the site, conserving the old footings beneath its forecourt, the literal foundations of European settlement laid down beside the cove.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; MHNSW
properties.image_caption: An 1833 view of the First Government House, Sydney

properties.title: The Lord Nelson
properties.summary: A squat sandstone pub on a Millers Point corner that has brewed its own English-style ale in the basement since 1986. It is one of the long-running claimants to the title of Sydney's oldest.
body: The hotel stands on the corner of Kent and Argyle Streets in Millers Point, built in the late 1830s by the plasterer William Wells from sandstone quarried near the foot of Observatory Hill. Wells was granted the land in May 1836 and slowly turned his colonial house into a pub. He was licensed in the early 1840s, and June 1842 is the date usually given for the place trading as the Lord Nelson.

Since 1986 the pub has run a small brewery in the basement, turning out house ales with suitably nautical names. Three Sheets, Old Admiral and Victory Bitter are poured a few steps from where they are made.

It bills itself as Sydney's oldest continuously licensed hotel. The title is genuinely contested. The nearby Hero of Waterloo and the Fortune of War on George Street press rival claims, each resting on a different test of what 'oldest' should mean. The argument is a friendly one, and it has never quite been settled.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Lord Nelson Brewery Hotel, Millers Point

properties.title: German Sydney
properties.summary: Vinedressers, merchants and musicians built a German Sydney whose clubs survived the hostility of the world wars.
body: Germans were among colonial Sydney's quieter builders. They tended the Macarthur and Cox vineyards, worked as missionaries, merchants, printers and cabinetmakers, and above all played a large part in the city's concert life as musicians. They also gathered to keep their language and company. A German gentlemen's club, Der Deutsche Verein, formed in Phillip Street in 1853, the oldest such club in the country.

The Concordia Club followed in 1883. It began as nothing more than a group of card players meeting at the home of August and Carl Sommer, before taking premises of its own. At first it was strictly German, then it made room for Swiss and Austrians, and in time for everyone else.

The First World War fell hard on these communities. Many German nationals were interned, the old German Club had closed by 1915, and German place-names were scrubbed from the streets. In Sydney, Schlinkers Lane became Bullecourt Lane, named for the battlefield in France.

The Concordia Club came through it all. It prospered after the Second World War, spent more than fifty years at Stanmore, and moved to Tempe in 2003, where it carries on a thread of German Sydney that has held through a difficult century.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; MHNSW Migration Heritage Centre

properties.title: The Sirius Building
properties.summary: The Brutalist apartment block at The Rocks was built to rehouse residents displaced by redevelopment. It became the heart of the Save Our Sirius campaign.
body: Sirius rose at the foot of the Harbour Bridge around 1980, a stack of raw concrete modules designed by Housing Commission architect Tao Gofers. Its purpose was to rehouse low income residents uprooted by the redevelopment of The Rocks, so it stood as a built answer to the Green Bans and the Battle for The Rocks. Its 79 flats, from one to four bedrooms, gave pensioners and workers a home with one of the best views in the world.

When the state government moved to sell the site, the Heritage Council recommended listing the building in 2016. Two successive ministers refused. The Land and Environment Court found one refusal invalid and of no legal effect, ruling the minister had made procedural errors, yet the next decision still went against listing.

The Save Our Sirius campaign turned the block into a landmark argument about public housing and the worth of Brutalist heritage. The government sold the site in June 2019 for 150 million dollars, and the building was converted into private apartments.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW Land and Environment Court
properties.image_caption: The Sirius building at The Rocks

properties.title: Lachlan Swamps
properties.summary: The spring-fed wetlands that watered colonial Sydney through Busby's Bore, now the ornamental ponds of Centennial Park.
body: When the Tank Stream gave out, Sydney turned to the Lachlan Swamps for its drinking water. These spring-fed wetlands sat on the Sydney Common and took their name from Governor Lachlan Macquarie.
