The colony's surveyor John Busby proposed tapping them, and from 1827 convict gangs began hand-digging a tunnel to carry the water across to town.

Water started flowing through Busby's Bore in 1830, though the tunnel was not finished until 1837. It ran more than three kilometres underground to a reservoir at the Oxford Street end of Hyde Park, on the site of the old racecourse. For about eight years the bore was Sydney's main supply, until a drought in 1838 showed how easily the swamps could run low and pollution began to foul the water.

When the Common was turned into Centennial Park for the 1888 centenary of European settlement, the old wetlands were reshaped into the chain of ornamental ponds that walkers circle today. The city's first proper reservoir now hides in plain sight as parkland.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Centennial Parklands
properties.image_caption: A pond in Centennial Park, on the site of the Lachlan Swamps

properties.title: Kent Brewery
properties.summary: Tooth's Kent Brewery brewed on Broadway for 170 years under its leaping White Horse sign. The site is now the Central Park towers.
body: In 1835 John Tooth and his brother-in-law Charles Newnham opened the Kent Brewery on a few acres beside Parramatta Road at Chippendale, where Broadway runs today. Tooth had been born in Kent, the great hop-growing county of England, and he hung a leaping White Horse above the gates. The horse was the old emblem of Kent, traced back to the Saxon chiefs Hengist and Horsa, and it became the company's trademark for the next century and more.

Tooth & Co grew into one of Sydney's largest industrial concerns. Its beer, along with that of the rival Tooheys nearby, kept the city's pubs supplied for generations. The Adelaide Steamship Company sold the brewing side to Carlton & United Breweries in 1983, and the coppers boiled their last in 2005, ending 170 years of brewing on the one spot.

The vast site was rebuilt as the Central Park precinct, topped by Jean Nouvel's One Central Park, finished in 2014, with its hanging gardens down the facade. A cantilevered array of mirrors and rooftop heliostats catches the sun and throws it down into the streets and terraces below, throwing daylight across the ground where the brewery once stood.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: One Central Park on Broadway, built on the old Kent Brewery site

properties.title: Lady Bay, Sydney's first nude beach
properties.summary: A tiny cove below South Head that became Sydney's first legal nude beach in 1976.
body: Tucked below the cliffs just past Camp Cove, Lady Bay sits at the end of the South Head peninsula. It is also known as Lady Jane Beach, and it is a small, sheltered patch of harbour sand reached by a steep stairway down from the headland. Naturists had been using the cove quietly for years before it was given any legal standing.

In December 1976 the Wran state government declared Lady Bay a clothing-optional beach. That made it Sydney's first legal nude beach and one of the earliest in the country. It has stayed clothing-optional ever since.

The beach has also been an occasional flashpoint in the city's long argument over who its shoreline really belongs to. Debates over access, behaviour and who feels welcome on the sand have flared here more than once.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; contemporary news reports
properties.image_caption: Lady Bay Beach below South Head, Sydney

properties.title: Bronte Beach and its ocean baths
properties.summary: One of Sydney's oldest beach reserves, opened in 1887 with sea baths cut into the rocks and an early surf club that helped pioneer the movement.
body: Bronte became a public reserve in 1887, after Waverley Council won government approval to build sea baths at both Bronte and Bondi. The baths were carved into the rocks at the southern end of the beach and a caretaker was appointed that October. They remain among the oldest ocean pools on the Sydney coast.

Bronte Surf Life Saving Club formed in 1903, in the first years of the surf bathing movement. The club has long claimed to be the world's first, and so has Bondi, which formed the same year. When Surf Life Saving Australia weighed the rival claims in 2005, it recognised Bondi as the first. Bronte sits comfortably among the very oldest either way.
The name has nothing to do with the novelists. It comes from nearby Bronte House and the dukedom of Bronte, a Sicilian title that King Ferdinand granted Admiral Lord Nelson in 1799.
properties.source: Waverley Council; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Bronte Beach and its ocean pool

properties.title: Shelly Beach & Cabbage Tree Bay
properties.summary: Shelly Beach is one of the few Sydney ocean beaches that faces back toward the city, and the cove beside it became a fully protected no-take marine reserve in 2002.
body: Tucked around the headland from Manly, Shelly Beach is one of the rare Sydney ocean beaches that faces west, looking back across the water toward the city rather than out to the open sea. That orientation, sheltered by the surrounding sandstone headlands, gives it calm water and an unusual sense of stillness for a beach so close to the surf at Manly.

In March 2002 the whole of Cabbage Tree Bay was declared a no-take aquatic reserve, running about 20 hectares from the southern end of Manly Beach around to the Shelly Beach headland. Fishing and collecting are banned, even down to empty shells, because those shells become homes for living things.

With that protection the bay has become one of the best snorkelling and diving spots on the metropolitan coast. Swimmers regularly meet curious eastern blue gropers, wobbegong sharks resting under ledges and giant cuttlefish, part of a recorded mix of more than 160 fish species and around 50 kinds of marine invertebrate.
properties.source: NSW Fisheries (DPI); Northern Beaches Council
properties.image_caption: Shelly Beach and Cabbage Tree Bay, Manly

properties.title: Harry's Cafe de Wheels
properties.summary: A pie cart on the Woolloomooloo wharf that has fed sailors and night owls since the 1930s, famous for the Tiger pie piled with peas, mash and gravy.
body: Harry 'Tiger' Edwards rolled a small converted caravan up to the gates of the Woolloomooloo naval dockyard in the mid 1930s, selling pies and crumbed sausages to anyone still hungry late at night. The nickname Tiger came from his boxing during wartime service in the Middle East, and it stuck.

The cart closed around 1938 when Harry enlisted in the AIF, and stayed shut through the war. He reopened in 1945, finding that Sydney was still a hard place to get a decent late-night feed, and the green caravan has run on the wharf ever since.

The signature dish is the Tiger, a meat pie crowned with mushy peas, mashed potato and gravy. Over the decades the queue has drawn sailors, cabbies and coppers alongside visiting celebrities, whose photographs now cover the walls.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Harry's Cafe de Wheels, Woolloomooloo, 1948

properties.title: Doyle's on the Beach
properties.summary: A Watsons Bay seafood institution that grew from beachside tea rooms opened by a local fishing family in 1885.
body: Doyle's looks out over the harbour from the old fishing village of Watsons Bay, and its story starts in 1885 when a local fisherman, Henry Newton, and his wife Hannah opened tea rooms on the beach. They were the grandparents of Alice Doyle, the woman whose name the place now carries.

The tea rooms blew down in 1907, and the following year the Newtons' daughter Eva built the two-storey Ozone Cafe on the same spot. The business faltered during the Depression. After World War II, around 1948, Alice Doyle revived the dining room, and it grew into the seafood restaurant that generations of Sydneysiders have known.

Bound up with the bay's fishing families across several generations, Doyle's is often called Australia's oldest continually running fish and chip business. For decades a plate of fish caught on the harbour, eaten after a trip on the Watsons Bay ferry, was a Sunday ritual.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Doyles
properties.image_caption: Doyle's restaurant on the beach at Watsons Bay

properties.title: Curlew Camp, Sirius Cove
properties.summary: The harbourside artists' camp at Little Sirius Cove in Mosman where Arthur Streeton and Tom Roberts painted some of their best-known Sydney works.
body: On the wooded eastern shore of Little Sirius Cove, a camp of tents and a timber dining hut took shape around 1890, set up by the Sydney clothing manufacturer Reuben Brasch. It soon became a gathering point for artists, writers and musicians who wanted a cheap and sociable retreat within easy reach of the city.

When the economic crash hit Melbourne, the Heidelberg School painters Arthur Streeton and Tom Roberts came north and settled here, Streeton recalling the camp's busy years as about 1891 to 1898. Working out of doors on the harbour, they produced luminous pictures such as Streeton's 'Sirius Cove' and Roberts's 'The Camp, Sirius Cove'.

Visitors reached the camp by the Mosman ferry and a walk around the point, and life there ran to home comforts including a shared cook and a billiards tent. The camp wound down in the years before the First World War, by which time Taronga Zoo was taking shape on the slopes above.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Art Gallery of NSW
properties.image_caption: Tom Roberts, 'The Camp, Sirius Cove'

properties.title: Trim, Flinders's cat
properties.summary: Trim was the ship's cat who sailed right around Australia with Matthew Flinders, and he is now cast in bronze on the wall of the Mitchell Library.
body: Trim was a black-and-white cat born aboard ship around 1799, and he became the constant companion of the navigator Matthew Flinders. He sailed on HMS Investigator during the 1801 to 1803 voyage that first circumnavigated and charted the Australian coast, and he even survived the wreck of HMS Porpoise on a reef in 1803.

Flinders adored him. While held prisoner by the French on Mauritius, he wrote a fond little biography of his cat and called him the best and most illustrious of his race. Trim vanished on the island around 1804, and a grieving Flinders bitterly assumed he had been stolen and eaten.

A small bronze of Trim, by sculptor John Cornwell, has perched on a window ledge of the Mitchell Library since 1996. A full statue of Flinders himself, by Mark Richards, was unveiled outside on Macquarie Street in 2014, for the bicentenary of the navigator's death. Years earlier, in 1795 and 1796, Flinders and George Bass had explored Botany Bay and the Georges River in a tiny open boat they named Tom Thumb.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: The statue of Trim on the Mitchell Library window ledge

properties.title: The Argyle Cut
properties.summary: A road carved through the sandstone ridge of The Rocks, begun by convict chain gangs in the 1840s and finished with explosives in 1859.
body: The Argyle Cut slices through the sandstone spine of The Rocks, carrying Argyle Street from Sydney Cove across to Millers Point and Darling Harbour. Governor Macquarie had floated the idea of a passage through the ridge around 1816, but nothing happened for decades. Real work only began by 1843, when convict chain gangs were set to hacking at the rock with hand tools under a harsh overseer named Tim Lane.

The job proved too much for picks and crude tools, and the government left it half finished. Transportation to New South Wales had ended in 1840, and many residents disliked the sight and sound of men labouring in chains in the middle of town. The Sydney Municipal Council took over and completed the cut with explosives and paid labour in 1859. The spoil was carted off to fill the mouth of the Tank Stream and to shore up the sea wall at Circular Quay.

Overhead bridges were thrown across the cut through the 1860s, giving it the deep, tunnel-like feel it still has today. Look closely at the walls and you can pick out the marks left by the convict gangs' hand tools, a reminder of the brutal early labour that started the work.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; The Rocks Discovery Museum
properties.image_caption: The Argyle Cut through the sandstone ridge at The Rocks

properties.title: Parsley Bay and its bridge
properties.summary: Parsley Bay is a sheltered Vaucluse picnic cove crossed by a slender white suspension footbridge. It has been a public reserve since 1907.
body: Parsley Bay tucks into the harbour foreshore at Vaucluse, a small green cove that became a public reserve in 1907. Since then it has been a favourite for family picnics and harbour swimming, with shady lawns running down to a netted enclosure where children have learned to swim for generations.

The bay's landmark is its footbridge, a single wire-cable suspension span that still floats across the mouth of the inlet. It was built in 1910 to a design by Edwin S Sautelle, the town clerk and engineer for the old Vaucluse Council. The graceful white lines of the bridge remain the focal point of the reserve.

When Vaucluse was amalgamated with Woollahra in 1948, the reserve passed to Woollahra Council, which has looked after it ever since. More than a century on, the little bay is still one of the harbour's most loved playgrounds.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Woollahra Council
properties.image_caption: Parsley Bay and its suspension footbridge, Vaucluse

properties.title: “Welcome to television”
properties.summary: Australia's first regular television service went to air from the TCN-9 studios at Willoughby on 16 September 1956.
body: Australia's first regular television service began on the evening of 16 September 1956, broadcast from the TCN-9 studios on Sydney's lower north shore at Willoughby. Test transmissions had been running since July, but this was the night ordinary viewers could finally tune in. The announcer John Godson opened the station on air, and then a young man named Bruce Gyngell appeared and became the first face most Australians ever saw on a screen.

Gyngell's greeting, 'Good evening, and welcome to television', has become one of the most quoted lines in Australian broadcasting. The original footage was not kept, so the clip people know today is a recreation Gyngell made later, most likely for the station's first anniversary in 1957. The station ran for about three hours a night through its first weeks, then held its formal opening on 27 October 1956.

TCN-9 beat the Melbourne stations whose launch is popularly tied to that year's Olympic Games, which gave this plain suburban studio a fair claim as the birthplace of Australian television. The Willoughby site stayed Nine's Sydney home for more than sixty years before the move to North Sydney in 2020.
properties.source: National Film and Sound Archive; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Chinese Garden of Friendship
properties.summary: A walled southern Chinese garden at the edge of Darling Harbour, opened in January 1988 for the Bicentenary. It was a gift of friendship from Guangdong, the sister state of New South Wales.
body: At the southern edge of Darling Harbour, hard against Chinatown, the Chinese Garden of Friendship opened on 17 January 1988 to mark Australia's Bicentenary. It was built to honour the sister-state bond between New South Wales and Guangdong in southern China, and the sister-city link between Sydney and Guangzhou.

The Guangzhou Garden Planning and Building Design Institute drew up the design in the southern Chinese tradition, and Chinese and Australian craftsmen built it together. Guangdong provided the plans and many of the key materials, while New South Wales funded and ran the work through the Darling Harbour Authority. The result is a careful arrangement of lakes, pavilions and dragon walls that sets out to slow a visitor down in the middle of the city.
This is an ornamental garden made for contemplation, and it is a different thing entirely from the working Chinese market gardens that once fed Sydney from its low-lying flats.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Chinese Garden of Friendship, Darling Harbour

properties.title: Saving the Wolli Creek valley
properties.summary: The last bushland of Sydney's inner south-west, saved from a freeway by a long-running local campaign and a damning road inquiry.
body: The Wolli Creek valley is a ribbon of bushland that winds through Earlwood, Bardwell Park and Turrella. For decades it survived almost by accident. A road corridor reserved under the 1951 Cumberland County Plan kept houses and factories out, leaving the trees and sandstone to grow wild while the bulldozers waited.

The road never came as planned. The Kyeemagh-Chullora Road Inquiry, led by barrister David Kirby, reported in 1981 that the freeway made little economic sense and recommended the reservation be lifted and the valley returned to the public. Residents formed the Wolli Creek Preservation Society in 1983 and campaigned for years to defend that win. When the M5 East was finally approved, it was cut back to four lanes and tunnelled to the south, under Bardwell Park and Arncliffe, with the tunnel opening in 2002.

With the surface road gone, the valley could be protected at last. In 1998 the government committed to a new park, and over the following two decades the land was acquired piece by piece, building up to roughly 50 hectares of Wolli Creek Regional Park. It is a stretch of wild green country only a few kilometres from the airport, saved as much by patience as by protest.
properties.source: Wolli Creek Preservation Society; NPWS
properties.image_caption: Sandstone bushland along the Two Valley Trail, Wolli Creek Regional Park

properties.title: MacCallum Pool, Cremorne Point
properties.summary: A small free harbour pool on the western shore of Cremorne Point, beside a foreshore garden one couple tended for fifty years.
body: On the western shore of Cremorne Point sits MacCallum Pool, a small free swimming pool that began around 1915 as a tidal pool. The local resident Fred Lane, who had won the 200m freestyle at the 1900 Paris Olympics, started shifting boulders to shape it against the harbour.

Through the 1920s Hugh MacCallum took on much of the work and organised paying members to fund its upkeep. North Sydney Council took the pool over in 1930 and named it for him. It was carefully restored in heritage style in the mid-1980s, and swimmers still look straight out across the water as they do their laps.

Just along the foreshore is the garden begun in 1959 by Lex and Ruby Graham, who tended its terraces and harbour views for some fifty years as a gift to the public. The garden is now protected by the National Trust and kept up in their name.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Trust (NSW); Mosman Council
properties.image_caption: MacCallum Pool on the harbour at Cremorne Point

properties.title: Snapper Island, the ship-shaped isle
properties.summary: A tiny rocky outcrop in the Parramatta River off Drummoyne that sea cadets reclaimed, enlarged and reshaped into the form of a ship in the early 1930s.
body: Snapper Island was once a small rocky outcrop in the Parramatta River off Drummoyne. In 1930 Leonard 'Len' Forsythe, a founder of the Navy League in New South Wales, leased it from the Commonwealth as a place to train boys in seamanship. He put around fifty Sea Cadets to work, and over the following year they cleared the scrub and blasted roughly a thousand tonnes of rock off the top.

By the early 1930s the cadets had built the stone seawalls into the outline of a ship, with the bow pointing towards Cockatoo Island and the stern towards Drummoyne. The reclamation enlarged the island to more than seven times its original size. The training depot TS Sydney was established here as a memorial to HMAS Sydney (I), the cruiser decommissioned in 1928 and broken up for scrap in 1932.

Forsythe opened a museum of naval relics on the island in 1960, and for years it drew small boats of visitors. The collection was moved to Spectacle Island and Garden Island around 2006, and the island has sat empty ever since, its odd ship-shaped walls weathering quietly in the harbour.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Harbour Federation Trust
properties.image_caption: Snapper Island in the Parramatta River

properties.title: The 1938 Empire Games
properties.summary: The third British Empire Games came to Sydney in February 1938, staged to mark the city's 150th birthday.
body: From 5 to 12 February 1938 Sydney played host to the third British Empire Games, timed to crown the city's sesquicentenary, 150 years on from 1788. The athletics drew the crowds to the Sydney Cricket Ground, while the swimmers raced at the North Sydney Olympic Pool, a sleek new venue opened in 1936 right beside the Harbour Bridge.

Fifteen nations sent 464 athletes to seven sports, among them boxing, cycling, rowing and lawn bowls. The undisputed star was the West Australian sprinter Decima Norman, who came home with five gold medals across the sprints, relays and long jump. The Sydney crowd roared her on like she was Bradman.
These were the last Games before the world tipped into war. The next meet would not be held until 1950, once peace had returned.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Commonwealth Games Federation
properties.image_caption: North Sydney Olympic Pool, the Games' swimming venue (opened 1936)

properties.title: The convicts' Great North Road
properties.summary: Convict gangs cut this road north to the Hunter through the 1820s and 1830s, and the Devines Hill stonework above Wisemans Ferry is now World Heritage listed.
body: To link Sydney with the Hunter Valley, gangs of convicts hacked the Great North Road out of the bush between 1825 and 1836, many of them labouring in irons. It was punishing work, swinging picks at hard sandstone with the threat of the lash never far away.

The most spectacular surviving stretch climbs Devines Hill above Wisemans Ferry, built around 1829 to 1832 after a steeper first attempt up nearby Finchs Line was abandoned. Surveyor General Thomas Mitchell chose the route, and more than 500 convicts raised its ramp of massive hand-cut retaining walls, buttresses and stone culverts entirely by hand. Some of the walling stands close to nine metres high.

On the far north-western edge of greater Sydney, this is one of the best-preserved convict roads in the country. In 2010 it was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List as part of the Australian Convict Sites, a group of eleven places that tell the story of transportation.
properties.source: NSW NPWS; UNESCO; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The convict-built cutting at Devines Hill

properties.title: The capsize of the Rodney
properties.summary: In February 1938 a small harbour ferry capsized off Bradleys Head when its passengers rushed to one side, drowning nineteen people.
body: On 13 February 1938 the little wooden ferry Rodney steamed out onto the harbour, packed with people waving off the American cruiser USS Louisville as it left after Sydney's sesquicentenary celebrations. Dozens of small craft jostled around the warship that afternoon, eager for a closer look.

As the Rodney turned to pass behind the cruiser, the crowd surged across the deck for a last wave. The overloaded ferry listed hard, rolled and capsized in deep water off Bradleys Head, going under within a couple of minutes. Around fifty people were pulled from the harbour alive.

Nineteen drowned, seventeen of them women, along with one man and a seven-year-old boy. Some bodies were not recovered until the ferry was raised from the bottom. In 1939 the High Court found the vessel had capsized through negligence, and the disaster lingered as a hard lesson about overloaded boats and an over-eager crowd.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: The ferry Rodney listing moments before she capsized, 13 February 1938

properties.title: Maroubra Beach
properties.summary: A long south-eastern surf beach with a powerful break, Aboriginal rock carvings on its headland and one of the country's oldest surf clubs.
body: Maroubra is one of Sydney's great south-eastern surf beaches, known for a heavy and unpredictable break that has shaped a fierce local surf culture for more than a century. The name is usually traced to an Aboriginal word said to mean something like place of thunder, after the surf pounding the rocks, though the meaning is not certain.

The beach sits on Country long used by the local Aboriginal people, who camped and fished here. Rock carvings survive on the northern headland, and a stone tool workshop was uncovered at the southern end of the beach in the early twentieth century.

Organised surf lifesaving took hold here from 1906, and the Maroubra club was one of the nine foundation members of the Surf Bathing Association of New South Wales when it formed in October 1907. Behind the dunes lies Heffron Park, and the beach remains a magnet for swimmers and surfers alike.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Randwick City Council
properties.image_caption: Maroubra Beach

properties.title: Hunters Hill, the stone garden suburb
properties.summary: A peninsula of sandstone villas raised by French promoters and Italian and Swiss stonemasons. It is often called Australia's oldest surviving garden suburb.
body: On the peninsula between the Parramatta and Lane Cove rivers, Hunters Hill grew from the 1840s into a leafy enclave of sandstone villas. People often call it Australia's oldest surviving garden suburb, decades before that term was coined.

Its makers were the French-born Joubert brothers, Didier and Jules. Didier bought Mary Reibey's Figtree Farm in 1847, and the building really got going after Jules arrived in 1854. Jules brought out around seventy workmen, mostly stonemasons, to put up houses for sale. From 1855 hundreds of migrants from northern Italy and the Italian-speaking parts of Switzerland reached Sydney, and some of them settled here and worked the stone by hand.

For a time the area was known as the French Village. In 1981 the Heritage Council of New South Wales moved to protect more than three hundred of its old buildings and structures, and the suburb survives today as one of Sydney's finest conservation areas.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Passy, the Joubert villa at Hunters Hill

properties.title: The Sydney to Hobart
properties.summary: The Boxing Day ocean race that has started on Sydney Harbour every year since 1945.
body: It began in 1945 as a relaxed cruise to Tasmania planned by Peter Luke and a few friends. A visiting Royal Navy officer, Captain John Illingworth, suggested they make it a race instead. Nine boats set out, and Illingworth's cutter Rani was first into Hobart after six days, fourteen hours and twenty-two minutes.

From those casual beginnings it grew into one of the world's great ocean races. Every Boxing Day the fleet crowds a start line in the eastern harbour off Nielsen Park, then pours out through the Heads for the 630 nautical mile run south.

It is also a race that can turn deadly. In the 1998 storm, with winds well over 65 knots and waves up to 15 metres, six sailors died and five yachts sank. Only 44 of the 115 starters reached Hobart, and 55 sailors were plucked from the water by helicopters and navy vessels. The disaster forced a hard rethink of safety in ocean racing.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Boxing Day start of the Sydney to Hobart on the harbour

properties.title: Yurulbin Point
properties.summary: A headland at the meeting of the waters on the Balmain peninsula, given back its Aboriginal name in 1994.
body: At the northern tip of the Balmain peninsula, this headland marks where Sydney Harbour gives way to the Parramatta River. The tides here run fast and the water broadens out, and the place sits close to the border of Wangal and Gadigal Country, generally understood to fall just within Wangal land.

Colonists called it Long Nose Point for its shape. On 8 July 1994 it was given back its Aboriginal name, Yurulbin, usually translated as swift running water, at a reconciliation ceremony attended by local people and community groups.

The small reserve at the end of the point, Yurulbin Park, was made from a derelict industrial site in the early 1970s. It now looks out over the broad reach of water that has long made this a meeting place.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney, Barani
properties.image_caption: Yurulbin Point, Birchgrove

properties.title: Little Portugal, Petersham
properties.summary: The inner-west strip on and around Audley Street that became the heart of Portuguese Sydney.
body: From the 1970s, Portuguese migrants settled around Audley Street and New Canterbury Road in Petersham. Many had first lived in Paddington and Darlinghurst and moved west for cheaper housing. They opened the cafes, grocers and charcoal-chicken and bacalhau restaurants that earned the strip its nickname, Little Portugal.

Petersham and neighbouring Marrickville grew into what is often called the largest Portuguese community in the country. Around 40 per cent of Sydney's Portuguese live in the area, and the streets still carry the food, language and faith they brought with them.

Marrickville Council made the name official in July 2019, formally christening the Audley Street precinct Little Portugal. Each year since 2003 the Bairro Portugues festival has filled the street with Fado music, custard tarts and the colours of Portugal.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Inner West Council

properties.title: Galgalla: the smallpox of 1789
properties.summary: In the autumn of 1789, about fifteen months after the First Fleet anchored, the smallpox the Eora called galgalla swept the clans of Sydney Harbour and emptied much of the coast in a single season.
body: In April 1789 the colonists at Sydney Cove began to notice that the people of the harbour had stopped coming to the shoreline. When boats went out to look, they found the reason. The Eora knew the sickness as galgalla. Whole families lay dead in the rock shelters and caves that had always given them refuge in bad weather. The naval officer William Bradley recorded the dead on the beaches and in the caverns between the cove and the Heads, some who seemed to have died of thirst while too ill to crawl to water, children left beside a dead parent. Governor Phillip reckoned that about half the local Aboriginal people had died, and modern estimates put the mortality among communities with no immunity nearer seventy percent. David Collins wrote that of the Cadigal, whose country lay along the southern shore, only about three were left alive.

Among those who tried to help was Arabanoo, a young Eora man the colonists had seized late in 1788. He went out with the search parties, brought survivors back to a hut near the hospital, and nursed two orphaned children, Nanbaree and Boorong, before the disease took him too. He died on 18 May 1789. How galgalla reached Sydney at all is still argued, and the argument is a serious one rather than settled fact. Some historians point to the British, whether through the variolous matter the fleet's surgeons are known to have carried or, more contentiously, a deliberate act. Others, following Judy Campbell's careful case, trace it south from Macassan trepang fishers working the northern coasts. A documented minority, going back to a 1980s exchange involving Frank Fenner, have even questioned whether the disease was smallpox or chickenpox.

What no one disputes is the scale of what happened. In the space of a few weeks the clans that had lived around this harbour for thousands of years were thinned past recognition, their elders, their knowledge and their hold on country broken in a season the survivors would spend the rest of their lives reckoning with. The Sydney that grew up around the cove did so on emptied ground, and much of what came after, from Bennelong's diplomacy to the slow loss of the coastal clans, begins here, with the bodies in the caves.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW (and historians Campbell, Warren, Mear)
properties.image_caption: Sydney Cove and Port Jackson, around the time of the epidemic

properties.title: The Second Fleet, the 'Death Fleet'
properties.summary: In June 1790 the Second Fleet brought hundreds of sick and dying convicts to Sydney Cove. Its mortality was so dreadful that it became known as the Death Fleet.
body: In late June 1790 the Second Fleet reached Sydney Cove and brought with it a scandal. The Surprize anchored on 26 June, the Neptune and Scarborough two days later. Where the First Fleet had landed its convicts in reasonable health, these ships carried prisoners who were skeletal, filthy and barely alive. Many had to be lifted ashore. Of roughly a thousand convicts who set out, about a quarter died at sea, and close to forty per cent were dead within six months of landing.

Much of the blame lay in the contract. The shipping firm was paid for convicts put aboard in England rather than for those delivered alive to New South Wales. With no reward for keeping prisoners healthy, the contractors crammed them below, chained them, and starved them. The Neptune was the worst of the three and lost 158 of its 502 convicts on the voyage.

A portable hospital had been sent out in pieces, some 602 numbered parts with a copper roof, and the colonists raced to raise it at the Cove. It was soon overwhelmed. Hundreds of sick were landed and scores died within days of reaching shore.

The colony was itself close to starvation and could barely feed the living, let alone tend the dying. Officers and chaplains who watched the convicts come ashore wrote of their horror in plain words. The name Death Fleet stuck, and it came to stand for the cruelty that the convict system could breed when profit was placed above human life.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: A view of Sydney Cove, where the Second Fleet landed its sick in 1790

properties.title: The Blue Gum High Forest
properties.summary: A tall forest of Sydney blue gum and blackbutt once covered the shale ridges of the upper North Shore. Clearing for housing has left only scattered, protected remnants of this critically endangered community.
body: Before the suburbs spread north, the shale ridges of Sydney's upper North Shore carried a magnificent tall forest. Sydney blue gum and blackbutt drew on the high rainfall and rich soils to grow as much as 40 metres high. This was the green country that met the Cammeraygal long before it met the first colonists, and it stood thick across what are now the gardens and streets of Pymble, Turramurra and St Ives.

Clearing and housing have been brutal. Only about four and a half per cent of the forest survives, perhaps 150 hectares scattered in pockets across the region. The best remnants are the Dalrymple-Hay Nature Reserve at St Ives, declared a protected forest in 1972, and Sheldon Forest at Turramurra. Both owe their survival in part to determined local campaigns, including years of lobbying by groups such as the South Turramurra Environmental Protection society.

Recognition came slowly. The community was not treated as seriously threatened until the mid 1990s, and it was only in 2005 that governments listed Blue Gum High Forest as critically endangered. Walking the reserves today, with the grey trunks rising overhead, you get a living glimpse of the forest that once ran for kilometres along these ridges.
properties.source: NSW Office of Environment; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Tall blackbutt in the Blue Gum High Forest remnant at St Ives

properties.title: The Anniversary Regatta
properties.summary: Sydney Harbour's great sailing regatta first ran on 26 January 1837. It has been held every year since, and is reckoned the oldest continuously conducted annual sailing regatta in the world.
body: On 26 January 1837 the first Anniversary Regatta was held on Sydney Harbour, marking the anniversary of the colony's founding. Yachts raced out from Sydney Cove and rounded the Sow and Pigs reef, while crowds gathered at Dawes Point and Mrs Macquaries Chair to watch. The day's program mixed two yacht races with rowing contests for whaleboats, gigs and watermen's skiffs, a spread of craft that put working harbour boats alongside the gentry's yachts.

The regatta is reckoned the oldest continuously conducted annual sailing regatta in the world. The Royal Hobart Regatta, which began a year later in 1838, runs it close. Sydney's has been sailed every year since 1837, through depressions, epidemics and two world wars.

Now known as the Australia Day Regatta, it still fills the harbour with sail each 26 January. The course and the crowds have changed, but the habit of marking the date on the water has held for nearly two centuries.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australia Day Regatta

properties.title: The Cowpastures
properties.summary: The plains southwest of Sydney where the colony's strayed First Fleet cattle were found running wild in 1795.
body: A handful of cattle vanished from the struggling settlement at Sydney Cove in June 1788, just months after the First Fleet arrived, and the colony gave them up for lost. Seven years later word reached Governor John Hunter of a great herd grazing the rich plains beyond the Nepean River. In November 1795 he rode out with a small party, crossed the river and climbed a hill to see the cattle for himself. He marked the country on his map as the Cow Pasture Plains.

The strayed beasts had thrived on the open grasslands and multiplied into a wild herd many times their original number. To Hunter and his successors the cattle were a precious resource, so in 1803 Governor King declared the district a protected government reserve and posted a gamekeeper to keep poachers out.

When the reserve was eventually opened to settlers, this fertile country became the heart of John and Elizabeth Macarthur's Camden Park. From here grew much of the colony's early wool and wine industry, on land that had long been the Country of the Dharawal and Gundungurra peoples.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Camden Park
properties.image_caption: Camden Park, in the heart of the old Cowpastures

properties.title: The Kokoda Track Memorial Walkway
properties.summary: A living memorial to the 1942 Kokoda campaign, opened in 1996 along the Parramatta River at Concord.
body: Along the mangrove shore of Brays Bay, between Concord Hospital and Rhodes station, a bushland path of more than 800 metres traces the memory of one of the hardest campaigns Australians ever fought. The Kokoda Track Memorial Walkway was unveiled on 3 October 1996 to honour the soldiers who held the Japanese advance along the Kokoda Track in Papua New Guinea in 1942.

A series of memorial stations runs the length of the walk, each marking a significant place or battle of the campaign with maps, photographs and the names of the men who served and fell. The planting was chosen to echo the wet jungle of the track itself.

The site was no accident. It sits beside the old repatriation hospital at Concord, where many returned servicemen were treated after the war, so that the memory of the campaign and the men who survived it would stand close together.
properties.source: Kokoda Track Memorial Walkway; NSW War Memorials Register
properties.image_caption: The Kokoda Track Memorial Walkway, Concord

properties.title: The Pyrmont quarries
properties.summary: The Pyrmont sandstone quarries, nicknamed Paradise, Purgatory and Hell Hole, that built much of grand colonial Sydney.
body: From 1853 the Saunders family cut a fine golden sandstone from the Pyrmont peninsula. Quarrymen called the best seam yellowblock. Freshly cut it came out soft and grey, then hardened and ripened to a warm honey colour as it weathered. Strong yet easy to carve, it was prized as some of the finest building stone in the world.

The Scottish quarrymen who worked the three great pits named them with grim humour after how hard the stone was to win. Paradise gave up its blocks kindly, Purgatory made them sweat, and Hell Hole was the worst of all.

Pyrmont stone faced many of Sydney's proudest buildings, among them the Town Hall, the Queen Victoria Building, the University quadrangle, the General Post Office and the cathedrals. The quarries fell silent around 1930, and their old workings now lie buried beneath the streets and apartments of Pyrmont.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Engineers Australia
properties.image_caption: A surviving yellowblock cliff at the old Paradise quarry, Pyrmont

properties.title: The Great Maritime Strike, 1890
properties.summary: The 1890 waterfront strike whose crushing defeat pushed the unions towards politics and the birth of the Labor Party.
body: In August 1890 a quarrel over whether ships' officers could belong to a union flared into the Great Maritime Strike. It pulled out seamen, wharf labourers, miners and shearers until some 28,500 workers across the Australian colonies had downed tools.

On the Sydney waterfront the tension peaked in George Street on 19 September 1890. The Riot Act was read to a crowd while mounted troopers and special constables stood ready, and wool wagons pushed through to Darling Harbour under guard. Starved out and short of funds, the unions were beaten by the start of 1891.

The defeat taught a hard lesson. If workers could not win on the wharves, they would seek power at the ballot box. The New South Wales Labor Electoral League, forerunner of the Labor Party, contested its first election in 1891 and won 35 of the 141 seats.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.title: The 1971 Springbok protests
properties.summary: When the all-white Springboks played at the Sydney Cricket Ground in July 1971, they ran into some of the largest anti-apartheid demonstrations Australia had yet seen.
body: In July 1971 South Africa's all-white Springbok rugby team came to the Sydney Cricket Ground, and the matches there drew some of the largest anti-apartheid protests the country had witnessed. Demonstrators massed outside the ground while police made arrest after arrest. Before one fixture, a group that included the secretary of the New South Wales Builders Labourers Federation even tried to saw down the goalposts.

Sydney was one front in a campaign that followed the team from city to city. The tour was bitter and confronting everywhere it went. In Queensland, premier Joh Bjelke-Petersen declared a state of emergency to push the protests back, and across the six weeks roughly 700 people were arrested.

The pressure told. Within weeks the planned 1971-72 South African cricket tour of Australia was called off, and no apartheid-era South African team would tour the country again until the 1990s. For many Australians the winter of 1971 was the moment sport and the politics of race could no longer be kept apart.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Museum of Australia

properties.title: The Walsh Bay wharves
properties.summary: The great timber finger wharves of Walsh Bay, rebuilt after the 1900 plague scare cleared the waterfront, now hold one of Sydney's busiest arts precincts.
body: When bubonic plague broke out in The Rocks in 1900, the government seized the waterfront and set about remaking it. Construction of the timber finger wharves in the small bay between Dawes Point and Millers Point began in 1912, and over the next decade the Sydney Harbour Trust raised five great piers and their brick and stone shore sheds. The bay took its name in 1919 from Henry Deane Walsh, the Trust's engineer-in-chief who oversaw the work.

Wool and general cargo crossed the wharves for decades, and troops embarked from them in two world wars. The complex is often described as the last intact group of its kind anywhere, a survivor from the age when goods still moved by ship and stevedore.

From the 1980s the wharves found a second life as the Walsh Bay arts precinct, home to the Sydney Theatre Company. A major restoration between 2018 and 2022 reopened them as theatres, studios and rehearsal rooms while keeping the old timber bones on show.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Walsh Bay, c.1919

properties.title: The Vietnam Moratorium, 1970
properties.summary: On 8 May 1970 tens of thousands of Sydneysiders filled the city for the first nationwide Vietnam Moratorium, demanding an end to Australia's part in the war.
body: On 8 May 1970 Sydney joined the first nationwide Vietnam Moratorium. Students, unionists, churchgoers and ordinary families gathered at the Town Hall and marched through the city, calling for an end to Australia's involvement in the Vietnam War. Crowd estimates ran from about 25,000 to more than 40,000, part of roughly 200,000 who turned out across the country.

The mood was sharpened by the Kent State shootings in the United States only days before, when National Guardsmen killed four student protesters. In Sydney the march was largely peaceful, a sit-down crowd that surprised even its critics with its size and calm.

Once dismissed as the work of radicals, the Moratorium helped pull mainstream opinion against the war and against the conscription that fed it. Two more national moratoriums followed in September 1970 and June 1971.
properties.source: National Museum of Australia; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Vietnam Moratorium protesters, 1970

properties.title: The night Sydney's streets were lit
properties.summary: On the night of 24 May 1841 the Australian Gas Light Company switched on Sydney's first gas street lamps, fed from its works on the edge of Darling Harbour.
body: The Australian Gas Light Company was formed in 1837 and charged with lighting the streets of Sydney. It built its first gasworks on the Darling Harbour edge of Millers Point, where the town gas was stored in holder tanks cut from solid sandstone.

On the night of 24 May 1841, Queen Victoria's birthday, the company lit Sydney's first gas street lamps. Within two years some 165 lamps burned across the city, a mix of government, council, publican and private lights that pushed back a little of the colonial dark.

The company became one of Australia's oldest, and its later gasworks at Mortlake fuelled Sydney for generations. The original harbour works now lie buried beneath Barangaroo, with only traces of the site left behind.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; AGL
properties.image_caption: The early gasworks and gasometer at Millers Point

properties.title: The 1879 cricket riot
properties.summary: In February 1879 a disputed run-out at Sydney's Association Ground sent thousands of spectators surging onto the field, in one of cricket's earliest crowd riots.
body: On 8 February 1879, a cricket-mad colonial crowd packed the Association Ground, the future Sydney Cricket Ground, to watch New South Wales take on Lord Harris's touring English XI. With the local second innings only 19 runs old, the Victorian umpire George Coulthard gave the popular Billy Murdoch run out for 10. The crowd erupted. Many were already suspicious of Coulthard, who had been engaged by the Englishmen, and intercolonial feeling against a Victorian official only sharpened the anger.

Around two thousand spectators poured over the fence and seized the wickets. In the scramble Lord Harris was struck with a whip or stick, though he came away unhurt. The other umpire was a young barrister named Edmund Barton, later Australia's first prime minister. Barton held firm, ruling that the run-out was correct and that the English had behaved properly.

Order was eventually restored. After the Sunday rest day, play resumed and the Englishmen won comfortably by an innings, then cancelled the rest of their Sydney fixtures. The riot has lived on as one of the most notorious crowd disturbances in early cricket, a reminder that the line between passion and disorder at a Sydney sporting ground has always been thin.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Billy Murdoch, whose run-out lit the fuse

properties.title: Cumberland Oval & the Eels
properties.summary: Cumberland Oval is where Parramatta's fans burned the grandstand to the ground celebrating the Eels' first premiership in 1981.
body: The Parramatta Eels entered the New South Wales competition in 1947 and made Cumberland Oval their home. For 34 years they chased a premiership without success, a long drought by the standards of Sydney rugby league.

On 27 September 1981 they finally won it, beating the Newtown Jets 20 to 11 in the grand final. That night jubilant fans lit bonfires at the ground, and the flames spread to the old wooden grandstand and burned it to the ground. The club had to play out of Canterbury's ground for the next four seasons.
From the ashes rose Parramatta Stadium, opened in 1986. The same patch of western Sydney turf now holds CommBank Stadium, three grounds in succession on one well-worn piece of football ground.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Rugby League
properties.image_caption: CommBank Stadium, on the site of Cumberland Oval

properties.title: Rookwood Necropolis
properties.summary: Rookwood is the largest cemetery in the Southern Hemisphere, a Victorian necropolis in Sydney's west that was reached for decades by its own funeral railway and reads today as a quiet map of the city's many communities.
body: When Sydney's older burial grounds filled and were closed on health grounds, the colony laid out a vast new cemetery on sandy ground in the west. The first burials came in 1867, and the Necropolis was formally opened under its own Act of Parliament on the first day of 1868.

For more than eighty years the dead travelled here by train. Funeral services left the Gothic Mortuary Station near Central, the work of colonial architect James Barnet, and ran along a dedicated line to ornate receiving stations among the graves. Road hearses gradually took over, and after a wartime revival the last funeral train ran in 1948.

Rookwood grew into one of the largest operating cemeteries in the world, covering nearly 300 hectares with around a million burials. It was laid out in separate sections for different faiths and communities, from Anglican and Catholic to Jewish, Muslim, Chinese and Greek. Walking those grounds today is one way to trace the many peoples who have made Sydney home.
properties.image_caption: The old Chinese section, Rookwood Cemetery
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register

properties.title: Me-mel (Goat Island)
properties.summary: Me-mel is the largest island in Sydney Harbour, home to Bennelong and his family before it became a convict-quarried gunpowder store and shipyard. In 2022 the NSW Government announced its return to Aboriginal ownership.
body: To the Eora the island was Me-mel, recorded by the colonists as meaning the eye. It sits in Wangal Country, and it mattered deeply. The Wangal man Bennelong told the British, in words written down by the judge-advocate David Collins, that the island was his own. It had been his father's before him, and his father had been born there.

From 1833 the colonial government put convicts from the hulk Phoenix to work quarrying the island's sandstone. They cleared ground for a gunpowder magazine, the Queen's Magazine, completed by early 1839 to hold the colony's explosives at a safe distance from the town. Officers' barracks, a blacksmith's shop and a stone perimeter wall followed soon after.

In the twentieth century Me-mel became a working base on the harbour. A shipyard with slipways was built into the south-western corner in the 1920s, and the island also served as a depot and fire station for the harbour authorities.

In May 2022 the New South Wales Government announced that Me-mel would be returned to Aboriginal ownership, with funds set aside to repair seawalls and buildings and to clear contamination before the transfer.
properties.image_caption: Goat Island (Me-mel) in Port Jackson
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW National Parks & Wildlife Service

properties.title: The Clontarf Attack
properties.summary: At a harbourside charity picnic in 1868, a gunman shot Queen Victoria's son in the back. It was Australia's first attempted political assassination, and it set off a wave of anti-Irish-Catholic panic.
body: On 12 March 1868, Prince Alfred, Duke of Edinburgh and second son of Queen Victoria, attended a fundraising picnic for the Sydney Sailors' Home at the Clontarf pleasure grounds on Middle Harbour. As he stood among the crowd, Henry James O'Farrell stepped forward and shot him in the back at close range.

The bullet struck a rib, glanced off, and left only a slight wound. The Prince recovered within weeks and sailed for England in April. O'Farrell had a documented history of mental illness, worsened by heavy drinking, and his claims of a wider conspiracy were largely discounted. A plea of insanity failed, and he was convicted of wounding with intent to murder. He was hanged at Darlinghurst Gaol on 21 April 1868, just forty days after the shooting.

The attack unleashed a wave of loyalist and anti-Irish-Catholic feeling, and a Treason Felony Act was rushed through the colonial parliament within days. Something more lasting also came of it. Money raised by public subscription to mark the Prince's recovery helped found the Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, which opened in September 1882 and gained its 'Royal' prefix in 1903.
properties.image_caption: An engraving of the attempt on Prince Alfred at Clontarf, 1868
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.title: The Lapérouse Monument
properties.summary: On the Botany Bay headland a stone column honours the French navigator Lapérouse, whose two ships sailed into the bay in late January 1788, just behind the First Fleet, before vanishing into the Pacific.
body: As the First Fleet was shifting from Botany Bay north to Port Jackson in late January 1788, two more ships appeared off the coast. They were the Boussole and the Astrolabe, under the French explorer Jean-François de Galaup, comte de Lapérouse. The French camped on the northern shore of Botany Bay for about six weeks, on cordial terms with the British, before sailing away on 10 March 1788.

They were never seen again. Both ships were wrecked at Vanikoro in the south-west Pacific, and the fate of the expedition stayed a mystery for some forty years until the wreck site was found in the late 1820s. The chaplain and naturalist Louis Receveur, who died during the stay on 17 February 1788, was buried near the shore. His grave, the oldest known European grave in Australia, is marked close to the headland.

In 1825 the visiting French commander Hyacinthe de Bougainville paid for a tall stone column to Lapérouse's memory, designed by the colonial architect George Cookney. It still looks out over the bay where the two great voyages of 1788 briefly crossed.
properties.image_caption: The Lapérouse Monument on the Botany Bay headland
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Randwick City Council

properties.title: Taronga Zoo
properties.summary: Sydney's great harbourside zoo opened on the slopes above Bradleys Head in October 1916, after the city's animals were ferried across from a cramped earlier zoo at Moore Park.
body: Sydney's first public zoo had opened in the 1880s at Moore Park, but by the 1910s it was crowded and unfit for purpose. A new site was granted on the harbour's north shore, on Cammeraygal land at Mosman. The name Taronga is widely said to mean something like water view or beautiful view in an Aboriginal language, though that gloss rests on uncertain sources.

The move across the harbour became part of Sydney legend. In the early hours of 24 September 1916 the elephant Jessie was walked through the eastern suburbs streets to a vehicle ferry at Bennelong Point and carried across to her new home. Taronga Zoological Park was officially opened a fortnight later, on 7 October 1916.

Reached for generations by ferry and later by an aerial cable car, with sweeping views back to the city, Taronga grew into one of Sydney's best-loved institutions. It is now a centre for wildlife conservation and breeding programs.
properties.image_caption: The entrance to Taronga Zoo, Mosman
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Taronga Conservation Society

properties.title: Sydney's First Aerodrome
properties.summary: Sydney Airport began on the market-garden flats at Mascot, where the returned wartime pilot Nigel Love laid out the city's first proper aerodrome around 1919 and 1920. It was later named for Sir Charles Kingsford Smith.
body: In 1919 Nigel Love leased open ground beside Cooks River at Mascot from the Kensington racing club and set out Sydney's first dedicated aerodrome. He made the first flight from the field in an Avro in November that year, and the aerodrome had its official opening flight on 9 January 1920. The flat, well-drained land near the bay suited the fragile aircraft of the day.

The Commonwealth Government soon took the site in hand, buying part of it in 1921 and compulsorily acquiring the rest in 1923 when Love's lease expired. Mascot became Sydney's principal airport and grew busy with commercial traffic through the 1920s.

On 14 August 1936 the airport was renamed in honour of Sir Charles Kingsford Smith, the aviator whose Southern Cross had made the first trans-Pacific flight to Australia. For decades many Sydneysiders kept calling it Mascot. From these beginnings it became the nation's busiest airport.
properties.image_caption: Royal Aero Club members at Mascot Aerodrome
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.title: Dixon Street Chinatown
properties.summary: The pedestrian run of Dixon Street in Haymarket is the heart of Sydney's Chinatown, the third home of a Chinese community whose Sydney roots reach back to the gold rushes.
body: Chinese migrants came to New South Wales in numbers during the gold rushes of the 1850s, and their first Sydney quarter grew around the lower end of George Street in The Rocks. Over the following decades the community shifted south, to the Campbell and Goulburn Street area on the Surry Hills fringe by the 1870s. As slum clearances and the move of the city markets reshaped the district, it settled into Haymarket in the early twentieth century.

Dixon Street became Chinatown's main street, lined with restaurants, grocers, clan associations and temples. The city council set up the Dixon Street Chinese Committee in 1971 to revive a fading precinct. In 1979 the council closed the street to traffic to form a pedestrian mall, and the ceremonial paifang gateways, designed by the architect Henry Tsang, were raised at each end and finished in 1980.
Dixon Street remains the symbolic centre of Chinese Sydney. The surrounding Haymarket has since become home to many later migrant communities, who have made the streets around it their own.
properties.image_caption: The southern ceremonial gate at the Dixon Street entrance, Haymarket
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney

properties.title: The First Mardi Gras
properties.summary: On a winter night in 1978 a festive march down Oxford Street ended in police violence and dozens of arrests. In defiance, it became the beginning of the Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras.
body: On the night of 24 June 1978 the Gay Solidarity Group called a Mardi Gras, a late-night street party and march to round off a day of protest for gay rights. Several hundred people set off from Oxford Street near Taylor Square behind a truck carrying a sound system.

Police seized the truck, and as the crowd moved on towards Kings Cross they blocked the street and waded in. Fifty-three people were arrested, and many were beaten at Darlinghurst Police Station. Days later the Sydney Morning Herald printed the names, addresses and occupations of those charged, and some lost their jobs as a result.

Those who marched that night became known as the 78ers. Far from being crushed, the event returned the following year and grew into the annual Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras. In 2016 the Herald formally apologised for publishing the names, and the New South Wales Parliament expressed its regret for the events of 1978.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Museum of Australia; 78ers.org.au
properties.image_caption: Sydney's Mardi Gras today, the parade that grew from the 1978 march (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 2.0)

properties.title: Garden Island
properties.summary: This harbour island grew the infant colony's vegetables in 1788 and became the navy's great Sydney base. On the night of 31 May 1942, Japanese midget submarines slipped into the harbour and sank the depot ship HMAS Kuttabul here.
body: Garden Island earned its name in the first months of the colony. In February 1788 Governor Phillip handed the island to the crew of HMS Sirius, who cleared the ground and planted vegetables to fend off scurvy. Three of those sailors cut their initials and the date into a rock on the northern hill, and the carving 'FM 1788', thought to be the work of Sirius steward Frederick Meredith, is still there. It is among the oldest pieces of colonial graffiti in the country.

The island later became the Royal Australian Navy's main Sydney base. During the Second World War a large reclamation joined it to the shore at Potts Point, and the great Captain Cook Graving Dock opened in March 1945. On the night of 31 May 1942 three Japanese midget submarines penetrated the harbour. One fired its torpedoes at the cruiser USS Chicago and missed, but a torpedo ran on and exploded near the moored depot ship HMAS Kuttabul, a converted ferry. Twenty-one sailors, nineteen Australian and two British, were killed in their bunks.

Garden Island is still the navy's Fleet Base East, a working dockyard in the middle of the harbour. The Kuttabul memorial stands close to the dock, and the shore base was commissioned as HMAS Kuttabul in 1943 to keep the ferry's name and its lost men in memory.
properties.image_caption: HMAS Kuttabul, sunk at Garden Island, 1942
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Royal Australian Navy Sea Power Centre

properties.title: The Wreck of the Dunbar
properties.summary: On a wild night in 1857 the clipper Dunbar was driven onto the cliffs below South Head, almost within sight of home. Of the 122 aboard, only one survived, making it Sydney's deadliest shipwreck.
body: The Dunbar was coming home from England crowded with passengers, many of them Sydney colonists returning to the city. On the stormy night of 20 August 1857 her captain misjudged the harbour entrance in driving rain. Steering for what he took to be the gap into Port Jackson, he ran her onto the rocks below South Head, near the spot now called The Gap at Watsons Bay.

The ship broke up almost at once. Of the 122 people aboard, 121 were lost. The single survivor was a young crewman, James Johnson, flung onto a rock ledge where he clung for about thirty-six hours until he was spotted and hauled to safety on 22 August.

The city was stunned. An estimated twenty thousand people lined George Street for the funeral procession, and bodies and wreckage washed about the harbour for days. The disaster, followed by the loss of the Catherine Adamson nine weeks later, prompted the building of the Hornby Lighthouse at South Head. In a strange epilogue, Johnson became a lighthouse keeper at Newcastle and in 1866 helped rescue the sole survivor of another wreck, the paddle steamer Cawarra.
properties.image_caption: The clipper Dunbar
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.title: Salt Pan Creek
properties.summary: On the Georges River in Sydney's south, an independent Aboriginal community took root in the 1920s. It lay beyond the reach of the Protection Board and became an early seedbed of the fight for Aboriginal rights.
body: Salt Pan Creek, near today's Riverwood, became something rare in early twentieth-century New South Wales. Land here had been bought outright by the Dharawal woman Ellen Anderson and her family, which meant the camp lay largely beyond the control of the Aborigines Protection Board, whose officials could otherwise remove people and their children at will.

Through the 1920s and into the 1930s the camp grew into a haven for families dispossessed from missions and reserves, and a meeting place for a new kind of Aboriginal politics. In the winter of 1933 Ellen's son Joe Anderson, who took the title King Burraga after his grandfather, was filmed here by Cinesound. Standing among the trees, he demanded justice and a fair hearing for his people. It is thought to be the first appeal for Aboriginal equality recorded on film in Australia.

Figures connected to the camp, among them the activist Pearl Gibbs, helped carry these ideas into the wider movement that produced the 1938 Day of Mourning. The houses are long gone, but Salt Pan Creek remains a place of deep significance in the history of Aboriginal Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; AIATSIS

properties.title: Duke Kahanamoku at Freshwater
properties.summary: In the summer of 1914-15 the Hawaiian Olympic champion Duke Kahanamoku rode a wooden board across the waves at Freshwater Beach. The demonstration helped turn surfing into an Australian passion.
body: Duke Kahanamoku came to Australia in the summer of 1914-15 as the world's fastest swimmer, a Hawaiian who had won Olympic gold at Stockholm in 1912. Invited to give exhibitions, he shaped a board from local sugar pine and, at Freshwater Beach near Manly on 24 December 1914, rode it standing up before a delighted crowd and the press.

Australians already swam and bodysurfed, and a few had ridden boards before. What Duke brought was the grace of Hawaiian stand-up surfing. A young local, Isabel Letham, was famously taken out to ride tandem with him, though researchers now dispute the exact day and place. The ride may have happened slightly later, at Dee Why, rather than at Freshwater as the popular story holds.

His visit caught the imagination of a beach-loving city, and surfboard riding spread quickly along the Sydney coast. A bronze statue of the Duke, unveiled in 1994, now looks out over the beach where he rode.
properties.image_caption: Duke Kahanamoku, around 1912
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian National Maritime Museum

properties.title: Mort's Dock
properties.summary: Mort's Dock opened at Balmain in 1855 as the first completed dry dock in Australia. The shipbuilding and engineering works that grew around it became a powerhouse of harbour industry and a cradle of organised labour, and the site is now parkland.
body: In 1853 Thomas Sutcliffe Mort and Captain Thomas Rowntree formed a company to dig a dry dock at Waterview Bay on the northern side of the Balmain peninsula. By March 1855 it was at work, the first dry dock completed in Australia, ahead even of the Fitzroy Dock being built at Cockatoo Island. Around it grew Mort's Dock and Engineering Company, repairing and building ships and turning out locomotives and heavy machinery.

For a century the dock was one of the harbour's great workplaces. It became the largest private employer in the colony and a stronghold of the union movement, and the Ship Painters and Dockers Union was formed here in 1872. During the Second World War the yard built corvettes for the navy.

The company closed at the end of the 1950s and the dock was eventually filled in. The site is now Mort Bay Park, where stone walls and a great iron caisson survive as reminders of the industry that once roared along this shore.
properties.image_caption: Mort's Dock & Engineering Works, Waterview Bay, c.1889
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Engineering Heritage Australia

properties.title: The Blacktown Native Institution
properties.summary: On Richmond Road near Blacktown stood a colonial boarding school where Aboriginal children, often drawn away from their families, were sent to be 'civilised'. The site is now recognised as a forerunner of the Stolen Generations.
body: Governor Macquarie founded the Native Institution at Parramatta in December 1814, a boarding school meant to school and 'civilise' Aboriginal children. In practice it drew children away from their families and communities, and it is now understood as an early chapter in the long history of removal that became the Stolen Generations.

In 1823 the institution moved to land at Black Town on Richmond Road, beside a grant Macquarie had made to the Darug men Colebee and Nurragingy. Among its earliest students was the Darug girl Maria Lock, daughter of Yarramundi of the Boorooberongal clan. She outshone more than a hundred children at the school examinations in 1819, and later fought to hold land in the district.

The school was effectively abandoned by the end of the 1820s, with the last children sent away by 1833. No buildings remain, but the site on Richmond Road is heritage-listed and held as a place of deep significance, and sorrow, in the history of Aboriginal Sydney.
properties.image_caption: The Blacktown Native Institution site, near the corner of Richmond and Rooty Hill Roads
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register

properties.title: The Parramatta Observatory
properties.summary: In 1822 Governor Brisbane built an observatory in the grounds of Government House at Parramatta. It was the first serious astronomical observatory in the colony, and from it the southern skies were catalogued for the first time.
body: Sir Thomas Brisbane arrived as governor in 1821 a keen astronomer, bringing fine instruments and two skilled assistants, Carl Rümker and James Dunlop. In 1822 he had a small twin-domed observatory built beside Government House at Parramatta.

From here the southern heavens were charted as never before. On 2 June 1822 Rümker recovered Encke's Comet just where its predicted orbit said it should be, a striking confirmation of Newton's theory of gravitation applied to comets. The team went on to gather the observations behind the Parramatta Catalogue of more than seven thousand southern stars, published in 1835.

The observatory closed in 1848 and the decaying building was soon pulled down. Its stone transit markers survive in Parramatta Park, with an obelisk raised in 1880 to mark where the great telescope once stood.
properties.image_caption: The 1880 obelisk marking the Parramatta Observatory site
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Royal Society of NSW

properties.title: The Sydney Stadium
properties.summary: The 'Old Tin Shed' at Rushcutters Bay, built in 1908, was where Jack Johnson became the first Black world heavyweight champion. Half a century later, the Beatles played there to screaming crowds.
body: The promoter Hugh D. McIntosh threw up a great open-air arena of corrugated iron at Rushcutters Bay in 1908. On Boxing Day that year, before some twenty thousand spectators, the American Jack Johnson battered the Canadian champion Tommy Burns until police stopped the fight, and Johnson became the first Black world heavyweight champion.

Roofed over in 1912 and nicknamed the 'Old Tin Shed', the Sydney Stadium became the city's temple of boxing and wrestling, ringing with the crowds of the Les Darcy era and after. From the 1950s it doubled as a concert hall, and in June 1964 the Beatles played six shows there over three nights to delirious fans.
The stadium was pulled down in 1970 to make way for the Eastern Suburbs railway. A plaque near the old site at Rushcutters Bay now marks where it stood.
properties.image_caption: The Burns–Johnson title fight at the new Sydney Stadium, 26 December 1908
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.title: The Yellow House
properties.summary: For a few giddy years from 1970, a terrace at 57-59 Macleay Street, Potts Point, was turned into a living artwork, with every wall painted and every room an installation, in homage to Van Gogh's dream of a house for artists.
body: Martin Sharp came home to Sydney in 1969, fresh from London and the psychedelic poster scene, and found the local gallery world dull. He approached Thelma Clune, whose son Terry had run the Terry Clune Galleries in the front rooms of the family home at Macleay Street before moving to Macquarie Street in 1967. The space stood empty, and Sharp moved in during 1970.

With the filmmaker Albie Thoms and a shifting cast of collaborators, Sharp turned the building itself into the artwork. Almost every surface was painted with images borrowed from Van Gogh and the Surrealists, and the rooms became immersive installations, among them George Gittoes's psychedelic Puppet Theatre. The young Brett Whiteley contributed, and films and performances ran through the place. From 1971 the collective took the name the Yellow House, after the Arles house where Van Gogh hoped artists might live and work together.

The Yellow House ran almost around the clock until early 1973, when Sharp moved on and it closed. It lasted barely three years, yet it is remembered as one of the boldest experiments in Australian art.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Art Gallery of NSW

properties.title: The Villawood Migrant Hostel
properties.summary: From 1949 this site in Sydney's south-west was one of the great reception hostels of postwar migration, where tens of thousands of New Australians first lived in army huts as they began life in a new country.
body: As Australia threw open its doors to mass migration after the Second World War, a former munitions factory at Villawood was turned into a migrant hostel. It opened on 29 December 1949, run by the non-profit Commonwealth Hostels Ltd. New arrivals from Britain and across Europe, and later refugees and displaced people, were housed here in rows of corrugated-iron Nissen huts while they looked for work and a home of their own.

For families stepping off ships into an unfamiliar country, the hostel was a first Australian address. It was crowded and basic, but it was a beginning. By the mid-1960s the place held more than fourteen hundred people at a time, and in 1968 the old Nissen huts were replaced with multi-storey accommodation blocks.

The hostel's migrant-accommodation role wound down by the mid-1980s. In 1976 a secure section had been opened on part of the site for people awaiting deportation, and that section continues today as the Villawood immigration detention centre, a very different chapter on the same ground.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW Migration Heritage Centre

properties.title: Henson Park
properties.summary: A much-loved sporting ground at Marrickville, shaped out of an old brick pit and opened in 1933. It has been home to the Newtown rugby league club since 1937.
body: For decades a brick company dug clay from a great pit at Marrickville. When the works closed and the pit fell idle, Marrickville Council bought the land and reshaped the hollow into a sporting ground. It opened on 2 September 1933 with a cricket match between a local Marrickville side and a North Sydney side that featured Don Bradman, who took several wickets in the contest.

The ground took its name from William Henson, a former mayor of Marrickville, and it honours his son Alfred as well. From 1937 it became the home of the Newtown rugby league club, the Bluebags who later became the Jets. Its banked cycling track was built for the 1938 Empire Games.
With its 1930s grandstand looking down into the old pit, Henson Park remains one of Sydney's best-loved suburban grounds. It still rings with football crowds on a winter Saturday.
properties.image_caption: Henson Park, Marrickville
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Inner West Council

properties.title: Clyde Engineering
properties.summary: The great engineering works between Clyde and Granville stations in Sydney's west, which from 1898 built steam locomotives, railway carriages and, in the diesel age, the famous Clyde-GM locomotives. It employed generations of western-suburbs workers.
body: On the Duck River flats between Clyde and Granville stations stood one of Australia's largest engineering works. It was founded in 1898 on the site of the earlier Hudson Brothers works, after a syndicate bought the bankrupt firm's Granville factory. Clyde won its first government steam-locomotive contract in 1907 and turned out locomotives, carriages and machinery for decades, building more than 500 steam engines in all.

During the Second World War the works made munitions and repaired military aircraft. From 1950 Clyde became the Australian builder of General Motors diesel-electric locomotives. Its Clyde-GM engines, among them the streamlined 42 class of the mid-1950s, hauled trains across the continent.

A huge employer through the twentieth century, the firm was taken over by Evans Deakin in 1996. The works are gone, but the locomotives Clyde built still run on heritage railways, and Clyde remains a place-name on the Sydney rail map.
properties.image_caption: The 200th steam locomotive built by Clyde Engineering
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Powerhouse Museum

properties.title: Longbottom & the Canadian Exiles
properties.summary: A convict stockade on the Parramatta Road at Concord, best remembered as the place of exile for 58 French-Canadian rebels transported after the 1838 uprisings in Lower Canada. Their story survives in the names Canada Bay and Exile Bay.
body: Longbottom, on the road between Sydney and Parramatta, served from the early years of the colony as a government farm and a half-way stockade where convict road gangs were held. Its most remarkable inmates arrived in 1840.

After the failed rebellions of 1837-38 in Lower Canada, fifty-eight French-speaking Patriotes were transported to New South Wales aboard the Buffalo. They reached Port Jackson late in February 1840 and were marched to Longbottom Stockade in March. For about two years they broke stone for the Parramatta Road. They were released on ticket-of-leave from late 1842 and pardoned by Queen Victoria in 1844, after which most made their way home to Canada.

Their exile is written into the map of the district. Canada Bay, Exile Bay and France Bay all recall the Patriotes. A monument to them in Bayview Park, on the foreshore, was unveiled in 1970 by the Canadian Prime Minister, Pierre Trudeau.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Canada Bay

properties.title: De Groot & the Harbour Bridge Ribbon
properties.summary: At the opening of the Sydney Harbour Bridge on 19 March 1932, Francis de Groot of the right-wing New Guard rode his horse forward and cut the ribbon with his sword before Premier Jack Lang could do it. It remains one of the strangest moments in Australian political history.
body: On 19 March 1932 a crowd of about 300,000 people gathered at the southern approach to watch Premier Jack Lang open the new Sydney Harbour Bridge.
