Among the mounted escort of the New South Wales Lancers sat Captain Francis de Groot, an Irish-born former army officer who had no place in the official party. De Groot belonged to the New Guard, a militant anti-Labor and pro-Empire organisation led by Eric Campbell that despised Lang and everything he stood for. Campbell himself had lent De Groot the horse.

As Lang stepped forward to cut the ribbon, De Groot spurred his mount ahead of the dignitaries, drew his ceremonial sword and struck the ribbon, declaring the bridge open in the name of the decent and respectable citizens of New South Wales. In the confusion some witnesses said his rearing horse helped break the ribbon, but the gesture was unmistakable. The official opening had been hijacked in front of the largest crowd Sydney had ever seen.

De Groot was hauled from his horse, arrested and taken to the reception house at Darlinghurst, where he was charged with being insane and not under proper care. Doctors examined him the same day and found him perfectly sane. The ribbon was retied and Lang cut it as planned, and the ceremony went on.

De Groot was later convicted of offensive behaviour and fined the maximum 5 pounds with 4 pounds in costs. He then sued the police for wrongful arrest and settled out of court for 69 pounds 1 shilling and 9 pence. The episode was part farce and part symptom of the bitter politics of the Depression, and Sydney has never quite forgotten it.
properties.image_caption: The arrest of Francis de Groot at the bridge opening, 1932
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.title: Scheyville
properties.summary: A site on Sydney's north-western fringe that served in turn as a training farm for young British migrants, a wartime army camp, a postwar migrant hostel and an officer-training school for Vietnam-era conscripts. Its paddocks hold a layered record of a century of Australian life.
body: Out beyond Windsor, the Scheyville site began in 1893 as the Pitt Town Co-operative Labour Settlement, a depression-era scheme to put unemployed men on the land. A government training farm for city lads followed in 1905, and from 1911 the place trained teenaged British Dreadnought boys, assisted migrants sent out to learn farming before being settled on the land. The site took its name from William Schey, the colony's director of labour.

During the Second World War it was an army camp. From 1949 to 1964 it was a Commonwealth migrant reception centre, where newly arrived European families and displaced persons lived while they found their feet in a new country. Then, from 1965 to 1973, it became the Officer Training Unit Scheyville, which turned national-service conscripts into army officers for the Vietnam era.
The huts and parade grounds survive within Scheyville National Park, a quiet place that holds, in one paddock, the stories of migration, war and the making of modern Australia.
properties.image_caption: A surviving hut at Scheyville National Park
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Kissing Point
properties.summary: Kissing Point at Ryde was one of the colony's first farming reaches on the Parramatta River. It is also where the Wangal leader Bennelong, and later the young Nanbaree, were buried in a riverside orchard.
body: Kissing Point, on the river at today's Putney and Ryde, was one of the first farming districts of the colony. Land was granted here to emancipated convicts from 1792. Among the settlers was the former convict James Squire, who planted an orchard and the colony's first successful hops, and who is remembered as Australia's first commercial brewer.

It was in Squire's orchard that the Wangal man Bennelong was buried after his death on 3 January 1813. Bennelong had been a diplomat and traveller who moved between the world of the Eora and the world of the newcomers. His wife Boorong, a Burramattagal woman who had survived the smallpox of 1789, was buried with him. Eight years later the young man Nanbaree, another survivor of that epidemic, asked to be laid in the same grave, and Squire honoured the request when Nanbaree died in August 1821.

The exact spot was lost for generations. In 2011 the researcher Peter Mitchell identified it beneath a quiet suburban block at Putney, and the NSW Government later bought the property so the place could be cared for. Kissing Point Park, on the foreshore, keeps the old name and looks out over the river they all knew.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Ryde
properties.image_caption: Kissing Point on the Parramatta River, once James Squire's estate (National Library of Australia)

properties.title: Scotland Island
properties.summary: In 1810 Governor Macquarie granted this Pittwater island to the emancipist Andrew Thompson, who built a salt works and a shipyard here. It was the first land grant on Pittwater, and today it is a car-free community reached only by boat.
body: Governor Macquarie granted the island to Andrew Thompson on the first day of 1810. Thompson was a Scottish-born former convict who had risen to become chief constable and the colony's leading emancipist man of business, and the grant rewarded his daring rescues during the Hawkesbury floods. It was the first land grant on Pittwater. Thompson, who almost certainly named the place for his homeland, set up a salt works that evaporated seawater and a yard for building boats.

A coastal trading vessel, the Geordy, was launched from the island in November 1810, only weeks after Thompson died. Old stone walls near the jetty on the northern side are thought to be remnants of his salt and shipbuilding works. The island was subdivided from 1906, though few people lived there permanently until the 1960s.

Scotland Island is now one of Sydney's most unusual suburbs. Several hundred homes climb its steep, bush-covered slopes, there are no roads and no cars, and the only way across is the Church Point ferry or your own boat.
properties.image_caption: Scotland Island, Pittwater
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Northern Beaches Council

properties.title: RAAF Base Richmond
properties.summary: This air force base sits on the Hawkesbury flats at Richmond, where military flying began in 1916. It became a Royal Australian Air Force station in 1925, making it the oldest airbase in New South Wales, and it is still flying today.
body: Military flying began on the Ham Common at Richmond in August 1916, when the New South Wales government set up a flying school there as part of the First World War effort. In June 1925 the site became a station of the young Royal Australian Air Force, when the first aircraft of No. 3 Squadron arrived from Point Cook in Victoria. That made Richmond the first RAAF base in New South Wales and, after Point Cook, the second in the country.

Through the Second World War Richmond was a major base. It sent squadrons north, trained aircrew and ran its own air force hospital. Set on the broad Hawkesbury flats in Sydney's far north-west, it has stayed an active air force base ever since.
Today it is home to the heavy-lift transport squadrons whose C-130 Hercules aircraft fly relief and military missions across the region. It is a working airfield that has now served for a century.
properties.image_caption: A C-130J Hercules at RAAF Base Richmond
properties.source: Royal Australian Air Force; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Bankstown Airport
properties.summary: Opened in 1940 in Sydney's south-west, this airfield became a wartime fighter base flown by American and Australian squadrons. It grew into the busiest general-aviation airport in New South Wales.
body: Bankstown Airport was carved out of the south-western suburbs in 1940, soon after the outbreak of the Second World War. From 1942 American pursuit squadrons of the United States Army Air Forces flew P-39 Airacobras and P-40s from its runways. Australian units serviced aircraft here too, and de Havilland built Mosquito fighter-bombers in a factory on the site.

After the war the airfield passed back to civilian use and became the home of light aviation in Sydney. Flying schools, charter operators, aerial ambulances and private planes all worked out of Bankstown.

Today it is the premier general-aviation airport in New South Wales and one of the busiest airfields in the country by aircraft movements. Its circuit is rarely quiet, with training flights coming and going all day.
properties.image_caption: Bankstown Airport
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Bankstown Airport

properties.title: The Homebush Abattoir
properties.summary: For most of the twentieth century the meat that fed Sydney was slaughtered at the great State Abattoir on Homebush Bay. The site was later cleaned up and transformed into the 2000 Olympic Park.
body: The government opened a vast new State Abattoir on the flats of Homebush Bay in 1915, replacing the notoriously filthy works at Glebe Island. Bungled construction meant stock was not actually processed there until about a year later. With its saleyards it grew into one of the largest industrial enterprises in the state, employing around 1,600 people at its peak and slaughtering more than 20,000 animals a day.
The abattoir closed in 1988 and left behind heavily contaminated ground. Through the 1990s the site was remediated in one of the largest environmental clean-ups the country had seen.

On that reclaimed land rose Sydney Olympic Park, the centrepiece of the 2000 Olympic Games. A handful of the old abattoir buildings survive among the stadiums as a reminder of what the place once was.
properties.image_caption: Surviving buildings of the State Abattoir, Sydney Olympic Park
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Olympic Park Authority

properties.title: St John's Cemetery
properties.summary: Laid out at Parramatta in 1790, St John's is the oldest surviving European cemetery in Australia, holding First Fleeters and many of the people who made and suffered in the early colony.
body: As the young colony pushed inland to farm at Parramatta, it set aside a burial ground there in 1790. The first to be buried was a convicts' child, James Magee, on 31 January that year. The oldest marked grave still standing in Australia belongs to Henry Dodd, Governor Phillip's gardener, who was laid to rest in January 1791. His headstone has held its place for more than two centuries.

Inside the high wall of convict-made bricks, begun in the mid-1820s under Governor Brisbane, lie people who shaped the colony and others who simply endured it. The chaplain and magistrate Samuel Marsden is here, along with the surgeon and landholder D'Arcy Wentworth, members of the Blaxland family, and the colonial botanist Charles Fraser. More than fifty First Fleeters rest here too, many of them in unmarked ground.

Still in use and carefully tended, St John's reads like a quiet archive in stone. The names cut into the sandstone trace the beginnings of colonial New South Wales, the strivers and the forgotten alike.
properties.image_caption: St John's Cemetery, Parramatta
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; St John's Cemetery Project

properties.title: Toongabbie Government Farm
properties.summary: Toongabbie was one of the colony's earliest inland settlements, a government farm cleared by convict gangs west of Parramatta in 1791-1792. Worked under harsh discipline and short rations, it became a byword for the cruelty of the early penal years.
body: In 1791 the struggling colony was desperate to feed itself, so it pushed west of Parramatta onto the creek flats of Darug Country and began clearing a great government farm. The local clan knew the place as Tugagal, and the name Toongabbie comes from a Darug word usually understood as the meeting of the waters. Gangs of convicts, many of them newly landed and half starved from the Third Fleet, felled and burned roughly 300 acres in about a month. By 1792 Toongabbie was the principal farm of the colony, its fields sown with wheat, maize and barley.

The work was brutal. Convicts laboured in the sun with poor tools and little food, driven hard by superintendent Thomas Daveney, who ran the place from 1791 to 1795. Newly arrived prisoners were sent here even when they were sick and worn out. Toongabbie earned a fearsome name, and later campaigners against transportation invoked it as a byword for slavery and famine. Some historians believe the worst stories grew in the retelling, but few doubt the early years were grim.

The farm declined once Governor Phillip left the colony. The soil wore out, and by 1801 Governor King was already clearing fresh ground at Castle Hill to take its place. Cropping at Toongabbie wound down through the early 1800s.
Today the old fields survive as a reserve near Winston Hills, a heritage-listed patch of ground where the colony first tried in earnest to grow its own bread.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register

properties.title: The Balmain Colliery
properties.summary: On the Birchgrove peninsula, two enormous shafts were sunk from 1897 to mine coal from far beneath Sydney Harbour. They reached the deepest workings of any coal mine ever dug in Australia.
body: In 1897 the Sydney Harbour Collieries company began sinking two great brick-lined shafts at Birchgrove, in the heart of residential Balmain, to reach a coal seam thought to lie deep beneath the harbour. They named them the Birthday and Jubilee shafts, after Queen Victoria's birthday and her 1897 Diamond Jubilee. Each was about five metres across and lined with more than four million bricks.

The sinking was slow and deadly. In March 1900 six men were being lowered down the Birthday Shaft in a bucket when it struck an obstruction and tipped, and five of them fell to their deaths far below. The shaft was finished only in late 1902, at a depth of around 800 metres, making it the deepest coal mine ever sunk in Australia. The coal proved poor and the workings were gassy.

Coal came out in fits and starts, from 1897 to 1915 and again from 1924 until the company collapsed in 1931. From 1937 the site was used to draw off and sell natural gas, which carried on until about 1950. The shafts were then sealed and the land built over. Quiet streets of houses now cover the deepest mine the country ever sank.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Powerhouse Museum
properties.image_caption: The Balmain colliery head (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: The Devonshire Street Cemetery
properties.summary: Sydney's main nineteenth-century burial ground, the 'Sandhills', lay where Central Station now stands. The dead were exhumed from 1901 to clear the way for the railway.
body: Governor Macquarie ordered the consecration of the Devonshire Street Cemetery in 1820, on sandy ground near Brickfield Hill. Sydneysiders knew it as the Sandhills. It became the town's principal burial ground, with separate sections for the colony's many denominations, and over the decades many thousands were laid to rest there. By the 1860s it was full, and it closed to new burials in 1867.

In 1901 the government resumed the land to build the new Central railway station. Families were given two months to arrange the removal of their dead. The graves were opened and the remains taken away, many to the purpose-built Bunnerong Cemetery near Botany, others to Rookwood, Waverley and Gore Hill. Bunnerong was later folded into what is now Eastern Suburbs Memorial Park. Central Station opened on the cleared site on 4 August 1906.
Nothing of the cemetery survives at Central. Its memory sits quietly beneath one of the busiest places in the city, a reminder of the older Sydney that lies under the modern one.
properties.image_caption: The old Devonshire Street Cemetery, painted by Julian Ashton, 1894
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW

properties.title: Bunnerong Power Station
properties.summary: A huge coal-fired power station on the shore near Botany Bay at Matraville, opened in 1929 and for a time the largest in the southern hemisphere. It lit Sydney's homes and ran its trams and trains.
body: Through the late 1920s the Sydney Municipal Council built an enormous power station on the shore near Botany Bay at Matraville. The first turbo-alternator started up in January 1929, and over the next few years Bunnerong kept growing. At its peak it reached about 375 megawatts from eleven turbo-alternators, which made it the largest power station in the southern hemisphere.

Its coal-fired turbines supplied much of the state's electricity. They powered homes across Sydney and drove the city's vast electric tram network and its railways. For decades the tall chimneys were a landmark of the southern shore.

Newer and bigger stations eventually overtook it. The old 'A' station was out of use by 1973, and the whole installation was decommissioned in 1975 before the great halls came down through the 1980s. Houses and industry now cover the ground that once electrified Sydney.
properties.image_caption: Bunnerong Power Station, Matraville, 1936
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Powerhouse Museum

properties.title: Newington Armory
properties.summary: A naval armament depot on the Parramatta River, where buildings first went up in 1897 to store explosives well clear of the city. A century later it was swept into the site of the Sydney 2000 Olympics.
body: In 1897 the New South Wales Military Forces began building a magazine on the mudflats of Homebush Bay, putting the colony's explosives safely away from settled streets. The Commonwealth took it over after Federation, and in 1921 the store passed to the Royal Australian Navy. Through both world wars and the Cold War the Newington Armament Depot was the navy's main munitions store in New South Wales. During the Second World War the Australian, American and British navies worked side by side here.

The navy wound down through the 1990s and finally left at the end of 1999, the last ammunition crossing the wharf on 14 December that year. Homebush Bay was already being remade for the Sydney 2000 Olympics, and the southern half of the site became the athletes' village. That ground is now the suburb of Newington.

The northern half survives as the Armory, a heritage precinct of old magazines and a small railway inside the Newington Nature Reserve. These days it hosts arts events and visitors rather than shells and powder.
properties.image_caption: The Newington Armory, Homebush Bay
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Sydney Olympic Park Authority

properties.title: Harold Park
properties.summary: For more than a century this Glebe trotting track was the home of harness racing in Sydney. It opened in 1902 and ran its last race in 2010.
body: A trotting club was formed at Glebe in 1902 on part of the old Toxteth Estate, holding its first meeting that November. The track went through several names before 1929, when it was rechristened Harold Park after Childe Harold, an imported trotter, to clear up confusion with the suburb of Epping.

For generations the trots under lights at Harold Park were a fixture of Sydney sporting life. Big crowds came for events like the Miracle Mile, and the track also hosted greyhound racing. The last harness meeting was run in December 2010, and the site became the Harold Park residential precinct.

Next door stands the grand former Rozelle tram depot, which once housed the trams that carried the crowds to the track. It has been restored as the Tramsheds, holding on to a thread of the area's busy past.
properties.image_caption: Harold Park Paceway, 2009
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia

properties.title: Thompson Square
properties.summary: One of Australia's oldest surviving public squares, laid out at Windsor in 1811 in Governor Macquarie's Hawkesbury town and ringed by Georgian buildings above the river.
body: When Governor Lachlan Macquarie laid out the town of Windsor on the Hawkesbury in 1811, he set aside an open square that slopes down to the river. He named it for Andrew Thompson, an emancipist magistrate and friend who had died the year before. The ground had been the heart of the riverside settlement of Green Hills since the 1790s, but it was Macquarie who gave it form and name, making Thompson Square one of the oldest surviving public squares in the country.

Buildings from the colony's first decades still rim the square, among them the Macquarie Arms Hotel of 1815 and the Georgian Doctor's House. For two centuries it has been the civic heart of Windsor, and it was added to the State Heritage Register in 1999.

In recent years the square and the neighbouring Windsor Bridge were at the centre of a long heritage dispute over the bridge's replacement. Campaigners argued that the works would disturb one of the most intact early-colonial townscapes in Australia.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Warwick Farm Racecourse
properties.summary: A horse-racing track near Liverpool, opened in 1889. For thirteen years it also held a motor-racing circuit that hosted the Australian Grand Prix.
body: Warwick Farm Racecourse opened on the flats beside the Georges River near Liverpool in 1889. The Australian Jockey Club bought the property in 1922 and reopened the rebuilt course in 1925. It remains one of Sydney's principal racing and training tracks, run today by the Australian Turf Club.

Its more surprising life ran from 1960 to 1973, when a motor-racing circuit was laid out within and around the racecourse. The Warwick Farm circuit became one of the country's finest road courses. It hosted the Australian Grand Prix four times, in 1963, 1967, 1970 and 1971, and a round of the international Tasman Series every year from 1964. Drivers including Jim Clark, Jack Brabham and Stirling Moss raced there before the circuit closed in 1973 over the cost of safety upgrades.
The horse track outlived the cars, and the roar of the Tasman Series is now only a memory of the infield.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Australian Turf Club
properties.image_caption: The starting grid at the Warwick Farm motor-racing circuit, 1965

properties.title: Camden Airport
properties.summary: A south-western airfield that grew out of a grazier's private airstrip in the 1930s, was pressed into RAAF service through the Second World War, and still hums with light aircraft today.
body: Camden Airport sits on the Macquarie Grove property near Camden, in the family of the grazier Macarthur-Onslow since 1916. The story really begins in the 1920s, when an Avro biplane landed on a racecourse on the estate during the filming of Silks and Saddles. The spectacle hooked the young Macarthur-Onslow brothers on flying. Edward took over Macquarie Grove in 1934, built a hangar, and in 1937 opened the Macquarie Grove Flying School on a private aerodrome of his own making.

The Commonwealth took over the field during the Second World War, and Macquarie Grove became Camden RAAF station. It hosted the Central Flying School and a string of squadrons, including Nos. 4, 15, 21, 32 and 78, as they trained and flew patrols over the approaches to Sydney. The airfield returned to civil hands after the war.

Camden remains a busy general-aviation airport and one of the oldest continuously operating airfields in the Sydney basin. Light planes and gliders still use ground that a film stunt first turned skyward.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Camden Airport
properties.image_caption: Aerial view of Camden Airport

properties.title: Ingleburn Army Camp
properties.summary: The first purpose-built camp raised for the Second AIF in 1939, where soldiers trained before sailing to war, and later a major national service base.
body: When the Second World War broke out in September 1939, the Army moved onto open farmland at Ingleburn in Sydney's south-west. Tents went up on 8 October, and the first troops of the 16th Brigade arrived in the first week of November. In 1940 the Commonwealth bought 684 acres and built rows of corrugated-iron huts. The early conditions were spartan, with men sleeping in unlined tin sheds, but Ingleburn became the first purpose-built camp to train infantry for the Second Australian Imperial Force.

Through the war it was one of the state's great training and embarkation grounds. The 16th Brigade went on to be the first Australian troops in action at Bardia in 1941, and units bound for the Middle East, Greece and the Pacific passed through. In 1951 the camp was renamed Bardia Barracks after that first battle, and it became a principal centre for National Service trainees until conscription ended in 1972.
The camp wound down through the 1990s and stood empty from late 2000. Part of it survives as the Ingleburn Military Heritage Precinct, while the land around it has been redeveloped for housing.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Ingleburn Military Heritage Precinct
properties.image_caption: National servicemen outside their barracks at Ingleburn

properties.title: Balls Head
properties.summary: A sandstone headland at Waverton, known as Yerroulbine in Cammeraygal country, carved with Aboriginal rock engravings and later half-swallowed by a harbourside coal loader.
body: Balls Head, known to the Cammeraygal as Yerroulbine, is a sandstone headland on the lower North Shore that carries some of harbour Sydney's most striking Aboriginal rock art. The best known carving is a large whale with a human figure near its tail, first recorded in the late 1890s and mapped in 1899. Shell middens and a rock shelter nearby speak of long occupation. The meaning of the whale-and-man motif remains genuinely uncertain. Some read it as a man working magic to bring whales ashore, others as a figure who has entered a stranded whale to be healed.

The headland took its colonial name from Lieutenant Henry Lidgbird Ball of HMS Supply, a First Fleet ship. From the early 1920s its western flank was given over to the Balls Head Coal Loader, where colliers transferred coal until 1992. Some of the smaller engravings recorded in 1899 were lost or buried as the site was industrialised.

In 1926 the rest of the headland was set aside as a public reserve, and in 2007 the old coal loader reopened as a sustainability centre. Work to clean up the site has since uncovered further figures, leaving the engravings and the machinery as two very different marks on the one point of land.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Balls Head Reserve, Waverton

properties.title: Granville Rail Disaster
properties.summary: On 18 January 1977 a crowded commuter train from the Blue Mountains derailed at Granville and brought a road bridge down on its carriages, killing 83 in the worst rail disaster in Australian history.
body: A little after 8.10am on 18 January 1977, a crowded peak-hour train that had left Mount Victoria in the Blue Mountains derailed on a curve about 250 metres west of Granville station. The electric locomotive struck a steel-and-concrete pillar carrying the Bold Street road bridge, and the heavy span dropped onto the third and fourth carriages, both packed with commuters.

Eighty-three people were killed and 213 injured, making it the deadliest rail disaster Australia has known. An unborn child was added to the official toll in 2017. Hundreds of emergency workers and bystanders worked for hours beneath the unstable bridge to reach the trapped and the dying.

The official inquiry placed the blame not on the driver but on the very poor condition of the track, where loose fastenings had let the rails spread under load. The disaster forced a sweeping overhaul of railway safety in New South Wales and a large rise in maintenance spending. The bridge was rebuilt as a single clear span, and a memorial service is held at the site each year for those who never came home.
properties.source: Wikipedia; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Memorial at the Granville rail disaster site

properties.title: Cataract Dam
properties.summary: Cataract Dam, completed in 1907 near Appin in Sydney's far south-west, was the first of the great Upper Nepean storage dams. Its huge sandstone wall helped drought-proof a fast-growing city.
body: Cataract Dam stands across the Cataract River near Appin, at the southern edge of greater Sydney. Built between 1902 and 1907, it was the first of the four big storage dams of the Upper Nepean Scheme, raised to secure water for a city whose earlier sources had proved fragile in drought.

The wall is a work of cyclopean masonry, with huge blocks of locally quarried sandstone, some weighing up to four and a half tonnes, set in cement mortar. It was the largest dam in New South Wales at the time and a notable feat of engineering. Construction finished late in 1907, the dam passed to the Metropolitan Water Board on 10 June 1908, and the reservoir first filled to capacity on 13 January 1911.
With its Federation-era buildings and landscaped grounds, Cataract became a popular outing as well as a working reservoir. It remains part of Sydney's water supply more than a century on.
properties.source: WaterNSW; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Cataract Dam and spillway

properties.title: Freedom Plaza, Cabramatta
properties.summary: Freedom Plaza is the heart of Cabramatta in Sydney's south-west, home to the country's largest Vietnamese community, which grew from the refugees of 1975. The ornate Pai Lau gateway marks its entrance.
body: From 1975, as people fled the end of the Vietnam War and the upheavals across Indochina, many of those who reached Australia were first housed at the Cabramatta migrant hostel and settled nearby. Over the following decades Cabramatta became the centre of the largest Vietnamese community in the country, its streets filled with the food, language and trade of a remade suburb.

The pedestrian mall at its core, Freedom Plaza, is marked by the Pai Lau, an ornate gateway designed by the architect Henry Tsang and opened during Lunar New Year in 1991. Its carved columns carry the words for liberty and democracy in Chinese, Vietnamese, English, Khmer and Lao, honouring the South-East Asian communities who found refuge here.

Cabramatta's path was a hard one at times. It struggled with a heroin trade through the 1980s and 1990s, and the local MP and anti-crime campaigner John Newman was shot dead outside his home here in 1994. The suburb endured to become one of multicultural Sydney's proudest places, thronged each Lunar New Year.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Fairfield City Heritage
properties.image_caption: The Pai Lau gateway at Freedom Plaza, Cabramatta

properties.title: Bradleys Head
properties.summary: Bradleys Head is a fortified Mosman headland that once helped guard the inner harbour. Since 1934 it has held the mast of HMAS Sydney, the cruiser that destroyed the German raider Emden in 1914.
body: Bradleys Head is the wooded point that pushes out into the harbour at Mosman. It was Borogegal country long before it took the name of Lieutenant William Bradley of the First Fleet flagship Sirius. Defences began here in 1840 under Major George Barney, the colony's first engineer, with a stone gun pit and jetty built by convict labour. A bastion followed in 1854, and from about 1870 work began on higher ground on a battery of five circular gun emplacements, linked by tunnels and a firing gallery cut into the sandstone.

The guns were never fired in anger, and the headland's best-known feature is a memorial rather than a weapon. In 1934 the tripod foremast of the cruiser HMAS Sydney was raised here, saved from the ship when it was broken up at Cockatoo Island. In 1914 that cruiser had hunted down and destroyed the German raider SMS Emden off the Cocos Islands, the young navy's first great victory at sea.

Today Bradleys Head sits within Sydney Harbour National Park. The old gun emplacements and the grey steel mast look out across one of the world's great harbours, and a Doric column moved here from the original George Street post office in 1871 still marks one nautical mile from Fort Denison.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The HMAS Sydney memorial mast at Bradleys Head, with the old fort walls

properties.title: Rachel Forster Hospital
properties.summary: A hospital founded by women doctors in 1922 for women and children, which gave them somewhere to practise and lead when honorary hospital posts were closed to women elsewhere.
body: On 3 January 1922 the doctors Lucy Gullett and Harriett Biffin bought a small two-storey terrace at 11 Lansdowne Street, Surry Hills, and opened it as the New Hospital for Women and Children. It was a rare thing, a hospital founded and staffed by women. It grew out of the long struggle of early women physicians, who were shut out of the honorary hospital posts their male colleagues held, and it gave them a place to practise, train and run things themselves.

The little dispensary soon outgrew Surry Hills. In 1924 it moved to a larger house on George Street, Redfern, and on 22 August 1925 Lady Rachel Forster, wife of the Governor-General, opened the new premises. The hospital took her name in honour of her interest in women's welfare, and for decades it served the women and children of the inner city.

A purpose-built hospital opened on Pitt Street, Redfern, in 1941, on the very site of the cottage of William Redfern, the convict surgeon who gave the suburb its name. The hospital closed in September 2000 and its services passed to Royal Prince Alfred. The Pitt Street building was later demolished, but for most of a century the Rachel Forster marked the women who forced their way into Australian medicine.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; The Australian Women's Register
properties.image_caption: A room at the Rachel Forster Hospital for Women, 1944 (State Library of NSW)

properties.title: Lapstone Zig Zag
properties.summary: The Lapstone Zig Zag of 1867 was the first railway switchback built on a main line anywhere in the world, carrying trains up the steep eastern face of the Blue Mountains across the towering Knapsack Viaduct.
body: Getting a railway up the abrupt eastern wall of the Blue Mountains defeated ordinary engineering. John Whitton, engineer-in-chief of the New South Wales railways, answered with a clever switchback. Built between 1863 and 1865 and opened to traffic in 1867, the Lapstone Zig Zag let trains climb the escarpment by reversing back and forth up the legs of a giant Z cut into the hillside. The trick gained height without the expense of long tunnels, and it was the first zig zag built on any main line in the world.

The line crossed Knapsack Gully on a graceful seven-arch sandstone viaduct standing more than thirty metres high. Completed in 1865 and still in place today, the viaduct was the showpiece of the climb. For a quarter of a century the Little Zig Zag was the gateway to the west.

As traffic grew the switchback became a bottleneck. In 1892 it was abandoned for a deviation through Glenbrook. The rails are long gone, but the viaduct and the old earthworks remain, walked now by people tracing one of colonial Australia's boldest pieces of railway engineering.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: The Knapsack Viaduct, completed 1865 for the Lapstone Zig Zag

properties.title: The Sydney Twelve
properties.summary: Twelve members of the radical IWW were jailed in Sydney in 1916 on conspiracy charges, in a wartime case many saw as a political frame-up, and most were freed after a 1920 royal commission.
body: In the tense middle of the First World War, as Australia argued bitterly over conscription, twelve members of the Industrial Workers of the World were arrested in Sydney in September 1916. The IWW, known as the Wobblies, had campaigned hard against the war and against conscription, and the authorities moved to break the union.

The men were charged first with treason-felony and then with conspiracy, the alleged plots ranging from arson to freeing their gaoled comrade Tom Barker to stirring up sedition. Their trial opened in the Central Criminal Court at Darlinghurst on 20 November 1916, and they were convicted in early December, drawing sentences of five to fifteen years of hard labour. The case leaned heavily on the evidence of paid police informants, and many people then and since have regarded it as a political frame-up.

The outcry did not fade. In 1920 a royal commission by Justice Norman Ewing recommended their release, finding that six of the men had not been justly convicted, though he stopped short of declaring the case a deliberate frame-up. Ten of the men walked free that August, and the last two followed in 1921. The Sydney Twelve became a byword for the wartime suppression of political dissent.
properties.source: Museum of Australian Democracy; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Darlinghurst Courthouse, where the Sydney Twelve were tried in 1916

properties.title: Smith's Weekly
properties.summary: Smith's Weekly was the larrikin Sydney paper of 1919 to 1950 that championed the digger and gave Australia some of its finest black-and-white cartoonists.
body: Smith's Weekly was a Sydney newspaper like no other. Founded in 1919 by Sir James Joynton Smith with the editor Claude McKay and the businessman Clyde Packer, it was irreverent, fiercely nationalist, and aimed squarely at the ordinary man and the returned soldier. Its Unofficial History of the AIF helped fix the larrikin digger in the national imagination.

From 1922 the paper was produced from offices in Phillip Street, and it became famous above all for its black-and-white artists. Stan Cross, Cecil Hartt, George Finey and Emile Mercier were masters of the cartoon and the caption. Cross's 1933 drawing of two workmen dangling from a girder, captioned For gorsake, stop laughing, this is serious, is among the most celebrated cartoons ever drawn in Australia.

The paper's humour eventually dated, and the final issue appeared on 28 October 1950. For three decades, though, Smith's Weekly had been both a school of Australian cartooning and a cheeky, distinctive voice of its city.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: A 1925 Smith's Weekly cartoon by Cecil Hartt

properties.title: Sydney Flying Squadron
properties.summary: Mark Foy founded the Sydney Flying Squadron in 1891, turning the spectacular 18-foot skiffs into a harbour sport that drew cheering crowds onto chartered ferries.
body: In 1891 the department-store magnate Mark Foy founded the Sydney Flying Squadron to bring open-boat racing to ordinary people. Breaking with the gentlemanly yacht clubs, he introduced coloured sails, distinguishing marks and prize money, and made the racing a popular spectacle open to sailors of any means.

The stars of the show were the 18-foot skiffs, wildly over-canvassed open boats crewed by as many as a dozen men hiking out hard to keep them upright. Crowds packed the foreshores and chartered ferries to follow the fleet across the harbour, often with bookmakers working the decks.

Still based at Careening Cove below Kirribilli, the Sydney Flying Squadron calls itself Australia's oldest open-boat sailing club. Its Saturday skiff races remain one of Sydney Harbour's great traditions.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: 18-foot skiffs racing on Sydney Harbour

properties.title: Waterfall Train Disaster
properties.summary: On 31 January 2003 a crowded commuter train derailed in a cutting south of Waterfall station, killing seven people. The inquiry that followed laid bare deep flaws in the railway's safety systems.
body: On the morning of 31 January 2003, a four-car Tangara train was running south from Sydney towards Port Kembla. Just south of Waterfall station, at the city's edge near the Royal National Park, it hit a curve in a rock cutting at 117 kilometres an hour. The bend was rated for 60. The train left the rails, rolled over and slammed into the cutting wall. Seven people died, including the driver, and about forty were injured.

A special commission of inquiry led by Peter McInerney found that the driver had most likely suffered a sudden heart attack and been left unconscious at the controls. The train should have stopped itself. Its deadman safety system was meant to halt the train the moment the driver let go, but the inquiry found the pedal could be held down by the dead weight of a collapsed body. For a large share of drivers, their own legs were heavy enough to defeat it.

McInerney's report, released in January 2004, forced major changes to driver health checks and to the safety systems across the New South Wales railways. Trains were fitted with a vigilance system that demands the driver keep acknowledging it, and brakes automatically if no one responds. A memorial near the line remembers those who were killed.
properties.source: Special Commission of Inquiry into the Waterfall Rail Accident (McInerney); Wikipedia

properties.title: Chullora Railway Workshops
properties.summary: The great south-western rail workshops opened in 1926 to maintain and build New South Wales trains. A century on, Chullora is still a working hub of the state's railways.
body: In 1926 the New South Wales Government Railways opened the first of its new workshops at Chullora, in Sydney's south-west, spread across some 200 hectares beside the Enfield marshalling yards. Eveleigh had run out of room, the metropolitan network was being electrified, and the railways needed somewhere far larger to repair and overhaul locomotives and rolling stock. Chullora became that place.

The site grew with the system it served. During the Second World War the workshops turned their hands to assembling aircraft and tanks. In the late 1940s and 1950s they built new trains of their own, including the 600 and 620 class diesel railcars that worked country and branch lines. The 'Elcar' electric-carriage works looked after Sydney's suburban fleet until it closed in March 1994, and from October 1984 the Sydney Freight Terminal took over the goods traffic once handled at Darling Harbour.

Chullora remains railway land today, with trains still assembled and maintained on the site. A heritage hub has been planned here to house Transport Heritage NSW's historic collection, keeping a living link to a century of work on the rails.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: The Chullora railway workshops

properties.title: Maroota Engravings
properties.summary: On the high sandstone country near Wisemans Ferry, in Darug Country above the Hawkesbury, sits one of the densest concentrations of Aboriginal engravings and grinding grooves in the Sydney basin.
body: The sandstone ridges around Maroota, on the southern side of the Hawkesbury near Wisemans Ferry, hold one of the richest collections of Aboriginal sites in the Sydney region. Engravings, axe-grinding grooves, occupation shelters and shell middens cluster across the rock platforms, all on the Country of the Darug people. In one square kilometre south of the river, archaeologists have recorded a dozen engraving sites alone.

The figures were cut into the stone over many generations. At Devil's Rock, now known as the Guragalung Gayanayung Aboriginal Place, more than eighty figures cover the platform, with the ancestral beings Baiame and Daramulan at either end. Among them is a rare engraving of a sailing ship, a craft of the kind that worked the Hawkesbury in the 1790s and early 1800s, carved alongside a man in a top hat and a woman. These contact-era images record the arrival of a new and disruptive world.

Land at Maroota was first reserved for the preservation of Aboriginal carvings in 1952, and the Maroota Historic Site was formally created in 1983. It is now cared for by the National Parks and Wildlife Service, and the sites remain living heritage for the Darug community. To protect the fragile engravings, the precise locations of many are kept quiet.
properties.source: NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service; Heritage NSW

properties.title: Georges Heights
properties.summary: A Mosman headland fortified from the 1870s to guard the harbour. It was an army post for about 130 years and is now the studios and parkland of Headland Park.
body: On the high ground of the Middle Head peninsula at Mosman, the colony built one of the harbour's strongest defences. After British troops withdrew from Australia in 1870, work began in 1871 on the Georges Head Battery. By 1873 it had six circular gun pits linked by zig-zag passages and tunnels to underground magazines, and the elegant Gunners Barracks, designed by colonial architect James Barnet, rose nearby.

From this headland the gunners watched the heads. In 1888 Georges Head was chosen as the best place to observe and fire a new kind of defence, the controlled minefields laid across the harbour channels. The miners themselves worked from a base built down at Chowder Bay, and minefields stayed in the harbour from the 1870s into the early 1920s.

The army held Georges Heights for roughly 130 years. A military hospital stood here during the First World War, and women of the army trained on the site through the post-war decades, until the soldiers finally left in 2002.

The headland was saved from development and is now part of Headland Park, managed by the Sydney Harbour Federation Trust together with the National Parks service. The old batteries survive among the trees, the Gunners Barracks has become a restaurant, and artists work in studios where soldiers once drilled.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Sydney Harbour Federation Trust
properties.image_caption: A 10-inch gun at the Georges Head battery, 1891

properties.title: Lewisham Hospital
properties.summary: The Little Company of Mary opened its first Australian hospital at Lewisham in 1889. For a century the order's blue-veiled sisters ran it as a place of care in the inner west.
body: Six sisters of the Little Company of Mary reached Sydney in 1885 at the invitation of Cardinal Patrick Moran, Australia's first cardinal. People knew them by their blue veils and called them the Blue Sisters. On land at Lewisham given by the Cardinal they built the order's first hospital in Australia. Moran opened the first wing on 9 June 1889, and it began life as the Children's Hospital of the Holy Child. Within a few years it had broadened into general care.

Over the following century the hospital grew into a major general hospital and nurse-training school for the inner west, run throughout by the sisters. Tragedy struck on 1 February 1946 when a Fleet Air Arm Firefly lost rudder control and crashed onto the boiler house. The pilot parachuted clear, but the observer came down with the plane and two kitchen staff on the ground were killed.
The hospital closed in 1988 and the site passed to the St Vincent de Paul Society. It has since been redeveloped for aged care, carrying on a long tradition of Catholic care on the hill at Lewisham.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Little Company of Mary
properties.image_caption: Lewisham Hospital

properties.title: Arnott's at Homebush
properties.summary: In 1908 Arnott's opened a huge biscuit factory at Homebush, beside the railway in Sydney's inner west. For nearly ninety years it baked Australian staples like the SAO, the Iced VoVo and the Tim Tam.
body: William Arnott began baking biscuits in Newcastle in 1865, and his firm, marked by its rosella parrot, grew into one of Australia's best-loved brands. An early Sydney factory opened at Forest Lodge in 1894, but demand soon outgrew it. In 1908 Arnott's opened a far larger works at Homebush, beside the railway, and it became one of the biggest biscuit factories in the southern hemisphere.

Out of its ovens came staples of the Australian pantry, among them the SAO cracker, the Iced VoVo and, from 1964, the Tim Tam. For generations the factory was a major local employer and a landmark, its parrot sign familiar to passengers on passing trains.

Baking shifted to a new plant at Huntingwood in 1997, and the old works were redeveloped from 1998 as the Bakehouse Quarter. The heritage factory buildings survive, along with the SAO sign and small parrot emblems worked into the facades.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: The former Arnott's factory at Homebush, now the Bakehouse Quarter

properties.title: East Hills Migrant Hostel
properties.summary: A camp of former army huts on the Georges River near Hammondville that, through the post-war decades, was the first home in Australia for thousands of assisted migrants and displaced persons.
body: On Heathcote Road near Hammondville, across the Georges River from East Hills station, a cluster of former army huts became one of Sydney's post-war migrant hostels. Commonwealth Hostels Limited took over running it in 1952, and the first group of migrants to live here, in 1954, were British families. Over the years that followed it took in assisted migrants and displaced persons from across war-torn Europe as well.

The accommodation was spartan. Rows of corrugated huts, shared facilities and communal meals were the order of the day. Migrants typically stayed for months while they found work and a place to live. People walking to jobs in Liverpool crossed the river by a wooden footbridge to reach the train.

Most of the huts were pulled down around 1970 and replaced by brick flats, and Commonwealth Hostels wound up its operations in 1978. For the many who passed through, this crowded waystation was the place where their Australian life began.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Archives of Australia

properties.title: Bidjigal Reserve
properties.summary: A large tract of bushland along Darling Mills Creek in the Hills, holding Aboriginal rock shelters and a midden, and named in 2004 to honour the Bidjigal, the clan of the resistance leader Pemulwuy.
body: Running along Darling Mills Creek through the Hills district, Bidjigal Reserve protects close to two hundred hectares of the bushland that once covered the Cumberland Plain. Among its sandstone gullies are Aboriginal sites, including rock shelters and a shell midden, evidence of long occupation. The land lies on Dharug Country.

The reserve was named in 2004 to honour the Bidjigal, the clan tied to the resistance leader Pemulwuy, who fought the British from 1788 until his death in 1802. The Bidjigal are usually placed further south, with Country reaching from Botany Bay along the Georges River, so the name here is an act of commemoration rather than a claim about exactly which lands the clan held. It honours the Dharug connection to this place.

The reserve was formed by joining the old Darling Mills State Forest with Excelsior Park, following a Dharug native-title claim lodged in 1994 and a deed of agreement signed in 2003. It was dedicated on 22 October 2004. What survives is a rare fragment of the bush the first peoples knew.
properties.source: NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service; The Hills Shire
properties.image_caption: Darling Mills Creek, in Bidjigal Reserve

properties.title: Royal Sydney Golf Club
properties.summary: The grand Rose Bay golf club, founded in 1893 and granted its 'Royal' title by Queen Victoria in 1897, and a regular home of the Australian Open for more than a century.
body: The Royal Sydney Golf Club began in 1893 as the Sydney Golf Club, playing at Concord, before moving within a few years to its present links at Rose Bay. The course was laid out on harbourside flats that had been Chinese market gardens. In 1897 Queen Victoria granted the club its 'Royal' prefix, sealing its place at the head of Sydney society.

Its championship course has been one of the homes of Australian golf. It hosted the men's Australian Open fifteen times between 1906 and 2016, drawing the game's greatest players to the eastern suburbs.
Royal Sydney has long been more than a sporting ground. It remains one of the city's most exclusive institutions, and its sweep of fairways is a striking green among the dense suburbs of Rose Bay.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Royal Sydney Golf Club
properties.image_caption: A golf championship at the Royal Sydney Golf Club

properties.title: Hermitage Foreshore
properties.summary: A harbourside track winding from Rose Bay to Nielsen Park through bush and hidden beaches, among them Milk Beach, with its famous view back to the city.
body: The Hermitage Foreshore track runs for around two kilometres along the Vaucluse shoreline, between Bayview Hill Road at Rose Bay and the beaches of Nielsen Park. It threads through pockets of surviving harbour bushland within Sydney Harbour National Park, past secluded coves such as Queens, Hermit and Tingara, and the small sandy crescent of Milk Beach, framed by one of the city's most celebrated skyline views.

The walk passes below the grand grounds of Strickland House and takes its name from The Hermitage, a colonial estate that once stood on the foreshore. The estate began in the 1840s as a sandstone cottage and grew into a Gothic mansion later in the century. The Hermitage Foreshore Reserve itself was acquired for the public in 1912, and the national park around it dates from 1975.
For all its closeness to the eastern suburbs, the track keeps something of the wild edge of the harbour that the Gadigal and Birrabirragal people knew.
properties.source: NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service
properties.image_caption: Milk Beach on the Hermitage Foreshore, with the city beyond

properties.title: Mater Hospital
properties.summary: The North Shore hospital founded by the Sisters of Mercy in 1906, growing from a tiny cottage for women and children into a great maternity hospital above the harbour.
body: The Mater Misericordiae Hospital opened in 1906 as a small cottage hospital for women and children, run by the Sisters of Mercy on Willoughby Road at Crows Nest with twelve beds and twelve cots. In 1910 the Sisters moved to 'Wenona', a grand house near North Sydney, and in 1914 they opened a public general hospital alongside it. Through the century it grew into one of the lower North Shore's major hospitals.

Generations of Sydneysiders were born in its maternity wards, and from 1968 it became a teaching hospital of the University of Sydney. In 1982 the government refused funding for the ageing buildings, choosing instead to invest in the western suburbs, and the public and maternity sections closed.
The Sisters reopened the Mater as a private hospital on Rocklands Road in 1990, and it is now part of the St Vincent's group. It carries more than a century of Catholic care on the North Shore.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Mater Hospital
properties.image_caption: The Mater Misericordiae Hospital, North Sydney

properties.title: Belmore Sports Ground
properties.summary: The spiritual home of the Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs, a working-class rugby league fortress in the south-west where the crowds packed the hill from the 1930s.
body: Belmore Sports Ground opened around 1920 on land once known as Gunn's Paddock, in the heart of Sydney's south-west. Canterbury-Bankstown joined the rugby league premiership in 1935, and the 'Berries', later the Bulldogs, played their first match at the ground in 1936.

For nearly six decades it was one of the great suburban grounds of the game. It drew fiercely partisan, working-class crowds who packed the grassy hill, and a record 27,804 squeezed in on 12 April 1993 to watch Canterbury thrash Parramatta.

The Bulldogs moved their major games to larger stadiums in the 1990s, but they came back in 2012 to make Belmore their training base and spiritual home. They still play the occasional heritage match there before the faithful.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Belmore Sports Ground

properties.title: Mortlake Gasworks
properties.summary: From 1886 the Australian Gas Light Company ran its largest gasworks on the Mortlake peninsula, turning coal into the town gas that lit Sydney. The site is now the suburb of Breakfast Point.
body: In 1886 the Australian Gas Light Company opened a great gasworks on the Mortlake peninsula, in a bend of the Parramatta River. It grew into AGL's largest works, built to supply the whole city, turning coal into the town gas that lit Sydney's streets and homes.

Colliers from the Hunter, the sixty-milers, unloaded at a T-shaped river wharf, and about eleven kilometres of internal railway worked by little steam locomotives shunted coal and coke around the site. Hundreds of people worked here at its peak.

The making of gas from coal stopped on the last day of 1971, and the works finally closed in 1990 after more than a century. Once the contaminated ground was cleaned up, the peninsula was reborn as the residential estate of Breakfast Point. The gasworks is gone, but its shape is still written into the streets.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Unloading coal at the AGL Mortlake gasworks, c.1917

properties.title: Angus & Robertson
properties.summary: The Sydney bookshop and publisher that, from 1886, became the heart of Australian letters, printing Lawson, Paterson and a young nation's own stories.
body: In 1884 the Scottish-born bookseller David Angus opened a shop in Sydney selling secondhand books. In January 1886 he took on a fellow Scot, George Robertson, who put in his savings of fifteen pounds, and Angus & Robertson was born. From 1895 the firm's shop and offices stood at 89 Castlereagh Street, where it grew into the most important bookseller and publishing house in the country.

As a publisher it did more than any other to give Australia a literature of its own. Its lists carried Henry Lawson, A.B. 'Banjo' Paterson, Norman Lindsay, C.J. Dennis and May Gibbs. The rambling city store, with shelves climbing to the ceiling, became a place of pilgrimage for readers and writers alike, and was once billed as the biggest bookshop in the world.
The firm outlasted both its founders and ran on for more than a century. Its name still hangs over bookshops today, long after the great Castlereagh Street store had gone.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Inside the Angus & Robertson bookshop, Castlereagh Street, 1946

properties.title: The 1931 Eviction Battles
properties.summary: In the depths of the Depression, unemployed workers barricaded homes against the bailiffs and fought running battles with police in Bankstown and Newtown over two days in June 1931.
body: By 1931 the Great Depression had thrown much of Sydney's workforce out of jobs, and families who could not find the rent faced eviction. The Unemployed Workers' Movement, tied to the Communist Party, refused to go quietly. Members moved into threatened houses, sandbagged the windows, strung barbed wire across the doors and waited for the bailiffs and the police.

The first big clash came on 17 June at a house in Brancourt Avenue, Bankstown, where Alfred Parsons and his family rented. Police arrived by the truckload before dawn, hurled stones at the house, then charged the front and back. They fired live rounds into the building and wounded two of the defenders. Seventeen men were arrested, and most were sentenced to hard labour.

The next day came the Siege of Union Street in Newtown. About eighteen activists held a two-storey terrace against roughly forty police. The battle lasted more than an hour. Police fired on the house, the men inside answered with a hail of stones that felled around fifteen officers, and thousands of locals packed the street until baton charges drove them back. Every defender was hurt, some of them badly, and an onlooker collapsed and died of a heart attack as the fighting raged.

The street fighting shook the public, and the Lang government acted. The Reduction of Rent Act cut rents across the state, and the Ejectments Postponement Act barred eviction without a court order. It was a hard-won mercy, wrung out of the barricades. The relief proved short-lived, swept away after Lang himself was dismissed in 1932.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; academic histories

properties.title: Berry Island
properties.summary: A wooded former island on Gore Cove at Wollstonecraft, in Cammeraygal Country. Its shell middens, grinding grooves and a great rock engraving can still be read along the Gadyan Track.
body: Berry Island sits on Gore Cove on the lower North Shore, and it was once a true island. In the early nineteenth century Edward Wollstonecraft built a stone causeway across the mudflats, and the channel was later filled to form the grassy neck that joins it to Wollstonecraft today. This is Cammeraygal Country, and the sandstone still carries the marks of long living here. There are shell middens of the Sydney cockle, axe-grinding grooves worn smooth, a freshwater rock pool, and a large engraving cut into the rock that ranks among the biggest found anywhere in Sydney.

The short Gadyan Track loops the island and tells its Aboriginal story as it goes. The name is fitting. Gadyan is the Cammeraygal word for the Sydney cockle, the shellfish gathered here in such numbers that its shells built up the middens you can still see.

The island took its English name from the merchant Alexander Berry, who with Edward Wollstonecraft received the surrounding land in a grant from Governor Macquarie in 1820. The two were business partners, and the names of both men were stamped across this corner of the harbour.

In October 2023 the area's Aboriginal heritage was recognised on the State Heritage Register. Berry Island was listed as part of Badangi, a 48 hectare stretch of foreshore reserves around the Greenwich and Wollstonecraft peninsulas that holds some of the few natural landscapes left in public hands on Sydney Harbour.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; North Sydney Council
properties.image_caption: Berry Island Reserve, Wollstonecraft

properties.title: Pratten Park
properties.summary: Pratten Park is the Ashfield sports ground that opened in 1912 and became the founding home of the Western Suburbs Magpies rugby league club.
body: Pratten Park opened in September 1912, when the Governor of New South Wales did the honours. It took its name from Herbert Pratten, the Ashfield mayor who pushed hard for the ground and helped pay for it. From that first season it was home to the Western Suburbs Magpies, one of rugby league's eight foundation clubs, who would play there for more than half a century.

Inner-west crowds packed the place for decades. The ground record came in 1964, when 12,407 people watched Wests take on St George. Pratten Park earned its keep beyond league too, hosting cricket, soccer and, in 1939, a Davis Cup tennis tie.

The Magpies eventually moved on in 1966, returning only now and then in later years. The ground itself carried on, and today it is home to Western Suburbs district cricket, still busy more than a century after the Governor first opened the gates.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Ashfield & District Historical Society
properties.image_caption: Davis Cup players John Bromwich and Adrian Quist at Pratten Park, 1939

properties.title: The Homebush Bay Shipwrecks
properties.summary: A cluster of rusting ship hulks sits abandoned in Homebush Bay on the Parramatta River, including the SS Ayrfield, whose hull now carries a small forest of mangroves.
body: From 1966 a corner of Homebush Bay was used to break up unwanted ships for scrap. When the price of scrap metal fell and the trade stopped paying, several half-dismantled hulks were simply left to rust where they lay.

The best known is the SS Ayrfield, a steel ship built in Britain in 1911. It served as a supply vessel in the Pacific during the Second World War, then from 1951 carried coal from Newcastle to Blackwattle Bay under its new owner, R.W. Miller. Sent to Homebush in 1972, it was never fully cut up, and over the decades grey mangroves took root in its rusted hull. The result is one of Sydney's strangest and most photographed sights. Nearby lie the remains of the SS Mortlake Bank and other vessels.

Once a forgotten industrial backwater, the bay was cleaned up and reshaped beside what became Sydney Olympic Park. The old hulks survive within a heritage conservation area, a quiet reminder of the working river that once ran through here.
properties.source: Wikipedia; NSW Heritage
properties.image_caption: The SS Ayrfield, the 'floating forest' of Homebush Bay

properties.title: The Australian Women's Weekly
properties.summary: Launched in Sydney on 10 June 1933, The Australian Women's Weekly grew into the most-read magazine in the country and a fixture of Australian homes for generations.
body: The first Australian Women's Weekly appeared on 10 June 1933, billed as a Sydney newspaper for women and edited by George Warnecke. It was backed by the young press figure Frank Packer together with the former federal treasurer Ted Theodore. Priced at twopence, it was an instant hit, selling 121,162 copies of its first issue and selling out by lunchtime, far beyond what anyone had expected.

For decades it held the highest circulation of any magazine in the country, reaching around 800,000 copies a week by the 1960s. Its blend of news, fashion, fiction, royalty and, from the 1970s, its famous Test Kitchen recipes ran through ordinary Australian life. It was produced from the Packer publishing offices in the city.
The Weekly went monthly in 1983 yet still publishes today, one of the most enduring titles in Australian media.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: An early cover of The Australian Women's Weekly, 1933

properties.title: The Jibbon Engravings
properties.summary: On a sandstone headland at Bundeena, on the edge of the Royal National Park, a gallery of Dharawal rock engravings of whales, a shark and a great ancestral figure looks out to the sea.
body: At Jibbon Head, beyond the village of Bundeena on the northern edge of the Royal National Park, a flat sandstone platform carries one of the region's finest galleries of Aboriginal rock engravings. They are the work of the Dharawal people, on whose Country the headland sits. Cut into the rock are around a dozen figures, including two large whales, a shark, a stingray, kangaroos, and a tall ancestral being whom the park signage names as Daramulan.

The engravings are old beyond easy dating, made long before the British arrived and worn smooth by centuries of salt wind. A raised boardwalk and viewing platform, opened in December 2014 with the local Aboriginal community, now lets visitors see them without walking on the stone.
They remain living Dharawal cultural heritage, and one of the few such sites in the national park open to the public.
properties.source: NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service; Dharawal community

properties.title: Glenbrook Train Disaster
properties.summary: On 2 December 1999 a crowded interurban train ran into the back of the stationary Indian Pacific in a rock cutting near Glenbrook, killing seven people. The disaster forced a deep rethink of rail safety in New South Wales.
body: At 8.22 on the morning of 2 December 1999, a Sydney-bound interurban train rounded a tight curve in a deep rock cutting just east of Glenbrook station, in the lower Blue Mountains, and slammed into the rear of the long-haul Indian Pacific. Seven passengers died and more than fifty were taken to hospital. The two trains had no business sitting one behind the other on the same line, yet a chain of small failures had put them there.

The Indian Pacific had been authorised to pass a signal stuck at red and had then halted at a second failed signal further on. Its driver climbed down to phone the signaller, but a missing part led him to think the trackside telephone was broken. Meanwhile the interurban, also cleared past the red at Glenbrook, built up too much speed and could not see the stationary train around the blind curve in time to stop. The inquiry found no single careless driver, but a tangle of degraded signalling, poor visibility, confusing safe-working rules and weak training.

A special commission led by Acting Justice Peter McInerney made ninety-five recommendations, and the State Government accepted ninety-three of them. The findings reshaped how the railways were run, creating independent bodies to inspect safety and investigate accidents. The same commissioner would later examine the Waterfall derailment of 2003, another reminder of how quickly small lapses on the rails can turn deadly.
properties.source: Special Commission of Inquiry (McInerney); Wikipedia

properties.title: The Mushroom Tunnel
properties.summary: Bored through the Redbank Range and opened in 1867, Picton's tunnel was said to be the first railway tunnel used in New South Wales. Bypassed in 1919, it later grew mushrooms in the dark and gathered a reputation for ghosts.
body: When the southern railway pushed through Picton, it was carried beneath the Redbank Range in a brick-arched tunnel about 180 metres long, opened in February 1867 and said to be the first railway tunnel used by the New South Wales railways. Trains rumbled through it for half a century, until a new deviation in 1919 left the old line and its tunnel abandoned.

The cool, damp dark soon found other uses. The military stored materiel here during the Second World War, and afterwards the tunnel was given over to growing mushrooms, which is how it earned the name that stuck.

Today it is best known for its ghost. Local legend tells of Emily Bollard, said to have been struck and killed by a train in the tunnel in 1916, whose spirit is supposed to linger in the dark. No record proves the haunting, but the tale is enough to keep drawing the curious to the old Mushroom Tunnel.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Wollondilly heritage
properties.image_caption: The old Redbank Range 'Mushroom Tunnel' at Picton

properties.title: The Razorback Blockade
properties.summary: On 2 April 1979 five owner-driver truckies parked their rigs across the Hume Highway at the crest of Razorback Mountain near Picton. The nine-day blockade spread across the country and helped kill off the road maintenance tax.
body: On 2 April 1979 five owner-drivers, Ted Stevens, Barry Grimson, Jack Hibbert, Colin Bird and Spencer Watling, parked their trucks across the Hume Highway where it climbed over Razorback Mountain, just north of Picton. In one stroke they choked the main road freight route between Sydney and Melbourne. They were protesting the road maintenance tax, charged by the ton and the mile, which was grinding small operators into the ground.

Within days some two hundred trucks had massed at Razorback, and a driver blocked the nearby Menangle Road to stop traffic slipping around them. Sympathy blockades sprang up across New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia and Queensland, idling thousands of vehicles. For nine days the nation's road freight all but seized up.

It worked. The other states agreed to scrap the road maintenance tax, raise the allowable carrying weight and hold an inquiry into freight rates, and the NSW Premier Neville Wran matched the deal. The blockade broke up on its ninth day and passed into legend as the great stand of the Australian truckie. A memorial now marks the spot on the old highway.
properties.source: Wikipedia; trucking histories
properties.image_caption: The Razorback Blockade Memorial on the old Hume Highway

properties.title: The Nepean: Penrith's Rowing River
properties.summary: The broad, still reach of the Nepean at Penrith has been Western Sydney's rowing river for well over a century, from the 1938 British Empire Games to the rowing of the 2000 Olympics nearby.
body: The wide, slow stretch of the Nepean River at Penrith has long been Western Sydney's great course for rowing. Penrith Rowing Club traces its history to 1888, and the river earned international standing in 1938, when it staged the rowing of the British Empire Games over a measured course of about a mile and a quarter. The Great Public Schools regattas have long been rowed on these waters too.

Crowds gathered on the banks at Tench Reserve, near the Victoria Bridge that opened in 1867 as a railway crossing and later carried road traffic, to watch the eights sweep past. Since October 1982 the paddlewheeler Nepean Belle has carried sightseers along the same reach and up into the gorge.

The rowing story reached its height in 2000, when the purpose-built Sydney International Regatta Centre at nearby Penrith Lakes, laid out on reclaimed quarry land, hosted the rowing and canoe sprint events of the Sydney Olympic Games.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: A Great Public Schools regatta on the Nepean River at Penrith

properties.title: The Milperra Massacre
properties.summary: On Father's Day in 1984, rival outlaw motorcycle clubs fought a gun battle in the car park of a Milperra hotel, leaving seven people dead, among them a 14-year-old girl, and shocking the nation.
body: On the afternoon of Sunday 2 September 1984, Father's Day, members of the rival Comanchero and Bandidos motorcycle clubs met while armed in the car park of the Viking Tavern at Milperra in Sydney's south-west. A swap-meet for motorcycle parts had been advertised as the meeting ground, and it erupted into a chaotic close-quarters battle of guns and clubs that ran for about ten minutes.
Seven people were killed. Four were Comancheros, two were Bandidos, and the seventh was Leanne Walters, a 14-year-old bystander struck by a stray bullet. Around two dozen more people were wounded.

The mass trial that followed was one of the largest in Australian legal history, with dozens charged under the common purpose doctrine. Many of the murder convictions were later reduced to manslaughter on appeal. The killings horrified the public and helped drive tighter gun laws in New South Wales. The tavern still stands today under another name.
properties.source: Wikipedia; NSW court records

properties.title: The West Head Engravings
properties.summary: On the sandstone ridges of West Head in Ku-ring-gai Chase, the Garigal people left carved figures and the ochre hand stencils of Red Hands Cave.
body: The high sandstone of West Head, at the northern tip of Ku-ring-gai Chase National Park between Pittwater and Broken Bay, is rich with the rock art of the Garigal people. Along the Resolute track, engravings are cut into flat slabs of stone, among them human figures and the fish, whales and wallabies of the Country. Sheltered nearby is Red Hands Cave, where hands were stencilled onto the rock in red ochre.

The wider park holds hundreds of recorded Aboriginal sites, one of the densest concentrations anywhere near Sydney, with more than a thousand individual figures carved across its ridges. Some of the work is thought to be many thousands of years old.

The people of this coast were long labelled 'Guringai', a name invented in the 1890s and now regarded as unreliable. The Country is more properly understood as that of the Garigal. The engravings remain living heritage, and their exact locations are kept quiet to protect them.
properties.source: NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service

properties.title: The Royal Society of NSW
properties.summary: One of the oldest learned societies in the southern hemisphere, the Royal Society of New South Wales traces its lineage to a colonial gathering of scientific minds in 1821.
body: The Royal Society of New South Wales traces its origins to the Philosophical Society of Australasia, founded in Sydney on 27 June 1821 under Governor Brisbane to study the natural world of a new colony. That first society soon lapsed. It was revived in 1850 as the Australian Philosophical Society, renamed the Philosophical Society of New South Wales in 1856, and on 12 December 1866 received Queen Victoria's assent to become the Royal Society of New South Wales.

It grew into the leading forum of colonial science. Its members surveyed the colony's geology, astronomy, chemistry and natural history, and published their findings in the Society's journal, one of the oldest peer-reviewed publications in the southern hemisphere.

From 1875 the Society met in its own Society's House at 5 Elizabeth Street in the city. In 1931 it moved into Science House at Church Hill, a building it shared with the Institution of Engineers and the Linnean Society. The Society remains active today and is among the oldest learned societies in the southern hemisphere.
properties.source: Royal Society of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Redfern Oval
properties.summary: For four decades from 1948, Redfern Oval was the home of the South Sydney Rabbitohs, in a suburb that was a heart of working-class and Aboriginal Sydney. The red and green packed its hill until 1987.
body: Redfern Oval sits in Redfern Park, beside the inner-city streets known as The Block. In 1948 it became the home ground of the South Sydney Rabbitohs, and the faithful crowded onto its hill so devotedly that some called the place the Holy Land. The first match here, in April 1948, was a 19-all draw against arch rivals Eastern Suburbs.

Souths are the most successful club in the history of Australian rugby league, and the oval is still treated as their spiritual home. It was also the ground of the Redfern All Blacks, the longest-running all-Aboriginal rugby league club in the country. Through the 1960s and 1970s the club became more than a football team, with its leaders helping to build Aboriginal-controlled legal, housing and medical services in the area.

Souths played their last top-grade home games here in 1987 before moving to the Sydney Football Stadium. The ground then fell into neglect. The City of Sydney rebuilt it and reopened it in 2009 as a community ground and the club's training base, which kept football at the centre of Redfern life.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Spectators at Redfern Oval, 1934

properties.title: Moore Park
properties.summary: Moore Park was carved from the old Sydney Common in 1866 to give the city's south-east a great public playground. It once held a zoo and the showground, and its stadiums still crowd the edges.
body: Moore Park was set aside in 1866 from the open grazing land of the old Sydney Common, and named the following year after Charles Moore, the Mayor of Sydney. It became the great recreation ground for the city's south-east.

For decades the park drew crowds to its attractions. The colony's first public zoo opened on the old Billygoat Swamp in 1884, after the Zoological Society formed in 1879, and the animals later crossed the harbour to Taronga in 1916. From 1882 the Royal Agricultural Society ran its Easter Show on the adjoining showground, a fixture that lasted until 1997 before the Show moved to Homebush in 1998. Australia's first recorded polo match was played here in 1874, and the Australian Golf Club laid its first links across the grass.
The fig-lined park still holds the line of open green between the dense eastern suburbs and the big sporting grounds that grew up along its edge, the cricket ground and the stadium among them.
properties.source: Centennial Parklands; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Moore Park, c.1875

properties.title: Wisemans Ferry
