properties.summary: Wisemans Ferry is the Hawkesbury crossing started in 1827 by the ex-convict Solomon Wiseman. It opened the way to the convict-built Great North Road and is the oldest ferry crossing in New South Wales.
body: Solomon Wiseman, a Thames lighterman transported to Sydney in 1806, won his freedom and a grant of land on the Hawkesbury. There in the 1820s he built an inn called the Sign of the Packet, and in 1827 he began running a ferry across the river under a lease that let him charge a toll, on the condition that he carried government men and supplies for nothing.

His crossing became the gateway to the Great North Road, the highway that convict gangs hacked through the sandstone ranges between 1826 and 1836 to link Sydney with the Hunter Valley. Wiseman grew rich and powerful on the river trade, a hard man whose reputation later curdled into ghost stories.

River steamers soon stole most of the road's traffic, but the ferry has outlasted them all. A cable punt still carries cars across the Hawkesbury at Wisemans Ferry, the oldest ferry crossing in New South Wales and perhaps in the country.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: A car on the Wisemans Ferry punt, 1930s

properties.title: The Sutherland Hospital
properties.summary: The Sutherland Hospital was the public hospital the Sutherland Shire campaigned for over decades. It opened at Caringbah in 1958 as the booming postwar Shire came of age.
body: For years the growing Sutherland Shire, in Sydney's south, had no hospital of its own. The campaign for one began in the 1940s as the district filled with postwar families. The reward came at Caringbah, where Premier J.J. Cahill laid the foundation stone in 1955 and the Sutherland District Hospital opened in 1958.
Its first building rose four storeys with three wings and around 228 beds. It was a near twin of the hospital built at Bankstown about the same time, both stamped from the same postwar pattern.

The Shire kept growing, and the hospital grew with it. A new building went up behind the original in the 2000s, and the Sutherland is now the main public and teaching hospital for the south. Despite the name, it stands in the suburb of Caringbah.
properties.source: Sutherland Shire Libraries; South Eastern Sydney LHD
properties.image_caption: The Sutherland Hospital, Caringbah

properties.title: The Corso
properties.summary: The Corso is the promenade across the Manly sand spit, laid out in 1855 by Henry Gilbert Smith to link the harbour to the surf. It became the spine of Sydney's first great seaside resort.
body: When Henry Gilbert Smith set out to make Manly a seaside resort in the 1850s, he laid a boardwalk in 1855 across the low sandy neck between the calm harbour and the ocean beach. He named it the Corso after the famous street in Rome. Hotels rose at each end, the Pier Hotel by the wharf and the Steyne by the surf, and steamers brought day-trippers across the harbour to stroll it.
Manly became, in a later ferry company's slogan, seven miles from Sydney and a thousand miles from care. The Corso was its heart, a parade of bathers, holidaymakers and shopfronts.
Now a pedestrian mall, the Corso still carries the crowds from the ferry to the beach, much as Smith intended more than a century and a half ago.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Northern Beaches Council
properties.image_caption: The Corso, Manly

properties.title: Bull Cave
properties.summary: A Dharawal rock shelter near Campbelltown holds a charcoal and ochre drawing of the wild cattle that escaped the young colony and astonished the local people. It is one of the earliest known records of First Nations contact art in the Sydney region.
body: In the first months of the colony a few head of cattle wandered off from Sydney and disappeared into the bush. Seven years later, in 1795, Governor Hunter's men stumbled on them again. The lost beasts had bred into a wild herd of dozens, grazing the rich flats south-west of the settlement that the colonists named the Cowpastures.

The Dharawal people of that country had been watching these strange new animals too. In a sandstone shelter at Kentlyn, on the edge of Campbelltown, an unknown artist drew them in charcoal and ochre. Two large cattle dominate the wall, set among hand stencils and other figures. The animals were drawn without horns and with their hooves carefully marked, a record in the old way of a new world arriving on Country.

Vandals damaged the site in 1982, and it is now fenced and protected. Bull Cave was added to the State Heritage Register in 2017, recognised for the rare Aboriginal view it gives of the early contact years. Its exact location is kept quiet to guard it from harm.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Campbelltown City

properties.title: The Wreck of the Edward Lombe
properties.summary: On a wild August night in 1834 the barque Edward Lombe was driven onto the rocks at Middle Head, just inside the Heads, with the loss of twelve lives. Sydney remembered it as the colony's first great shipwreck.
body: On the night of 25 August 1834 the barque Edward Lombe, inbound from Hobart under Captain Stuart Stroyan, was caught by a southerly gale as she tried to enter Port Jackson without a pilot. Driven across the harbour, she struck the rocks at Middle Head and her hull began to break apart in the heavy seas.

Of the twenty-nine people aboard, twenty-two crew and seven passengers, twelve were drowned, the captain among them. The seas broke the ship's back quickly, but the stern section held on the rocks long enough for the other seventeen to cling on until they could be taken off at dawn.

The disaster happened almost within sight of the town, and Sydney had never before watched a large ship destroyed with such loss of life. The coroner's jury, which included several master mariners, pressed the government to mark the dangerous Sow and Pigs Reef. In 1836 a lightship, the first in Australia, was moored there. The exact wreck site marked here is approximate.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Disaster Resilience

properties.title: Canterbury Racecourse
properties.summary: The inner-west racecourse beside the Cooks River at Canterbury held its first meeting in 1884 and has been a fixture of Sydney racing ever since.
body: Horses had been raced on the Canterbury flats since the 1850s, and racing revived there in 1871, but the present course beside the Cooks River took shape when the Canterbury Park Race Club made the ground its headquarters. The club held its first meeting on 19 January 1884 before a crowd of about three thousand, and the opening race went to a horse named Boniface.
Close to the city and served by its own railway station, Canterbury grew into one of Sydney's busiest tracks and became known for its twilight and night meetings.

The Sydney Turf Club took over the course in 1945 and ran it until 2011, when it merged with the Australian Jockey Club to form the Australian Turf Club. Canterbury remains a working racecourse and training centre in the heart of the inner south-west.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Turf Club
properties.image_caption: Canterbury Park Racecourse

properties.title: The Sphinx War Memorial
properties.summary: A small sandstone Sphinx hidden in the Ku-ring-gai bush was carved by hand by a gassed and dying Great War digger as a memorial to the mates who did not come home.
body: Deep in Ku-ring-gai Chase, near North Turramurra, stands an unlikely monument. It is a scaled-down replica of the Great Sphinx of Giza, about one eighth the size of the original, flanked by little pyramids and cut straight from the living sandstone.

The work was done by one man, Private William Shirley of the 13th Battalion, an infantryman left with failing lungs after being gassed on the Western Front. Convalescing at the nearby Lady Davidson Home, he spent his last years from about 1926 chiselling the Sphinx by hand as a memorial to his fallen comrades. Shirley died in August 1929, and the memorial was unveiled on 3 May 1931 before a large crowd.
The little Sphinx still keeps its watch in the bush, at the head of a popular walking track that drops down to Bobbin Head.
properties.source: NSW War Memorials Register; NPWS
properties.image_caption: The Sphinx War Memorial, Ku-ring-gai Chase

properties.title: The St Marys Munitions Factory
properties.summary: A vast wartime ammunition factory was thrown up on the plains of Sydney's far west from 1941, where thousands of workers, many of them women, filled shells for the Pacific war.
body: When war reached the Pacific the Commonwealth resumed about 4,500 acres of the old Dunheved farm north of St Marys railway station and built one of its largest munitions works there. Construction began in 1941 and filling started late in 1942. The factory loaded cartridges, shells, mortar bombs and pyrotechnics, and repacked millions of rounds of American ammunition across hundreds of buildings spread over several kilometres.

At its peak around 4,500 people worked the site across three shifts, a great many of them women drawn into war work. Those handling explosives wore special metal-free uniforms and shoes. A dedicated railway line and a township of hostels kept the place running.

Munitions making carried on under Australian Defence Industries until the early 1990s. The huge tract has since become the suburbs of Ropes Crossing and Jordan Springs and the bushland of Wianamatta Regional Park, a wartime arsenal turned back into grass and houses.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Monument to the St Marys munitions workers

properties.title: Manly Dam
properties.summary: Built in 1892 to give the seaside town of Manly its own water supply, the dam on Curl Curl Creek later became the heart of a war memorial park, and a much loved place to swim and ride.
body: In 1892 the Public Works Department threw a concrete dam across Curl Curl Creek to give the booming seaside resort of Manly a water supply of its own. It was the largest independent water scheme in metropolitan Sydney, run apart from the main system that served the rest of the city. As Manly and the suburbs along the coast kept growing, the wall was raised more than once to hold back a bigger reservoir, with major works around 1909 and 1922.

Demand soon outgrew the dam. By 1929 it was phased out, and water for Manly and Warringah came instead through a pipeline from the main metropolitan system at Pymble. The dam and the bushland around it then took on a new role. After the First World War the catchment had been set aside as a memorial, and it grew into the Manly Warringah War Memorial Park.

Today the dam is one of the northern beaches' favourite playgrounds. People swim and picnic on the water, and a popular mountain bike trail loops through the surrounding bush. The old dam wall sits at the centre of it all, added to the State Heritage Register in 1999, with the wider memorial park listed in 2023.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Northern Beaches Council
properties.image_caption: The Manly Dam wall

properties.title: Bungarribee
properties.summary: A grand colonial estate on the western plains, taken up in 1822 to breed horses for the East India Company and crowned by a round-towered homestead that the press later dubbed the colony's most haunted house. It was demolished in 1957.
body: In 1822 John Campbell took up about two thousand acres on Eastern Creek, near present-day Doonside, with the formal grant following the next year. A former East India Company man, he meant to breed horses for the company on the open western plains. From around 1825 he built a substantial homestead with a distinctive round tower, its main rooms set in a full circle so that every window, door and mantelpiece had to be curved.

Campbell died in October 1827 with the house still unfinished. Through a long line of later owners it gathered a dark reputation, and by the twentieth century the newspapers were happily calling it Australia's most haunted house, full of screams, cold hands and a bloodstain that kept coming back. The tales owed far more to journalists chasing a good yarn than to anything in the record.

The homestead came down in May 1957, but the place was not lost. Its buried footings were added to the NSW State Heritage Register in 2000 as an archaeological site within Bungarribee Park, and the name carries on in the suburb. The word itself comes from Dharug. An older reading took it to mean a king's burial place, but it is now thought to mean something closer to creek with cockatoos.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Bungarribee homestead, 1954, before its demolition

properties.title: Romano's
properties.summary: For nearly three decades Romano's was Sydney's most glamorous restaurant and nightclub, where café society dined and danced under the eye of Azzalin 'the Dazzlin' Romano.
body: Azzalin Orlando Romano was a Paduan who had run the plush Ambassadors before opening his own Romano's in York Street in 1927. In 1938 he moved it to grander quarters in the basement of the new Prudential building, with the entrance on Castlereagh Street. There it reigned for years as the city's most fashionable restaurant and nightclub.

Through the war and the 1950s, a night at Romano's meant evening dress, an orchestra, dancing and a plate of Steak Diane. The guest list ran to film stars, society figures and visiting celebrities, among them Vivien Leigh, Maurice Chevalier and Prince Philip. American officers crowded the room during the war years.

Azzalin retired in 1964 and sold the restaurant as a going concern. The new owner tried to keep up with the times, relaunching the great room as Romano au Go Go in November 1965. The discotheque flopped, and in 1966 the doors closed for good. It was the end of an era of Sydney glamour.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Food Timeline

properties.title: Gore Hill Cemetery
properties.summary: The lower North Shore's great Victorian burial ground at St Leonards, dedicated in 1868, with its denominational sections and weathered headstones reading like a map of colonial Sydney's communities.
body: Gore Hill Cemetery was dedicated at St Leonards in May 1868 to serve the growing North Shore, the work of local politician William Tunks. He asked the surveyors to leave the existing trees standing for shade, wanting a place where people might walk and reflect rather than simply grieve. Tunks laid it out in separate sections for the Anglican, Catholic, Presbyterian, Methodist, Baptist, Congregational and Jewish faiths, and for those of no denomination, so the ground became a quiet record of Victorian Sydney's communities.

The first interment came in 1877, and about 14,456 people were eventually buried here, most of them between 1900 and 1930. Among them lie Dave Gregory, captain of Australia's first Test cricket side, and the architect Sir John Sulman. Mary MacKillop was buried here after her death in 1909, before her remains were moved to the chapel at North Sydney in 1914. Tunks himself was the first burial in the Church of England section, in 1883.
Closed to new burials in 1974, the heritage-listed cemetery survives as a green island of Victorian monuments beside the freeway that now carries its name.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Gore Hill Cemetery, St Leonards

properties.title: Tooheys' Standard Brewery
properties.summary: One of Sydney's two great brewing dynasties, the Toohey brothers' firm started in 1869 and grew into the Standard Brewery at Surry Hills, the colony's largest, and the chief rival to Tooth's across town.
body: In 1869 the Irish-born brothers John and James Toohey took out a brewing licence and began making ale near Darling Harbour. Both men had kept pubs in Melbourne before trying their luck in Sydney. As the city drank more, they needed room to grow.

In the mid 1870s they built up the Standard Brewery on the corner of Elizabeth and Arden Streets in Surry Hills, on the site of the old Albion Brewery. It grew into the largest brewery in the colony, and for most of a century Tooheys and its great rival, Tooth and Co's Kent Brewery across the city, between them ruled Sydney's beer. The two names were a byword for the city's pubs.

The company floated as Tooheys Limited in 1902. Brewing carried on in Surry Hills until 1955, when the firm shifted to a modern plant out at Lidcombe. The old brewery quarter has since given way to apartments.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia

properties.title: The Elvina Engravings
properties.summary: On a sandstone platform above Elvina Bay in Ku-ring-gai Chase, the Garigal people cut one of the great engraving galleries near Sydney, with whales, fish, people and a towering ancestral being.
body: High on the West Head ridges, just off the Elvina Track in Ku-ring-gai Chase National Park, a broad sandstone platform holds one of the richest Aboriginal engraving galleries near Sydney. This is the Country of the Garigal and Darramuragal people. They cut more than fifty figures into the rock here, among them people, fish and eels, emus, goannas and wallabies, shields and a boomerang, and a great whale several metres long.

The most striking figure is an enormous ancestral being, a Daramulan-like creator with a human foot and what appears to be a bag of quartz crystals held at its forelimb. These were not simple pictures. They carried Dreaming stories, clan totems and teaching, and they tied the people to this country of ridge and water.

Ku-ring-gai Chase holds more than 800 recorded Aboriginal sites, the most of any park in the Sydney region, and this is among the most vivid. The engravings are living heritage, weathering slowly in the open air, and their exact whereabouts are kept low key to protect them.
properties.source: NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service

properties.title: Resch's Brewery
properties.summary: Edmund Resch, a German immigrant from Westphalia, built the third great name in Sydney beer from the 1890s, centred on Dowling Street in Redfern, until Tooth and Co absorbed it in 1929.
body: Edmund Resch came to Australia from Westphalia in 1863, aged sixteen. After mining and hotel-keeping in the bush, he turned to brewing and built the third great name in Sydney beer, after Tooth's and Tooheys. From the 1890s he bought up existing breweries, including the Waverley Brewery and the New South Wales Lager Bier Brewing Company, and brought everything together at a large new complex on Dowling Street in Redfern. He floated Resch's Limited in 1906.

Resch's pilsener and dinner ales became Sydney favourites, and Edmund was brewer by appointment to the Governor-General from 1904 to 1914. The family grew wealthy and prominent, and his son later left the harbourside mansion Swifts to the Catholic Church.

In 1929 the rival Tooth and Co took Resch's over after the company went into voluntary liquidation, though the brand kept selling for decades. The brewery itself closed in 1983, and the Dowling Street site was later redeveloped as the Moore Park Gardens apartments.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Time Gents
properties.image_caption: The former Resch's brewery building, Sydney

properties.title: The Anti-Chinese Agitation of 1888
properties.summary: In 1888 the steamer Afghan reached Sydney to angry crowds, and its Chinese passengers were refused permission to land, a flashpoint of racial exclusion that helped clear the road to White Australia.
body: In May 1888 the steamer Afghan reached Sydney carrying Chinese passengers, into a colony gripped by anti-Chinese feeling. Unions and crowds gathered at Circular Quay in protest, and Premier Sir Henry Parkes forbade most of those aboard from landing, even some who held naturalisation papers. He did so in defiance of a Supreme Court challenge brought by Chinese community leaders, among them the well-known Quong Tart, who argued the detention was unlawful. By the end of the month four Chinese ships sat detained in the harbour.

Within weeks the New South Wales Parliament rushed through the Chinese Restriction and Regulation Act 1888. It raised the poll tax on Chinese arrivals to 100 pounds, limited ships to one Chinese passenger for every 300 tons, and barred Chinese people from being naturalised.

This was a deliberate act of exclusion against people who had been part of the colony since the gold rushes. It marked a clear step towards the White Australia Policy of 1901, and a hard chapter in the long history of Chinese Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Archives

properties.title: Brookvale Oval
properties.summary: The fortress of the northern beaches, home of the Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles since 1947, laid out as a public recreation reserve before the First World War.
body: Brookvale Park was set aside as a public recreation reserve in 1911, after the New South Wales Government and Warringah Shire Council acquired the land, with part of the site given by local landowner Jane Malcolm on the condition that it stay public ground. Its fame came in 1947, when the new Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles entered the rugby league premiership and made the oval their home, playing their first match there against Western Suburbs on 12 April.

For generations 'Brookie' has been one of the league's great suburban grounds, a tight and partisan fortress where the maroon-and-white faithful pack the hill against the rest of Sydney. The ground has now hosted more than 760 top-grade premiership matches.
Redeveloped and renamed for sponsors in recent years, it remains the heart of football on the northern beaches.
properties.source: Wikipedia; Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles
properties.image_caption: Brookvale Oval

properties.title: The Gap
properties.summary: The Gap is the sheer sandstone sea-cliff at South Head guarding the entrance to Sydney Harbour, scene of the Dunbar shipwreck of 1857, a landmark of grandeur and of long sorrow.
body: At South Head, where the land drops sheer to the Tasman Sea, The Gap is the most dramatic of the cliffs that guard the harbour mouth. On the night of 20 August 1857 the migrant clipper Dunbar ran for the Heads in a wild storm and mistook The Gap for the entrance. She was driven onto the rocks below and broke apart. Of the 122 people aboard, only one survived. The seaman James Johnson clung to a ledge through the night and was hauled up the cliff the next day.

The disaster claimed around 121 lives and shook the young colony, which buried many of the dead in a mass funeral. It led to the building of the Hornby Lighthouse on the tip of South Head the following year, to mark the true entrance, while the signal station above had watched for ships since the earliest days of the settlement.

Today The Gap draws visitors for its ocean views, yet it remains a place shadowed by tragedy. Safety fencing, cameras and the quiet watchfulness of locals, most famously Don Ritchie, the so-called Angel of the Gap, have tried to make it a place of rescue rather than despair.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW heritage
properties.image_caption: The Gap, South Head, 1880s

properties.title: Diamond Bay
properties.summary: A slender inlet bitten into the high sandstone cliffs of Vaucluse, Diamond Bay sits on the Federation Cliff Walk, a clifftop path Waverley Council built to mark the centenary of Australia's federation.
body: South of The Gap, the suburb of Vaucluse ends sharply at Diamond Bay, a narrow inlet cut into sandstone cliffs that rise around 80 metres above the Pacific. This is the Country of the Bidjigal, Birrabirragal and Gadigal peoples, who have long known these headlands and the sea below them.

The bay and its clifftop reserve form part of the Federation Cliff Walk, a five-kilometre path running between Watsons Bay and Dover Heights. Waverley Council won a $55,000 Commonwealth grant in 1999 to build it as a project marking the centenary of Australia's federation in 1901. Work continued over several years, and the Diamond Bay boardwalk section opened in 2004.

The timber platforms thread along the clifftops and pass Rosa Gully, a picturesque cleft in the rock that draws climbers and abseilers. Like the headlands to its north, Diamond Bay is both beautiful and sombre. Its lookouts now carry the same protective fencing that watches over The Gap.
properties.source: Waverley Council; Federation Cliff Walk

properties.title: Bents Basin (Gulguer)
properties.summary: A deep, near-circular waterhole where the Nepean River spills out of its sandstone gorge near Wallacia in Sydney's far west. Known as Gulguer, it is a place of deep Aboriginal significance and a long-standing meeting ground.
body: Where the Nepean River pours out of a sandstone gorge in Sydney's far west, it gathers into Bents Basin. The pool is near-circular, about 120 metres across and more than 22 metres deep at its centre. It is a scour hole, carved over centuries when floodwaters drive rocks across the riverbed and grind down the sandstone and shale beneath. The country here is shared by the Dharug, the Dharawal and the Gundungurra, and the basin has long served as a meeting and trading place.

The Aboriginal name, Gulguer, carries the place in a single word. The Gundungurra man Billy Russell described it as the swilling and falling of water that swirls round to cut a great round hole, which is exactly what the river has done. The name belongs to the water and the work it does, not just to the spot on a map.

In Gundungurra tradition the basin is one of the resting places of Gurangatch, a giant eel-like serpent being. Gurangatch was pursued across the land by Mirragan, a quoll-man hunter, and as the two moved through the country their chase gouged out the rivers and waterholes of the region. The surveyor and ethnographer R. H. Mathews recorded these accounts from Aboriginal informants, Billy Russell among them, around the turn of the twentieth century.

Today the waterhole sits within Bents Basin State Conservation Area, alongside the neighbouring Gulguer Nature Reserve. People come to swim, paddle and fish, but the place remains one of living cultural significance for the communities whose Country it is.
properties.source: NSW NPWS; R. H. Mathews ethnography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Bents Basin, on the Nepean River

properties.title: Oran Park Raceway
properties.summary: A motor-racing circuit in Sydney's south-west, opened in 1962 and a fixture of Australian touring-car racing for nearly fifty years before it closed in 2010 and disappeared beneath a new suburb.
body: Oran Park Raceway opened over the weekend of 17 and 18 February 1962 on grazing land near Narellan, established by the Singer Car Club. Over the next half-century the tight, undulating circuit became one of Australia's great motorsport venues. A longer Grand Prix layout, famous for its overpass and figure-eight shape, was added in 1974.

The track hosted a round of the Australian Touring Car Championship every year from 1971, ran V8 Supercars until the final round in December 2008, and even held World Superbike rounds in 1988 and 1989. As Sydney's south-west filled in around it, the circuit's days were numbered. Its last race meeting ran on 16 January 2010, and the track closed for good on 25 January.

The land was sold and redeveloped into the housing estate of Oran Park Town. Streets and a shopping centre now carry the names of the racing that once happened there, so the pin marks a place that survives mostly in memory and in the asphalt buried beneath the suburb.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; motorsport histories
properties.image_caption: Oran Park Raceway, 1990

properties.title: Wattamolla
properties.summary: A freshwater lagoon and waterfall spilling into a sheltered cove on the coast of the Royal National Park, deeply significant to the Dharawal people. Bass and Flinders sheltered here from a gale in 1796 and named it Providential Cove.
body: On the coast south of Sydney, in what is now the Royal National Park, the freshwater lagoon at Wattamolla slips over a low waterfall and runs down into a sheltered cove. This is Dharawal Country. The name is usually translated as 'place near running water', and it fits. The fresh water, the fish and the shelter made it a favoured camping and fishing place over many thousands of years. Rock shelters, shell middens and engravings along this coast still record that long Aboriginal life.

In late March 1796 the explorers George Bass and Matthew Flinders, with the boy William Martin, were caught in a southerly gale while making their way back along the coast in their small boat, the Tom Thumb. With little hope left, they ran into the cove and got ashore safely. Flinders thought Providential Cove a well-adapted name for the place. He also recorded that the local people called it Watta-Mowlee, and that name has stayed.

The cove sits within the Royal National Park, proclaimed on 26 April 1879. It was only the second national park declared anywhere in the world, after Yellowstone, and the first in Australia. The lagoon and waterfall remain a much-loved swimming and picnic spot, carrying the layered history of the place in a single short walk from car park to sand.
properties.source: NSW NPWS; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Wattamolla lagoon, Royal National Park

properties.title: Speakers' Corner, The Domain
properties.summary: From 1878 the open grass of The Domain became Sydney's great soapbox forum, where preachers, radicals and reformers argued politics, religion and revolution on Sunday afternoons.
body: When the colonial government closed Hyde Park to public speaking after a sectarian riot in 1874, Sydney's orators carried their soapboxes a little east to the open grass of The Domain, near where the Art Gallery now stands. The riot had grown out of heated argument over Irish Home Rule, and a Baptist pastor named Daniel Allen was at the centre of it. In 1878 Allen took up the same subject in The Domain, and that is usually counted as the birth of Speakers' Corner.

On Sunday afternoons preachers, radicals, cranks and reformers held forth to whatever crowd would gather. Hecklers were part of the deal, and a speaker who could not hold a hostile audience did not last long. For decades it was a genuine open forum, where anyone could climb up and talk on any subject they liked.

It was also a place where serious causes found a voice. Aboriginal rights campaigners spoke here in the late 1930s and 1940s, among them Jack Patten, William Ferguson and Bill Onus. Pearl Gibbs gave her first public speech at the corner in 1937, later recalling that she shook and shivered because she was so fighting mad at the injustices done to Aboriginal people. The soapbox tradition carried on into the twenty-first century, though it has faded a good deal in recent years.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Bradfield Park Migrant Hostel
properties.summary: A post-war migrant hostel on the leafy upper North Shore, built into the huts of a former RAAF training station, that became one of the first homes in Australia for thousands of British and European arrivals.
body: Bradfield Park sat in the bush at what is now West Lindfield. During the Second World War it was a Royal Australian Air Force training station, and more than 200,000 men and women of the RAAF and the WAAAF passed through on their way to war.

When the war ended and Australia opened its doors to mass migration, the old air-force huts found a new job. From 1949 the site took in displaced European refugees, and from 1951 it ran as a migrant hostel for the great wave of British and European arrivals through the 1950s and 1960s.

For thousands of families these spartan quarters were the first home they knew in Australia. They meant unfamiliar food, shared bathrooms and the unsettling work of starting again, until the hostel closed in 1971.
The CSIRO took over the grounds in 1973 and opened its National Measurement Laboratory there in 1978. Much of the surrounding land became the suburb of West Lindfield.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Ku-ring-gai Historical Society

properties.title: The Great Strike of 1917
properties.summary: In August 1917 workers at the Eveleigh railway workshops walked off over a new system that timed and measured their every task. The dispute grew into one of the largest strikes in Australian history.
body: On 2 August 1917 about 5,780 workers downed tools at the Eveleigh Railway Workshops and the nearby Randwick Tramway Workshops. They were furious at a new card system that recorded what each man did and how long he took, an American idea of efficiency that they read as the thin end of the wedge.

Within weeks the walkout had swelled into the Great Strike of 1917, one of the largest the country has seen. Coal miners, wharf labourers and seamen came out in sympathy until roughly 77,000 workers across New South Wales were on strike, and great crowds packed the Domain for mass rallies.

After about six weeks the strike collapsed. Many strikers never got their old jobs back. Others were taken on again only on lower rungs, and the bitterness lingered in the unions for a generation. It all began here, in the great railway workshops at Eveleigh, part of which is now the Carriageworks arts precinct.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Strike-era crowd, Sydney, c.1917 (photo: Sam Hood)

articles.heading: N.S.W. RAILWAY STRIKE Industry Almost Paralysed ATTEMPTS TO FORCE GENERAL STRIKE.
GENERAL STRIKE PROCLAIMED. WORK TO CEASE AT MIDNIGHT. COAL DECLARED "BLACK. "

properties.title: The George Dean Case
properties.summary: In 1895 the ferry-master George Dean was sentenced to death for poisoning his wife Mary, then pardoned amid huge public sympathy, only for his own confession to undo him. The affair became a landmark in the law of the royal pardon.
body: George Dean ran a ferry between Circular Quay and Milsons Point, and in 1895 he stood trial for trying to poison his wife Mary with arsenic and strychnine slipped into lemon syrup, tea and medicine. The evidence was thin on one crucial point, for nobody could show that Dean had bought or held the poisons. Yet the trial judge, Justice Windeyer, pressed the jury hard, and they returned a guilty verdict that sent Dean to the gallows. Sydney was gripped. The press called it the Lemon Syrup Case and it became one of the colony's great sensations.

Public opinion swung sharply behind Dean. Windeyer already carried the nickname of a hanging judge after the Mount Rennie case, and many believed Dean had been wronged. The government appointed a royal commission, which accepted that Mary might have poisoned herself, and at the end of June Dean walked free on a royal pardon to wild rejoicing in the streets.

Then the whole edifice fell apart. Dean's solicitor, Richard Meagher, boasted to the barrister Julian Salomons that he had bluffed Dean into admitting his guilt and naming the chemist who sold him the arsenic, and the chemist confirmed the sale. Dean, Meagher and others were charged with conspiring to pervert justice, and Dean himself was convicted of perjury and sentenced to 14 years. Meagher was struck from the roll of solicitors. Dean served part of his term at Goulburn Gaol, won remissions for good behaviour, and was released in December 1904, after which he returned to the ferries. The case left its mark on the law of the royal prerogative of mercy, and a longer mark as a warning about the certainties of public sympathy.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW

properties.title: Foundation for Aboriginal Affairs
properties.summary: Through the 1960s and 1970s the Foundation for Aboriginal Affairs was the gathering place of Aboriginal Sydney and a crucible of the urban civil rights movement.
body: The Foundation took its name in 1964, growing out of work by the Aboriginal man Bill Geddes, the Methodist minister Ted Noffs and activists including Ken Brindle and Charles Perkins. In 1965 it bought a building at 810-812 George Street, and in October 1966 it moved in. The premises held meeting rooms, a gymnasium, a library and a cultural centre that sold Aboriginal art, along with short-term lodgings for people new to the city.

People knew it simply as the Foundo. It helped Aboriginal people arriving in Sydney find work and housing, and its Saturday-night dances became famous, sometimes spilling over to Redfern Town Hall. For many it was the warm centre of Aboriginal life in the city.

It was also where the movement found its feet. Charles Perkins managed the place from 1965 to 1969, and a generation of activists passed through, among them Chicka Dixon and Gary Foley, as the campaign for the 1967 referendum gathered force. The Foundation closed in 1977 as Aboriginal-run organisations took up the work it had begun.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; History of Aboriginal Sydney
properties.image_caption: Chicka Dixon at the Foundation for Aboriginal Affairs

properties.title: St Mary & St Mina's: Sydney's first Coptic church
properties.summary: At Sydenham in 1969, Egyptian migrants founded St Mary and St Mina's, said to be the first Coptic Orthodox church established anywhere outside the Nile Valley.
body: When Egyptian Christians began arriving in Sydney during the 1960s, they carried with them the ancient faith of the Coptic Orthodox Church. In 1967 the community wrote to Pope Kyrillos VI in Cairo, asking for a church and a priest. The first Coptic wedding in Australia followed on Australia Day in 1969, held in a Salvation Army hall at Redfern.

Later that year the community bought a former Methodist church at 24 Railway Road, Sydenham, and dedicated it as St Mary and St Mina's. It is said to be the first Coptic Orthodox church established anywhere outside Egypt's Nile Valley, a quiet milestone in the long history of a faith that traces itself to the early centuries of Christianity.

For three decades the small Sydenham church was the spiritual home of Sydney's fast-growing Coptic community. As aircraft noise spread around the airport, the cathedral moved to Bexley in 2002, and the old church was demolished in 2017.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: The Nugan Hand Bank
properties.summary: The Nugan Hand Bank was a Sydney merchant bank whose collapse in 1980, after its co-founder was found shot dead, became one of Australia's most notorious financial scandals.
body: Frank Nugan, a Sydney lawyer, and Michael Hand, a former US Green Beret, founded Nugan Hand Ltd in Sydney in 1973. It grew into a merchant bank with a web of overseas branches and a reputation for moving money in ways few could trace. From its offices on Macquarie Street, it ran an operation that would unravel in spectacular fashion.

In the early hours of 27 January 1980, Nugan was found shot dead by a rifle in his Mercedes near Lithgow, and an inquest later recorded a verdict of suicide. Within months the bank collapsed, insolvent by tens of millions, with many of its records destroyed. Michael Hand fled the country under a false name and vanished for decades.

A royal commission found money laundering and wholesale breaches of banking law. Darker claims of drug money, arms dealing and links to US intelligence have circled the affair ever since, and much of it was never resolved. The headquarters once stood on the 8th floor at 55 Macquarie Street.
properties.source: Stewart Royal Commission; press reports

properties.title: The Harbour Boom Net
properties.summary: The anti-submarine boom net strung across Sydney Harbour in 1942 was a kilometre-long wartime barrier, and the foundations of its winch house still survive at Green Point.
body: After Japanese midget submarines slipped into Sydney Harbour on the night of 31 May 1942, finishing the harbour's half-built defences became urgent. The navy strung an anti-submarine boom net more than a kilometre long across the water, from Georges Head on the north shore to Green Point here at Watsons Bay, with a gate vessel opening and closing two channels so ships could still pass.

The net was not yet complete on the night of the raid. One of the midget submarines fouled the central section and tangled its propellers near the western gate. Unable to break free, the two-man crew set off demolition charges and were lost. The boom went on guarding the harbour for the rest of the war and was removed in 1945 and 1946.

On the tip of Green Point, now called Laing Point, the foundations of the winch house that worked the net still survive. They are the last trace on land of Sydney's wartime harbour defences, alongside the buried fortifications and bunkers nearby.
properties.source: Naval Historical Society of Australia
properties.image_caption: Boom-net winch house foundations, Green Point

properties.title: Little Poland: Ashfield
properties.summary: Ashfield became the heart of Polish Sydney after the Second World War, its community gathering around the Polish Club on Liverpool Road, which opened in 1967.
body: After the Second World War, thousands of Poles made new lives in Sydney. Many were former soldiers of the Polish forces, others were displaced persons who could not return to a homeland now under Soviet control. A good number settled in and around Ashfield in the inner west, which came to be thought of as a little Poland.

The community's anchor is the Polish Club on Liverpool Road. In the early 1960s its founders bought a doctor's residence and the tennis court behind it, then 208 founding members paid to build a clubhouse on the site. It opened on 21 May 1967, and for generations it has kept Polish language, food, faith and culture alive in Sydney.

Among those who passed through were Cardinal Karol Wojtyla, who visited in 1973 and later became Pope John Paul II, and Lech Walesa, the Solidarity leader and Nobel Peace Prize winner. Their visits tied this suburban club to the larger story of Poland's struggle against communism.
properties.source: Polish Club Ashfield; Ashfield & District Historical Society

properties.title: The Wreck of the Malabar
properties.summary: A coastal steamer wrecked in fog on the rocks of Long Bay in 1931 drew crowds in their thousands, and the spectacle was so famous that the local village was renamed Malabar after the ship.
body: On the foggy morning of 2 April 1931, the Burns Philp motor vessel Malabar was steaming out of Sydney, bound for Singapore and Darwin via Melbourne, when she lost her bearings in the murk. She ran at speed onto the rocks of the northern headland of Long Bay, close to Miranda Point. Everyone aboard got safely ashore.

The stranded ship became a sensation. It was Easter Show weekend, and contemporary reports estimated that more than 100,000 Sydneysiders streamed out to the cliffs and beaches to watch her break up, while locals carried off cargo that washed ashore. Few wrecks have ever drawn such a crowd.

The Malabar left a lasting mark on the map. In September 1933 the nearby village of Brand, long burdened by its link to Long Bay Gaol, was officially renamed Malabar after the ship. A vessel lost on the rocks had given its name to a suburb.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian National Maritime Museum
properties.image_caption: The wrecked MV Malabar at Long Bay, 1931

properties.title: Ukrainian Lidcombe
properties.summary: Lidcombe in Sydney's west became the heart of Ukrainian Sydney, built by postwar displaced persons around St Andrew's Ukrainian Catholic Church.
body: From the late 1940s, Ukrainians who had been swept across Europe by the war began rebuilding their lives in Sydney. Many were displaced persons who could not return to a homeland under Soviet rule. A large number settled around Lidcombe in the city's west, and the suburb soon became the centre of Ukrainian life in Sydney.

Here the new arrivals built the institutions of a community in exile. They raised a church, two halls and two schools, keeping the Ukrainian language and faith alive in their adopted home. Lidcombe remains the cultural heart of the Ukrainian community in Sydney to this day.

The community's anchor is St Andrew's Ukrainian Catholic Church on Mary Street, built between 1959 and 1961 and dedicated as a memorial to Ukrainians who died in the war. Raised in the Byzantine Ukrainian style, its domes brought a piece of the old country to suburban Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Federation of Ukrainian Organisations
properties.image_caption: St Andrew's Ukrainian Catholic Church, Lidcombe

properties.title: Russian Strathfield
properties.summary: Strathfield became the home of Sydney's 'White Russian' emigres, who gathered around Saints Peter and Paul, the first Russian Orthodox cathedral built in Australia. Its foundation stone was laid in 1950.
body: After the Russian Revolution and then the Second World War, waves of 'White Russian' emigres reached Sydney. Many had already fled once, to Harbin and Shanghai in China, before the communist victory there uprooted them a second time. They put down roots around Strathfield and Cabramatta, and built the institutions of a community that had lost its homeland.

The heart of it became the cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul on Vernon Street, Strathfield. The Russian Orthodox Church Abroad resolved to build it in 1949, and Strathfield was chosen for its central position and easy access by train. Archbishop Theodore Rafalsky laid the first stone, set with holy relics, on 7 October 1950.

Construction began in 1951 and the cathedral opened in 1954. It was the first Russian Orthodox cathedral built in Australia, and it remains the seat in this country of the Russian Orthodox Church Abroad.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Russian Orthodox Church Abroad
properties.image_caption: SS Peter & Paul Russian Orthodox Cathedral, Strathfield

properties.title: The First Execution, 1788
properties.summary: On 27 February 1788, four weeks after the First Fleet came ashore, the convict and engraver Thomas Barrett became the first person legally hanged in the colony. The gallows stood on a rise in The Rocks the convicts came to call Hangman's Hill.
body: Barely a month after the First Fleet landed, the colony carried out its first execution. Thomas Barrett was a convict and a skilled engraver, and he is believed to have made the silver Charlotte Medal that marked the fleet's long voyage out. He and three others were convicted of stealing food from the government stores, and all four were sentenced to die.

On the evening of 27 February 1788 Barrett was hanged from a tree on the high ground between the male and female convict camps on the western side of Sydney Cove. Two of the men condemned with him were reprieved at the last moment and banished from the colony, and the fourth was sentenced to be flogged.

The hanging took place on a rise the convicts soon called Hangman's Hill, near where Essex and Harrington Streets meet today. In a settlement only weeks old, with rations already short, it announced plainly that the gallows had arrived along with everything else the fleet had carried.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Charlotte Medal, engraved by Thomas Barrett, 1788

properties.title: The Sudds and Thompson Affair
properties.summary: In 1826 a soldier named Joseph Sudds died after Governor Darling had him locked in spiked iron collars of his own design. The death helped fuel the colony's long fights for a free press and trial by jury.
body: In 1826 two soldiers of the 57th Regiment, Joseph Sudds and Patrick Thompson, hatched a desperate plan. They would commit a theft, be convicted, and so be discharged from the army into the freer life of a transported convict. The scheme misfired badly.

Governor Ralph Darling was determined to make an example of them. He overrode their sentence, ordered them into a chain gang, and had the men paraded in spiked iron collars and chains of his own design. Sudds was already ill, and he died on 12 November 1826, only days after the irons went on.

The colony erupted. W.C. Wentworth and the radical newspapers, the Australian and the Monitor, turned the death into a cause and cast Darling as a tyrant. The affair fed the colony's growing demands for freedom of the press and trial by jury, and it shadowed Darling until his recall in 1831. It all began at the military barracks on George Street, on the ground that is now Wynyard.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: Sudds and Thompson in spiked iron collars, 1826

properties.title: Lithuanian Bankstown
properties.summary: Bankstown became the heart of post-war Lithuanian Sydney, settled by displaced persons from a homeland under Soviet occupation. On a street nicknamed Litho Street they built their own hall, opened in 1959.
body: Among the post-war displaced persons who remade Sydney were several thousand Lithuanians, driven from a homeland that the Soviet Union had swallowed. Most arrived between 1947 and 1950, and after their first years in work camps at places such as Chullora and Regents Park, many gathered around Bankstown and Yagoona.

By the 1950s one stretch of Horton Street held so many Lithuanian families that locals called it Litho Street. The community bought a block of land in 1954 and set about raising a hall of its own, working with little money and a great deal of volunteer labour.

Designed by Izidorius Jonaitis, the building opened in 1959 under the name Dainava. With its auditorium, stage and weekend school, it became the place where the language, dances and faith of a captive nation were kept alive in exile, a small piece of Lithuania on the far side of the world.
properties.source: Lithuanian community histories; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: The Sophia Jane
properties.summary: The paddle steamer Sophia Jane reached Sydney in May 1831, the first sea-going steamer to arrive in the colony. Within weeks she was working under steam and carrying the age of steam onto Australian waters.
body: On 13 May 1831 the Sophia Jane sailed into Sydney from the Thames, her paddle wheels stowed for the long passage out. She had crossed the world under sail, with her engine quiet, and only once she reached the colony was she made ready to steam.

Refitted at the careening ground on the eastern side of Sydney Cove, she became the first vessel to turn a paddle-wheel under steam in local waters. On 12 June 1831 she towed the departing Lady Harewood down the harbour, and within days she was steaming up the coast to the Hunter River, opening the age of steam on the Australian seaboard.

A locally built rival, the Surprise, had been launched only weeks before, so the Sophia Jane is best remembered as the first sea-going steamer to reach Australia rather than the very first to move under steam here. For years afterward she plied Port Jackson and the coast, her smoke trailing over a harbour that had until then known only sail.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Living Histories (Newcastle)
properties.image_caption: The paddle steamer Sophia Jane

properties.title: The First Legislative Council, 1843
properties.summary: In 1843 New South Wales held Australia's first colonial election, sending 24 elected members to a reshaped Legislative Council and beginning representative government in a colony founded as a gaol.
body: In the winter of 1843 the people of New South Wales voted for the first time, or at least its propertied men did. The poll ran from 15 June to 3 July and was Australia's first colonial election. Women, most working men, and former convicts without enough property were shut out. Still, it was a real shift. For the first time, colonists chose who would help govern them.

The election filled 24 of the 36 seats in a remade Legislative Council. The remaining 12 members were senior officials and men nominated by the Crown, so the elected majority was thin and watched closely. It was a long way from full self-government, which was still more than a decade off, but the principle had arrived.

The new Council first sat on 1 August 1843. It met in a chamber added to the northern end of the old 'Rum Hospital' on Macquarie Street, the surgeon's quarters that would grow into today's Parliament House. A colony begun as a place of punishment had taken its first real step towards democracy.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Parliament of NSW

properties.title: Latvian Strathfield
properties.summary: Strathfield became the heart of Latvian Sydney, where post-war exiles from a Soviet-annexed homeland built Latvian House and worked hard to keep their language and culture alive.
body: Among the post-war Baltic displaced persons who remade Sydney were thousands of Latvians, driven into exile when the Soviet Union annexed their homeland. By 1953 about five thousand of them had settled across the city, and they chose Strathfield as their meeting ground.

In November that year the Sydney Latvian Society bought two terrace houses at 31 Parnell Street. Over close to a decade the members reshaped them into Latvian House, known in their own language as Latviesu Nams. It became a small world of its own.

Inside there were Latvian primary and secondary schools, choirs, folk-dancing groups, scouts and guides, a theatre, a library and a bookshop. For families far from a country they could not return to, the house kept a whole culture warm and within reach.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Latvian Society

properties.title: The Coo-ee March, 1915
properties.summary: The Coo-ee March was the most famous of the First World War snowball recruiting marches, begun by a small band at Gilgandra and arriving in Sydney with more than 260 men.
body: In October 1915, with the casualty lists from Gallipoli growing longer by the week, about twenty-five men set out on foot from Gilgandra in the central west. Their destination was Sydney and the recruiting office, more than three hundred miles away.

As they passed through town after town they called out the old bush cry, 'Coo-ee, won't you come?', and young men stepped from the crowd to join them. By the time the marchers reached the city on 12 November the column had grown to more than 260.

Prime Minister Billy Hughes received them at Martin Place before a crowd said to number a hundred thousand, and they rested in the Domain. Their walk set the pattern for the snowball recruiting marches that crossed New South Wales through 1915 and 1916.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Coo-ee March recruits, 1915

properties.title: The Krait & Operation Jaywick
properties.summary: In 1943 the little fishing boat Krait carried Z Special Unit's commandos to limpet-mine Japanese shipping in Singapore Harbour, in the daring raid known as Operation Jaywick. She survives today at the Australian National Maritime Museum.
body: On the night of 26 September 1943 a battered fishing boat crept towards Japanese-held Singapore. She was the Krait, and her fourteen-man crew were the commandos and sailors of Z Special Unit, who had trained in secret for three months at remote Refuge Bay on the Hawkesbury River north of Sydney.

Paddling collapsible canoes up to the anchored enemy ships, they fixed magnetic limpet mines to the hulls. The next morning six explosions rolled across the harbour, and six Japanese vessels of about 26,000 tons were sunk or damaged. The raiders then slipped back undetected on the long voyage to Australia.

Operation Jaywick became one of the boldest commando actions of the Pacific war. The Krait herself outlived it all and is preserved today at the Australian National Maritime Museum in Darling Harbour.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; Australian National Maritime Museum
properties.image_caption: The MV Krait, raider of Operation Jaywick

properties.title: Carters' Barracks
properties.summary: Carters' Barracks was a convict barracks built under Governor Macquarie around 1820 at the edge of the town, home to carting and brickmaking gangs and a separate quarters for convict boys. It was cleared around 1901 to make way for Central station.
body: Where Central Station and Belmore Park stand today, Governor Macquarie had Carters' Barracks built around 1820, out at the Brickfields on the southern edge of the young town. It housed the convict gangs who carted goods about the colony and dug and shaped its bricks.

Behind a high dividing wall sat a separate barracks for convict boys, who were taught trades such as carpentry, blacksmithing and shoemaking and bound out as apprentices once they had served their time. The boys' establishment closed in the mid 1830s, and from about 1835 the site served as a debtors' prison and later for other official uses.

By around 1901 the whole site was swept away to clear ground for the grand new Central railway station. Nothing of it survives above the surface, a lost corner of convict Sydney lying beneath the city's busiest gateway.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: The Linnean Society of NSW
properties.summary: Founded in 1874 and largely funded by Sir William Macleay, the Linnean Society of New South Wales became the colony's chief forum for natural science, where Australia's plants, animals and fossils were described and debated.
body: The Linnean Society of New South Wales, founded in 1874 and incorporated a decade later, was for generations the leading forum for natural science in the colony. It was here that much of Australia's flora, fauna and fossil record was first described, named and argued over by working naturalists.

The society owed its existence to Sir William Macleay, a wealthy collector and its first president. He gave it accommodation and staff, built up a library, and in 1875 launched its scientific journal, the Proceedings, which still runs today. On his death he left a large bequest that funded research in bacteriology at the University of Sydney for decades after.

Its early home was Linnean Hall on Ithaca Road at Elizabeth Bay, close to Macleay's own grand estate. The society later moved through a string of city addresses, but the steady output of its meetings and journal did much to put the study of Australian nature on a serious scientific footing.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Linnean Society of NSW
properties.image_caption: Sir William Macleay, founder of the Linnean Society of NSW

properties.title: The Great White Fleet, 1908
properties.summary: In August 1908 sixteen white-hulled American battleships steamed into Sydney Harbour for a week of rapturous celebration. The visit sharpened the case for an Australian navy, founded just three years later.
body: In August 1908 the United States sent sixteen battleships, their hulls painted white as a sign of peace, on a voyage around the world. For one extraordinary week they filled Sydney Harbour. The 'Great White Fleet' steamed through the Heads on 20 August before crowds estimated at several hundred thousand, who packed every foreshore and headland to watch.

The welcome was overwhelming. A public holiday was declared, and an official reception was held at Farm Cove the next day, where the American admiral met the Prime Minister and other dignitaries. Fleet Week brought a review at Centennial Park, parades, balls, sporting matches and a harbour regatta, with buildings lit up at night and Pitt Street nicknamed 'America Avenue'.

Beneath the festivity ran a serious current. The visit dramatised how heavily Australia still leaned on others for its naval defence, and it strengthened the argument for a fleet of its own. The Royal Australian Navy was founded just three years later, in 1911.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian War Memorial
properties.image_caption: The Great White Fleet in Sydney Harbour, 1908

properties.title: Parramatta Girls Home
properties.summary: A girls' industrial school and reformatory at North Parramatta from 1887 to the mid 1970s, later recognised as a place of grave institutional abuse and, through the campaigning Parragirls, a place of memory and conscience.
body: On the Fleet Street precinct at North Parramatta, in buildings that had begun as the Roman Catholic Orphan School, the Parramatta Girls Industrial School opened in 1887. Girls from the earlier Biloela school were moved here, and over the decades that followed many thousands passed through its gates.

For close to ninety years the institution confined girls deemed orphaned, neglected or wayward, among them many Aboriginal girls taken from their families. Discipline was harsh, and later inquiries found grave abuse within its walls. It ran under changing names until it closed in the mid 1970s and the site reopened as the Kamballa centre.

The women who had lived through it, the Parragirls, campaigned for decades to have that history acknowledged. Their testimony reached the Royal Commission into Institutional Responses to Child Sexual Abuse, and in November 2017 the precinct was added to the National Heritage List as a place of memory and conscience. It sits beside the convict-era Female Factory, a second chapter in Parramatta's long history of confinement.
properties.source: Find & Connect; Parramatta Female Factory Precinct (National Heritage List)

properties.title: The Government Lumber Yard
properties.summary: The great convict workshop at the corner of George and Bridge Streets, where much of the early colony was actually made. Blacksmiths, carpenters and founders turned out the tools, ironwork and furniture of colonial Sydney.
body: Before Sydney had factories, it had the Government Lumber Yard. The workshop stood on the corner of George and Bridge Streets and was established about 1789, after an earlier yard on Bennelong Point burned down. It was the biggest worksite in town, where much of the early colony was actually made.

Dozens of trades were carried on within its walls. Blacksmiths, sawyers, carpenters, wheelwrights, coopers and brass and iron founders worked alongside tailors and shoemakers, turning out tools, furniture, wheels, nails, ironwork and even clothes and shoes for the settlement. Governor Macquarie upgraded the yard during his building boom in the early 1800s, and it became the colony's chief manufacturing centre.

When the yard closed in 1832 it was employing about 183 convicts, and the works moved to a smaller site next to the Hyde Park Barracks. The land was sold off in 1833, and within a couple of years terraces had been built over part of it. Nothing of the workshop survives above ground today.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW

properties.title: The Old Sydney Burial Ground
properties.summary: Sydney's first cemetery lay where the Town Hall now stands, in use from 1792 to 1820. More than two thousand of the colony's dead were buried here, convict and free, and many were never removed.
body: Beneath Sydney Town Hall lies the town's first proper cemetery. Governor Phillip and the Reverend Richard Johnson chose the spot in September 1792, on what was then the edge of settlement. For nearly thirty years it served as the main burial ground for Sydney, taking convicts and free settlers alike. No formal register or plan of the graves seems ever to have been kept, so we know more than two thousand names from parish records and other sources, but not who lies exactly where.

By 1820 the ground was full, and a new cemetery was opened at the Sandhills on Devonshire Street, near present-day Central station. The old burial ground was eventually cleared to make way for the Town Hall, which began rising in 1869.

The exhumation was never complete. Most of the remains found during construction could not be identified, and those uncovered in 1869 were reinterred at Rookwood, marked by a classical monument carved by the stonemason Francis Murphy. Bones, coffins and headstones have continued to surface over the years whenever the city digs around its first graveyard.
properties.source: City of Sydney; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.title: Australia's First Railway, 1855
properties.summary: On 26 September 1855 the first steam train in New South Wales left Sydney for Parramatta, running out of a humble tin-shed terminus in the Cleveland Fields, just south of where Central station now stands.
body: On 26 September 1855, before a crowd of dignitaries, the first steam train in New South Wales pulled out of Sydney bound for Parramatta. Melbourne had beaten the colony to it by a year, but this was still a turning point, the start of the railways that would remake New South Wales. Locomotive No. 3 hauled the official train, and on that first day the line carried more than three and a half thousand passengers.

The original Sydney terminus was not today's grand Central station but a humbler affair a little to the south, in the Cleveland Paddocks. It was a single thirty-metre wooden platform under a corrugated-iron shed, and locals took to calling it Redfern station because of where it sat. From this tin shed the line ran out through Newtown, Ashfield and Homebush to a junction at Granville.
Half a century later the little terminus was swept away when the present Central station rose one block to the north, built over the old Devonshire Street cemetery.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The first steam train in NSW, 26 September 1855

properties.title: The Anti-Transportation Protest, 1849
properties.summary: In June 1849 several thousand Sydneysiders crowded onto Circular Quay to protest against the return of convict transportation, a great rally sparked by the convict ship Hashemy that helped bring transportation to eastern Australia to an end.
body: On 11 June 1849, several thousand Sydneysiders gathered at Circular Quay in the rain. By some accounts the crowd ran to seven or eight thousand. They had come to protest against the return of convict transportation. The ship Hashemy had anchored in the harbour three days earlier with 212 convicts aboard, men who had already served part of their sentences in Britain and now arrived as 'exiles' on tickets of leave. A colony that believed it had left the convict era behind was outraged.

From the roof of a horse-drawn omnibus the barrister Robert Lowe told the crowd that the injustice done here was greater than the one that had cost Britain its American colonies. Henry Parkes was among the other speakers, an early outing for the man who would later be called the father of Federation.

The protest did not turn the Hashemy away. Most of its men were landed and hired out to landholders across New South Wales, and a smaller group was sent north to Moreton Bay and the Clarence River. The rally still mattered. It fed a growing movement against transportation, and within a few years the practice of sending convicts to eastern Australia had ended.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Morning Herald (1849)
properties.image_caption: Robert Lowe, who denounced transportation at the protest

properties.title: The Big Brother Movement
properties.summary: A youth-migration scheme founded in 1925 by Melbourne businessman Richard Linton, the Big Brother Movement brought thousands of young British men to Australia, each 'Little Brother' paired with an Australian 'Big Brother', mostly to work on the land.
body: The Big Brother Movement was founded in 1925 by Richard Linton, a Melbourne businessman, to bring young British men out to start new lives in Australia. The idea was a kind of sponsorship. Each 'Little Brother', usually a teenager of about sixteen, was notionally paired with an established Australian 'Big Brother', often a city professional, who would act as mentor and help him find his feet. Most were destined for work on the land, and almost two thousand arrived in the scheme's first few years.

In New South Wales the Movement ran reception and training places around Sydney. After the Second World War it bought Karmsley Hills, a six-hundred-acre farm at Bossley Park near Liverpool, as a memorial to the Little Brothers who had died in the war. Young migrants spent four to six weeks there learning to farm Australian-style. From 1951 a hostel at Homebush, Gunning House, took in those who wanted to enter trades or commerce.

Unlike the schemes that shipped out unaccompanied young children, later the subject of painful national reckonings, the Big Brother Movement dealt with working-age youths, and a Senate inquiry into child migration set it apart from those darker chapters. It still exists today as an education foundation.
properties.source: Senate Inquiry into Child Migration; Big Brother Movement histories

properties.title: The Roosevelt
properties.summary: The Roosevelt was the most glamorous and most notorious of the Kings Cross nightclubs, and it became Abe Saffron's first club, with a sly-grog trade later examined by a royal commission into the liquor laws.
body: Up on Orwell Street in Potts Point, the Roosevelt was the most glamorous of the Kings Cross nightclubs, and the most notorious. The restaurateur Sammy Lee opened it in 1939, and it boomed through the war as American servicemen and their dates crowded the floor, drinking on in defiance of Sydney's strict liquor laws.

In 1947 it passed to Abe Saffron. This was his first club and the foundation of the empire that earned him the nickname 'Mr Sin', the so-called King of the Cross. When Justice Maxwell's royal commission into the liquor laws sat in the early 1950s, witnesses described the Roosevelt's sly-grog takings and its dummy licences.

The club outlasted the inquiry, trading until six o'clock closing ended in 1955. After that it was leased to radio station 2KY and turned into studios. By then it had already set the template for the neon-lit, half-illicit nightlife that came to define the Cross.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Roosevelt nightclub, Kings Cross, 1945

properties.title: The Prince Edward Theatre
properties.summary: Known as 'the Theatre Beautiful', the Prince Edward was Sydney's first grand picture palace, opening on Castlereagh Street in 1924 and demolished in 1965.
body: On Castlereagh Street stood 'the Theatre Beautiful', the Prince Edward, Sydney's first great picture palace. It opened on 22 November 1924 with Cecil B. DeMille's silent epic 'The Ten Commandments', which ran for thirty-six weeks. Patrons climbed marble staircases past a fountain and gazed up at a crystal chandelier set in the dome.

A few months later, in February 1925, the mighty Wurlitzer organ was unveiled with Eddie Horton at the console, the first of its kind in the state. For four decades the Prince Edward was the grandest place in Sydney to see a film, though rivals like the Regent, Capitol and State soon matched its splendour.
The theatre closed on 4 December 1965 and was demolished soon after. Only the Wurlitzer survives. The Theatre Organ Society bought it, and it now sounds at Marrickville Town Hall.
properties.source: Wikipedia; National Film and Sound Archive
properties.image_caption: The Prince Edward Theatre, Sydney

properties.title: The Sydney Glaciarium
properties.summary: Opened at Railway Square in 1907, the Sydney Glaciarium was the first indoor ice rink in New South Wales and the home of the colony's first ice skating and ice hockey.
body: In July 1907 the Premier, Joseph Carruthers, opened the Sydney Glaciarium, a great refrigerated rink on George Street West by Railway Square. It was the first indoor ice rink in New South Wales, built on the site of a former cyclorama. Behind a narrow shopfront laneway, a plant pumped chilled brine through coiled pipes to freeze the surface, and crowds of around two thousand came to watch and to learn to skate.

The rink quickly drew the new winter sports north. Ice skating had begun in Adelaide in 1904 and ice hockey in Melbourne the year before the Sydney rink opened, but the Glaciarium gave both a home in Sydney. The first interstate hockey match, New South Wales against Victoria, was played here on 5 August 1907, only weeks after the doors opened.

When the American Great White Fleet steamed into Sydney in August 1908, the rink's players took on teams drawn from the visiting sailors, part of the carnival that greeted the warships. The Glaciarium kept its ice until 1955.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Ice hockey histories
properties.image_caption: Ice hockey at the Sydney Glaciarium, 1937

properties.title: Clifton Gardens
properties.summary: Clifton Gardens at Chowder Bay was a beloved harbourside pleasure ground, with a dance pavilion and a grand circular sea baths that drew Sunday ferry crowds for generations.
body: From the early 1880s, Clifton Gardens at Chowder Bay was one of Sydney's favourite harbourside pleasure grounds. The sea captain David Thompson took on the estate and built it up, running the Marine Hotel from 1885 and adding a wharf and a dance pavilion to land and entertain the Sunday excursion crowds on the picnic slopes.

In 1906 Sydney Ferries bought the estate and crowned it with an enormous circular sea baths. A netted swimming enclosure sat inside a two-storey pavilion holding two hundred dressing rooms, a grandstand for three thousand and a diving tower rising fifty feet above the water.

Fire destroyed the timber pavilion in 1956, just before it was due to be demolished, and the old hotel came down in 1967. A shark-proof swimming enclosure, completed in 1958, still stands in its place, and the beach and wharf survive as part of Sydney Harbour National Park.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Mosman Council history
properties.image_caption: Sydney Long, "Clifton Gardens", 1905

properties.title: The Criterion Theatre
properties.summary: The Criterion, fondly known as the Cri, was a thousand-seat playhouse on the corner of Pitt and Park Streets. It delighted Sydney with operetta and melodrama for nearly fifty years before the Depression and the cinemas closed it in 1935.
body: The Criterion stood on the south-east corner of Pitt and Park Streets, and almost everyone called it the Cri. It opened on 27 December 1886 to a design by the architect George R. Johnson, a neat little playhouse of around a thousand seats with a Neo-Renaissance front and a drop curtain that pictured Captain Cook landing at Botany Bay.

For close to half a century the Cri rang with operetta, melodrama and musical comedy. From 1915 the theatrical firm J.C. Williamson took on the lease and folded it into their wider network of stages. It was Sydney's favourite intimate theatre, the sort of room where the audience felt close enough to touch the players.

The Depression thinned the crowds and the picture palaces pulled the rest away. The Cri gave its last performance on 13 July 1935 and was pulled down soon after, partly to widen Park Street. Only the Criterion Hotel still clings to a sliver of the old corner, a small reminder of the theatre that once filled it.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: The Criterion Theatre, Sydney, c.1920

properties.title: Manly Fun Pier
properties.summary: The Manly Fun Pier was an amusement park built onto an old cargo wharf at Manly Cove, packed with dodgems, a ghost train and a shark aquarium behind a giant shark's-mouth entrance. It greeted ferry day-trippers from 1931 until the wharf was rebuilt at the end of the 1980s.
body: Where the ferries land at Manly Cove, the day-trippers used to spill off the boat and straight into the Manly Fun Pier. The site began as a cargo wharf in the 1850s, closed to freight in 1927, and reopened in 1931 as an amusement park under the cheerful slogan 'Built for fun in 31'.

Over the years it filled up with rides. There was a Ferris wheel and a merry-go-round, a ghost train and a mirror maze, dodgems, the Octopus, the Mexican Whip and a tumbling house. The favourite was the shark aquarium, whose entrance was a giant synthetic shark's mouth that visitors had to walk through. Inside swam a grey nurse shark, a wobbegong and a stingray.

For more than half a century the pier was the noisy, salt-air heart of a Manly day out. It closed and was cleared away in 1989, when the passenger wharf was extended and rebuilt, and the rides gave way to the wharf and shops that stand there now.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia

properties.title: Cremorne Gardens
properties.summary: Cremorne Gardens was a harbourside pleasure garden at the tip of the point now named after it, offering band music, dancing and fireworks reached by ferry from the Quay. It ran only from 1856 to 1862, yet its name stuck to the suburb.
body: For a few short years the peninsula now called Cremorne Point was a pleasure garden. The showman J.R. Clarke and his partner Charles Woolcott rented the ground from the landowner James Milson and opened the gardens with a display of fireworks on 24 March 1856. They borrowed the name from the famous Cremorne Gardens beside the Thames in London.

Two shillings at the gate bought a whole afternoon, with the ferry fare from Circular Quay thrown in. Visitors could take in band music and dancing, ride the merry-go-rounds, or try their hand at archery and quoits on the harbour's edge. The Cammeraygal people had camped, fished and gathered on this point for thousands of years before it became a colonial playground.

The gardens never quite paid their way. By the time they closed in 1862 the place had earned a shabby reputation and was falling into ruin. The name stuck fast, though, passing to Cremorne Point and then to the suburb of Cremorne that grew up around it.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; North Sydney Council heritage; Wikipedia

properties.title: Holtermann's Tower
properties.summary: From a tower on his Lavender Bay mansion, the gold-rich Bernhardt Holtermann had Charles Bayliss photograph Sydney Harbour in 1875 on giant glass plates. They were among the largest photographs in the world.
body: On the ridge above Lavender Bay, the German-born miner Bernhardt Otto Holtermann built an Italianate mansion crowned by a tower nearly 27 metres high. Holtermann had struck it rich at Hill End, and he raised the tower partly as a platform for a camera.

In 1875 the photographer Charles Bayliss climbed the tower and made the celebrated Holtermann panorama of Sydney and its harbour. He worked in wet collodion, coating each huge sheet of glass and exposing it while still wet, with a darkroom fitted out just below the camera. The largest negatives ran to about a metre and a half across, and the finished panorama was shown off at the 1876 Philadelphia Exhibition as among the largest photographs on Earth.

Bayliss had trained under Beaufoy Merlin in Holtermann's photographic company, but Merlin had died in 1873, so the great panorama was Bayliss's own work. After Holtermann died in 1885 the estate eventually passed to Shore school. The tens of thousands of glass plates, rediscovered in a shed in the 1950s, are now a treasure of the State Library of NSW.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Art Gallery of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Panorama of Sydney Harbour from Holtermann's tower, by Charles Bayliss, 1875

properties.title: Ryde Pumping Station
