properties.summary: The great steam pumping station at West Ryde lifted Sydney's water up to the growing North Shore. Its larger second station, commissioned in 1921, was for a time the biggest water pumping station in Australia.
body: As Sydney's northern suburbs spread, the colony needed a way to push water uphill to reach them. The first Ryde Pumping Station, a steam works beside the railway at West Ryde, was built by the Harbours and Rivers Department and handed over to the new Metropolitan Board of Water Supply and Sewerage in 1891. Water arrived by main from the Potts Hill reservoir, fed from the Upper Nepean scheme, and was pumped onward to reservoirs serving Ryde, Chatswood, Pymble, Wahroonga and the rest of the North Shore.

A far larger Station No. 2 was commissioned on 15 September 1921, and at its completion it was the largest domestic water pumping station in Australia. The site became a proving ground for new engineering. It pioneered turbine-driven pumps, reinforced-concrete chimneys, Australian structural steel and electrically welded steam piping.

The works were converted fully to electric power by 1982, after which the station could move around 700 megalitres of water a day. It was added to the State Heritage Register on 15 November 2002 and carries an Engineering Heritage National Marker.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register (1634); Engineers Australia; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Ryde Pumping Station, West Ryde

properties.title: Rose Cottage, Wilberforce
properties.summary: A vertical-slab timber cottage in the Hawkesbury, built sometime between about 1810 and 1820 and long regarded as the oldest timber house still standing on its original site in Australia, though the exact date is not recorded.
body: At Wilberforce, one of the five Hawkesbury towns laid out by Governor Macquarie, sits a small dwelling of vertical timber slabs that many regard as the oldest timber house still on its original site in Australia. It is tied to Thomas Rose, who came free to the colony aboard the Bellona in 1793, among the first free settlers to arrive. The cottage is usually dated to around 1811.

The precise year was never written down. Heritage records put the main structure somewhere between 1810 and 1820, and the surviving fabric supports that. The slab walls, the early hand-made gudgeon hinges and the window sashes are all consistent with a build before 1820. Because the date is uncertain, the claim to be the very oldest is genuinely contested. The timber slab stables at Windsor's Macquarie Arms are a rival, which is why Rose Cottage is best called the oldest timber house rather than the oldest timber building.

The Rose family stayed in the cottage until 1961, when John Henry Rose died and the property passed out of their hands. It is now kept as a museum by their descendants through the Thomas and Jane Rose Family Society. A permanent conservation order protected it in August 1985, and it was added to the State Heritage Register on 2 April 1999.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Hawkesbury heritage; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Rose Cottage, Wilberforce

properties.title: Boomerang
properties.summary: Boomerang is the lavish Spanish Mission mansion on Elizabeth Bay, built in the late 1920s for music publisher Frank Albert and named for the mouth-organ brand that made the family fortune. It has long been one of Sydney's most valuable houses.
body: On the harbour edge of Billyard Avenue stands Boomerang, a rambling Spanish Mission villa of cream stucco and terracotta tiles, with shaded loggias and barley-twist columns. It was built from 1926 to 1928 for the music publisher Frank Albert, designed by the architect Neville Hampson, with a garden laid out by Max Shelley. Many regard it as the finest and most intact inter-war Spanish Mission house in Australia.

The name was a nod to where the family fortune began. J. Albert & Son trademarked the word Boomerang and stamped it on German-made mouth organs, which sold at a runaway rate of about 800 a week by 1897. From that start the firm grew into a powerhouse of Australian popular music, later publishing and recording The Easybeats and AC/DC.

Frank Albert lived in the house until his death in 1962. It was added to the NSW State Heritage Register on 2 April 1999, and it has changed hands several times since as one of the most expensive homes in the country.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Boomerang, Elizabeth Bay

properties.title: The School Ship Sobraon
properties.summary: A former Aberdeen clipper moored off Cockatoo Island that became a floating school and reformatory for neglected and destitute boys from 1891. The navy later took her over and renamed her HMAS Tingira.
body: The Sobraon was a graceful composite clipper, built at Aberdeen in 1866, and for a quarter of a century she carried passengers and migrants out to Australia. In 1891 the New South Wales government bought her and moored her off Cockatoo Island as the Nautical School Ship Sobraon. She became a floating home and reformatory for boys who were neglected, destitute or sent to her by the courts.

Over some twenty years more than four thousand boys lived aboard. They drilled in seamanship and trades alongside ordinary schooling, in a regime that was meant to be as much rescue as discipline. Life on the ship was strict, but for many it was the steadiest home they had known.

In 1911 the Commonwealth bought the old ship for the new Royal Australian Navy. Refitted and commissioned in 1912 as HMAS Tingira, an Aboriginal word for open sea, she trained around three thousand boy sailors off Rose Bay until she paid off in 1927. She was broken up in 1941.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; RAN Sea Power Centre; Museums of History NSW
properties.image_caption: Boys manning the yards aboard the school ship Sobraon

properties.title: The Rum Hospital
properties.summary: Macquarie's grand convict hospital on Macquarie Street, built between 1811 and 1816, was paid for in rum. The contractors took a three-year monopoly on the colony's spirits trade in return.
body: The hospital was Governor Macquarie's first great public building, and he wanted it to show what the settlement could become. Rather than wait on funds from London, he struck a deal in 1810 with three businessmen, Garnham Blaxcell, Alexander Riley and D'Arcy Wentworth. They would build a new convict hospital, and in return they were granted a monopoly on importing spirits into the colony for three years.

That bargain gave the place its lasting nickname. The right to bring in tens of thousands of gallons of rum was worth a fortune in a colony where spirits did much of the work of money. The foundation stone was laid in October 1811, but shoddy work delayed the opening until 1816.

The hospital was built in three parts, and all three still stand on Macquarie Street. The southern wing is now The Mint, and the northern wing became the Parliament of New South Wales. The central block was pulled down and rebuilt late in the nineteenth century, and it survives today as Sydney Hospital, where the bronze boar Il Porcellino sits at the gate.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Living Museums
properties.image_caption: The Rum Hospital's three buildings, the central block flanked by the wings that survive as The Mint and Parliament House, in an early drawing (public domain)

properties.summary: In 1971 builders' labourers refused to demolish the historic Rocks. It was the green ban that saved Sydney's oldest quarter.
body: In 1971 the New South Wales government planned to clear the historic Rocks, the convict-era tangle of lanes and terraces where the town began, and replace it with high-rise office towers. The people who lived there fought back first. The Rocks Resident Action Group, led by fifth-generation local Nita McRae, petitioned for a year before turning to Jack Mundey and the Builders Labourers' Federation for help.

The union answered with a green ban. Its members simply refused to do the demolition work, and without labour the bulldozers could not move. The standoff turned ugly in early 1972, with confrontations and more than a hundred arrests, including Mundey himself. Still the ban held.

The Rocks survived with its nineteenth-century streetscape intact. The idea travelled too. The green ban, a union backing its labour to defend heritage and the environment, was copied around the world, and a quarter from Sydney's earliest days was kept standing because workers declined to knock it down.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Trust
properties.title: The Rocks Green Bans
properties.image_caption: Jack Mundey, who led the union green bans, 1977

properties.summary: Sydney once ran one of the largest tram networks on earth, second in the Commonwealth only to London. By 1961 the whole thing had been torn up.
body: At its peak in the 1930s Sydney's tram system was one of the biggest in the world and second in the British Commonwealth only to London. Around sixteen hundred cars were in service at any one time, shooting through the city and out to the beaches and suburbs in their hundreds. For most Sydneysiders the tram was simply how you got about.

After the Second World War, governments decided the trams were old-fashioned and a brake on motor traffic. From the early 1950s closure became policy, and the network was shut down route by route. On 25 February 1961 the very last tram made its run, returning from La Perouse to the Randwick workshops in the late afternoon, and the tracks were lifted across the city.
Half a century later light rail began the long road back, threading new lines through some of the same streets the old cars once owned.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Tramway Museum
properties.title: The Last Tram, 1961
properties.image_caption: A Sydney tram on George Street in the system's heyday (State Library of NSW)

properties.summary: One year after the Gallipoli landing, returned soldiers marched through Sydney and crowds packed the streets for the first Anzac Day, on 25 April 1916.
body: On 25 April 1916, the first anniversary of the Gallipoli landing, Sydney kept Anzac Day for the first time. That morning trains and trams were brought to a halt so passengers could give three cheers for the King and the Anzacs. Around four to five thousand returned soldiers then marched down Macquarie Street, with a long line of cars behind them carrying wounded men and the nurses who had tended them.

Government offices and many businesses shut their doors for a combined commemoration service in the Domain, and a minute's silence fell over the city at noon. Crowds lined the route in their thousands. For a country still in the thick of the war, with the worst losses on the Western Front yet to come, the day was both grief and pride held together.

The march through the heart of Sydney became one of the most enduring rituals in the national calendar. It began here long before the city had a fixed place to receive it. The Cenotaph in Martin Place was not unveiled until 1929, and the Anzac Memorial in Hyde Park not until 1934.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian War Memorial
properties.title: The First Anzac Day, 1916

properties.summary: On 5 March 1803 a convict printer ran off Australia's first newspaper, the Sydney Gazette, working under the governor's eye on a borrowed colonial press.
body: George Howe knew the printing trade before a shoplifting conviction sent him out to New South Wales in 1800. On 5 March 1803 he produced the first issue of the Sydney Gazette and New South Wales Advertiser, the first newspaper printed in Australia. It came off a small second-hand press that had travelled out with the First Fleet in 1788 and had already worn through years of government work.

The paper was Governor King's idea, and it served his purposes. It carried official orders, shipping movements and advertisements, and an officer checked the copy before anything was set in type. Printed on government paper with government ink inside Government House, it had no room for editorial independence. The first year reached about 300 subscribers at sixpence a copy.

For more than two decades the Gazette was the only paper in the colony, the single printed voice of a settlement at the edge of the known world. That monopoly held until 1824, the same year the government finally let go of the censor's pencil. Howe printed it until his death in 1821, after which his son Robert carried it on, and it ran until 1842.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.title: The Sydney Gazette, 1803
properties.image_caption: The first issue of the Sydney Gazette, 5 March 1803

properties.summary: Before a crowd of more than 112,000 at Homebush, Cathy Freeman won the Olympic 400 metres, and a nation held its breath.
body: On the night of Monday 25 September 2000, during the Sydney Olympic Games, Cathy Freeman lined up for the 400 metres final at Stadium Australia. Ten days earlier she had lit the Olympic cauldron at the opening ceremony, and now more than 112,000 people packed the stands at Homebush to watch her run.

In a green-and-gold bodysuit she entered the home straight in third place, then powered clear to win by about three metres. The roar that followed carried through the whole stadium and across the country.

Freeman took her victory lap draped in both the Australian and the Aboriginal flags. For a moment the country seemed to share one heartbeat, and the run endures as one of the most watched and most emotional moments in Australian sport.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.title: The Night Cathy Freeman Ran, 2000
properties.image_caption: Cathy Freeman

properties.summary: On 3 February 1954 Queen Elizabeth II came ashore at Farm Cove, the first reigning monarch to set foot in Australia, before crowds estimated at a million.
body: On 3 February 1954 the royal yacht Gothic carried Queen Elizabeth II into Sydney Harbour. A royal barge brought her and the Duke of Edinburgh in to Farm Cove, where she stepped ashore as the first reigning monarch ever to set foot on Australian soil. She was met by the Governor-General and a line of premiers and the Prime Minister, Robert Menzies.

An estimated million people packed the streets and foreshores to see her, in a city of fewer than two million. The landing fell at Farm Cove for good reason. This was where Arthur Phillip had raised the flag in 1788, and the choice tied the young Queen to the colony's founding moment.

Over the following weeks the tour ran the length of the country, and the crowds it drew were among the largest the nation had seen. Coming only months after her coronation, the visit caught Australia at the high water mark of its sense of itself as British, a feeling that would ebb in the decades that followed.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Archives of Australia
properties.title: The Queen's First Visit, 1954
properties.image_caption: Queen Elizabeth II during the 1954 royal tour

properties.summary: News of gold drained Sydney of its workers in 1851, then flooded the colony with people and wealth.
body: When Edward Hargraves found payable gold at Ophir near Bathurst early in 1851, Sydney emptied almost overnight. Labourers, sailors, clerks and police left their posts and headed for the diggings. Ships sat idle at anchor with no crews to sail them, and shopkeepers struggled to find anyone to mind the counter.

Within a few years the panic gave way to prosperity. Gold flowed back through the port, and people poured in from across the world to chase it. The colony's population climbed from roughly 200,000 in 1851 to about 357,000 ten years later.

All that wealth reshaped the city. Gold revenue helped pay for the Sydney Mint, opened in 1854 as the first branch of the Royal Mint outside England, and it bankrolled the banks, terraces and grand public buildings that gave Victorian Sydney its look.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.title: The Gold Rush Empties Sydney, 1851
properties.image_caption: Gold diggings, 1854, Edward Roper
